chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics, varieties, and testing methods of the indranīla (sapphire) gemstone. It compares indranīla to padmarāga (ruby) and discusses its qualities, value, and how to distinguish genuine stones from imitations.

story.title: Examination of the Indranīla Gem
story.arc: Descriptive exposition on gemology
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a standalone treatise.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical description without dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: The indranīla gemstone has similar qualities to padmarāga and can be tested by the same methods, but it can withstand greater heat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this may symbolize the inner strength and resilience developed through disciplined practice, which can endure greater trials than ordinary efforts.

teachings.teaching: One should not test gems by fire without proper knowledge, as it can destroy them and bring harm to the owner, tester, and instigator.
teachings.lens_note: This cautions against reckless experimentation in spiritual practice; without proper guidance, one may damage one's own progress and affect others negatively.

teachings.teaching: Genuine indranīla can be distinguished from imitations by its weight and hardness, which increase progressively in imitations like glass.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches discernment: true spiritual experiences have a distinct quality that cannot be replicated by superficial practices.

teachings.teaching: A sapphire with a rainbow-like luster inside is called indranīla and is rare and precious.
teachings.lens_note: This may represent the inner light of self-realization that shines through a purified mind, a rare attainment in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: A sapphire that can turn a large quantity of milk blue is called mahānīla (great sapphire).
teachings.lens_note: Symbolically, this could indicate the transformative power of true knowledge that permeates and changes everything it touches.

teachings.teaching: The value of a high-quality indranīla is equal to that of a padmarāga of the same weight, measured in māṣa units.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches that different paths or practices, when genuine, hold equal value in leading to the ultimate goal.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin, characteristics, varieties, and evaluation of the vaidūrya (cat's eye) gemstone. It explains how the gem was born from the roar of a demon, its colors, imitations, and methods for testing its quality and value.

story.title: The Examination of the Vaidūrya Gem
story.arc: Instruction on gemology
story.timeline_note: Mythical past; the gem originated at the time of a demon's roar.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical description of gem testing.

teachings.teaching: The vaidūrya gem is born from the roar of a demon and has many colors; its best color is like a peacock's neck or bamboo leaf.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the demon's roar symbolizes the primal sound (nāda) from which all material forms emerge. The gem's colors represent the diversity of gunas in creation.

teachings.teaching: Imitations of vaidūrya include girikāca, śiśupāla, kāca, and sphaṭika; they can be distinguished by weight, luster, and color.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches discernment (viveka) in distinguishing the real from the apparent, a key practice in Kriya-Yoga for separating the eternal Self from the transient.

teachings.teaching: The value of a vaidūrya gem equals that of an indranīla (sapphire) of the same weight; flawless gems can fetch up to six times the base value.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of proportionate reward for purity and perfection, analogous to the yogi's attainment of higher states through flawless practice.

teachings.teaching: The standard weight units: suvarṇa (16 māṣaka), śāṇa (4 māṣaka), māṣaka (5 kṛṣṇala), and dharaṇa (1/10 pala).
teachings.lens_note: Precision in measurement mirrors the precision required in yogic practices (kriyā) for achieving desired results.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin and characteristics of the gemstone known as puṣparāga (topaz), which is said to have arisen from the skin of a demon that fell on the Himālaya mountain. It details the various color varieties, their names (padmarāga, kaukaṇṭaka, kāṣāyaka, somālaka, indranīla), and the value and benefits of wearing the gem, particularly for women desiring sons.

story.title: Examination of the Topaz Gem
story.arc: Description of the origin, varieties, and properties of puṣparāga.
story.timeline_note: Mythical origin story; no specific historical timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a technical description of gemstones.

teachings.teaching: Wearing a puṣparāga gemstone grants women the boon of sons.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this can be interpreted as the gemstone's vibration aligning with the creative energy (kriya shakti) to enhance fertility and procreative vitality.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics, types, and auspiciousness of the gemstone called karketana (a type of ruby or garnet). It explains how the gem is formed, its colors, qualities, and the benefits of wearing it, as well as how to identify inferior stones.

story.title: Examination of Karketana
story.arc: Description of the gemstone karketana, its origin, qualities, and effects.
story.timeline_note: Mythological time, no specific historical context.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a descriptive and instructional chapter.

teachings.teaching: The gemstone karketana, when properly set in gold and heated in fire, removes diseases, destroys the effects of Kali Yuga, increases lifespan, and brings prosperity and happiness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching can be seen as a metaphor for inner purification: the gem represents the purified self, and the heating in fire symbolizes the transformative practice of kriya-yoga that burns away impurities, leading to health, longevity, and spiritual joy.

teachings.teaching: Those who wear such a gem are honored, wealthy, surrounded by relatives, and always joyful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle that inner purification leads to outer harmony and abundance; the gem is an external symbol of internal purity achieved through practice.

teachings.teaching: Inferior stones have dull color, impurities, and lack luster; they are to be avoided.
teachings.lens_note: This can be interpreted as a warning against superficial or impure practices; only genuine kriya-yoga (like the perfect gem) yields true results.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the properties, varieties, and benefits of the Bhishma gem (a type of cat's eye gemstone). It explains where it is found, its colors, how to wear it, and the protections it offers against various dangers. It also warns against defective stones.

story.title: Examination of the Bhishma Gem
story.arc: Descriptive exposition on the Bhishma gem's origin, qualities, and effects.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical description.

teachings.teaching: The Bhishma gem, when worn properly, brings prosperity and protects from various dangers such as wild animals, poison, fire, enemies, and thieves.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this can be seen as a metaphor for the protective power of spiritual practice (kriya) that shields the practitioner from internal and external disturbances.

teachings.teaching: Defective stones (pale, dull, discolored) should be avoided.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on purity and discernment in spiritual tools and practices.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics of auspicious and inauspicious moles (pulaka) on the body, their colors, shapes, and effects. It also mentions the price of a mole.

story.title: Pulaka Parikshana (Examination of Moles)
story.arc: Descriptive
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely descriptive and instructional.

teachings.teaching: Moles with colors like conch, lotus, bee, sun, etc., and with clear lines are auspicious and bring prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this can be seen as a metaphor for inner purity and clarity of thought leading to spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Moles resembling crows, donkeys, jackals, wolves, or vultures, with flesh and blood, are inauspicious and should be avoided.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as warning against negative mental impressions that lead to downfall.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the characteristics and testing of the rudhirākṣa (blood-eyed) gem, including its color, shape, and properties. It also mentions that such gems are found in certain regions like Kāverī, Vindhya, Yavana, Cīna, and Nepāla.

story.title: Rudhirākṣa Gem Examination
story.arc: Description of the rudhirākṣa gem and its origins.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical description of a gem.

teachings.teaching: The rudhirākṣa gem has a color like an indragopa insect, a curved parrot-like shape, and a size similar to a pīlu fruit. It is red-eyed and when tested, its color resembles the moon, indranīla, or suravajra.
Such gems are found in the regions of Kāverī, Vindhya, Yavana, Cīna, and Nepāla.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the origin and characteristics of crystal (sphaṭika), which is said to have arisen from the fat of the demon Bala, scattered by Lāṅgalī in various regions. The crystal is pure like the sky, white like lotus fiber or conch, with slight color variations, and is considered unequaled among gems and capable of destroying sins. The chapter also mentions that when properly crafted by a skilled artisan, it yields some value.

story.title: Origin and Properties of Crystal
story.arc: Description of crystal's origin from demon Bala's fat, its qualities, and value.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; mythological origin story.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily descriptive and instructional.

teachings.teaching: Crystal (sphaṭika) is unequaled among gems and destroys sins.
Properly crafted crystal by a skilled artisan yields value.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin and characteristics of coral (vidruma), stating that it was created from the entrails of the demon Bala thrown by Śeṣa. It details the best varieties of coral, their colors, and their auspicious properties, including bringing wealth, grains, and relief from poison and fear. The chapter also mentions the examination of other gems like ruby and crystal.

story.title: Examination of Coral and Other Gems
story.arc: Description of coral's origin, qualities, and benefits.
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; no specific timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily descriptive and instructional.

teachings.teaching: Coral (vidruma) brings wealth, grains, and removes poison and fear.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this can be seen as a metaphor for the purifying and protective effects of disciplined practice (kriya) that removes inner poisons (negative tendencies) and bestows spiritual wealth.

chapter_summary: Suta narrates the glories of various sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites), listing many geographical locations and rivers, and emphasizes that internal tirthas like mind-control and self-restraint are supreme. The chapter concludes with the declaration that Gayā is the best of all tirthas, granting liberation.

story.title: The Glories of Sacred Tirthas
story.arc: Suta narrates the list and merits of various pilgrimage sites, culminating in the supremacy of Gayā.
story.timeline_note: Narrative frame: Suta speaking to sages; within the Garuda Purana context.
story.comic_potential: A catalog of sacred places; could be visualized as a journey across India's holy sites.

teachings.teaching: The internal tirtha of mind-control (indriyanigraha) and self-restraint (dama) are supreme.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes inner purification through control of senses and mind as the true pilgrimage.

teachings.teaching: Bathing in the lake of knowledge with the water of meditation, free from attachment and aversion, leads to the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the practice of meditative absorption (dhyana) and discriminative wisdom (jnana) to cleanse the mind.

teachings.teaching: Those who see differences between tirthas are advised to perform external pilgrimages; but for one who knows all as Brahman, no tirtha is separate.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's non-dual Kriya-Yoga: the highest realization is unity; external rites are for those with dualistic vision.

chapter_summary: Brahma narrates the glory of Gaya, describing how the demon Gaya was slain by Vishnu, the establishment of the holy site, and the immense merit of performing shraddha and pinda-dana there, which grants liberation.

story.title: The Glory of Gaya
story.arc: Brahma narrates the origin and sanctity of Gaya, from the slaying of the demon Gaya to the establishment of the holy site and the benefits of performing rites there.
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; the story is told by Brahma to Vyasa.
story.comic_potential: High; includes a demon slain by Vishnu, divine interventions, curses, and boons.

teachings.teaching: Performing shraddha and pinda-dana in Gaya grants liberation and destroys sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that ritual action (kriya) performed with devotion at a sacred site purifies the mind and leads to moksha.

teachings.teaching: Four types of liberation: Brahmajnana, Gayashraddha, death in a cow-pen, and residence in Kurukshetra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a graded path: knowledge (jnana) is highest, but ritual (kriya) and place (kshetra) are aids for those not ready for pure knowledge.

teachings.teaching: All sacred rivers and water bodies come to Gaya for bathing.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes that all purifying practices converge in the act of devotion at Gaya; in Kriya-Yoga, this means the essence of all sadhanas is present in sincere ritual.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacred geography of Gayā, listing numerous tīrthas (holy sites) and the benefits of performing śrāddha, piṇḍadāna, and darśana there. It emphasizes that Gayā is the most sacred place for ancestor rituals, and that offerings made there become akṣaya (imperishable). The chapter also mentions specific deities and locations, such as Janārdana, Phalgutīrtha, and Akṣayavaṭa, and the liberation of ancestors from the cycle of rebirth.

story.title: The Sacred Geography of Gayā and the Benefits of Ancestral Rites
story.arc: Exposition of tīrthas and rituals for ancestor liberation
story.timeline_note: Timeless; describes eternal sacred geography
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a list of tīrthas and ritual instructions

teachings.teaching: Performing piṇḍadāna at Gayāśiraḥ grants eternal satisfaction to ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of place and ritual action (kriya) to purify and liberate, aligning with the principle that physical acts done with intention yield spiritual results.

teachings.teaching: Seeing Janārdana (Viṣṇu) at Gayā frees one from the three debts (ṛṇatraya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the transformative power of darśana (vision) of the divine, which in Kriya-Yoga corresponds to the inner vision attained through meditation, leading to liberation from karmic bonds.

teachings.teaching: Offering piṇḍa into the hands of Janārdana with a mantra ensures imperishable results in the afterlife.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on mantra and sankalpa (intention) as essential components of effective ritual, channeling cosmic energy through sound and thought.

teachings.teaching: Residing in Gayā during both fortnights purifies the family up to seven generations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the cumulative effect of sustained spiritual practice (abhyasa) in a sacred environment, purifying not only oneself but one's lineage through the power of place and time.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacredness of Gayā and various tīrthas (holy sites) for performing śrāddha (ancestral rites) and piṇḍadāna (offering of rice balls). It emphasizes that offerings made in Gayā, especially during eclipses or at specific locations like the Vaitaraṇī river, can liberate ancestors from hell and grant them heavenly realms. The chapter lists numerous tīrthas such as Rāmatīrtha, Mataṅgavāpī, and Somakuṇḍa, each with specific benefits. It also highlights the importance of feeding brāhmaṇas and releasing bulls (vṛṣotsarga) in Gayā.

story.title: Gayā Māhātmya
story.arc: Description of the sacredness of Gayā and various tīrthas for performing śrāddha and piṇḍadāna to liberate ancestors.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the chapter is a discourse by Brahmā.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a didactic text listing holy sites and their benefits.

teachings.teaching: Offering piṇḍa in Gayā during a solar or lunar eclipse is rare and highly meritorious.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of timing and sacred geography for spiritual practice, aligning with the principle of utilizing auspicious moments to amplify the efficacy of ritual actions.

teachings.teaching: Performing śrāddha at Gayā liberates ancestors from hell and grants them heavenly realms.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga teaching that actions performed with devotion and in sacred spaces can purify the lineage and accelerate spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: Bathing in various tīrthas like Rāmatīrtha, Mataṅgavāpī, etc., yields specific fruits such as the result of giving a hundred cows or performing a horse sacrifice.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets such statements as symbolic: the 'fruit' corresponds to the purification of specific karmic impressions through focused practice at these power spots.

teachings.teaching: Releasing a bull (vṛṣotsarga) in Gayā yields the merit of a hundred Agniṣṭoma sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this act symbolizes the release of latent energies (kundalini) and the offering of one's animal nature, leading to immense spiritual merit.

teachings.teaching: Piṇḍadāna should be performed with sesame seeds (tila) for oneself and others; without tila it is incomplete.
teachings.lens_note: Sesame seeds represent the subtle essence of offerings; in Kriya-Yoga, they symbolize the seed of intention (saṅkalpa) that must be present for the ritual to bear fruit.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the pilgrimage to Gaya and the rituals of shraddha (offerings to ancestors) performed there. It details various tirthas (sacred sites) within Gaya, the procedures for offering pindas (rice balls) to ancestors, and the benefits of these acts, including liberation of ancestors from hell and attainment of heaven. The chapter also includes a story of a merchant who performed shraddha for a pret (ghost) and thereby liberated his own ancestors.

story.title: The Glory of Gaya: Shraddha Rituals and Liberation of Ancestors
story.arc: Instructional narrative with embedded story of Viśāla
story.timeline_note: Mythological time; the story of Viśāla is set in a legendary past.
story.comic_potential: The vision of three colored figures (white, red, black) explaining their identities and the liberation of ancestors could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: Performing shraddha in Gaya with proper rituals, including offering pindas at various tirthas, liberates ancestors from hell and grants them heaven or moksha.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching emphasizes the power of ritual action (kriya) performed with devotion and correct procedure to transform the subtle bodies of ancestors, aligning with the principle that disciplined practice can purify and elevate consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Even a single son who goes to Gaya and performs shraddha can liberate a hundred generations of ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of lineage and the duty of descendants, which in Kriya-Yoga can be seen as the collective responsibility to maintain spiritual practices that benefit the entire family line.

teachings.teaching: The story of Viśāla demonstrates that performing shraddha in Gaya for a preta (ghost) can liberate not only that preta but also one's own ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of interconnectedness and the ripple effect of sincere ritual action, a key concept in Kriya-Yoga where individual practice contributes to collective upliftment.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of offering pindas (rice balls) to ancestors at Gayā, specifically at the Pretaśilā. It lists various categories of deceased beings—including those who died by suicide, accident, or in hells—and prescribes mantras and offerings for their liberation. The chapter emphasizes the importance of performing these rites to free oneself from the three debts (ṛṇatraya).

story.title: The Pinda Offering at Gayā for Ancestral Liberation
story.arc: Brahmā instructs on the ritual of offering pindas at Pretaśilā in Gayā, covering various categories of deceased, and concludes with a declaration of freedom from the three debts.
story.timeline_note: Mythological time; the ritual is prescribed for the present age.
story.comic_potential: A solemn ritual manual; low comic potential.

teachings.teaching: Offering pindas at Gayā with specific mantras liberates ancestors from various hells and rebirths.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this ritual act is a form of kriya (action) that purifies the mind and aligns one with dharma, leading to liberation from karmic debts.

teachings.teaching: Performing the Gayā ritual frees one from the three debts (ṛṇatraya): debts to gods, sages, and ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views debt repayment as essential for spiritual progress; this ritual is a concrete kriya to achieve that.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacred Pretashila (stone for the dead) in Gaya, its divine nature as a manifestation of all deities, and the benefits of performing rituals like shraddha and pindadana there. It lists various deities and their associated boons, emphasizing that worship of Adigadadhara (Vishnu) grants all desires and liberation. The chapter also recounts the story of Gayasura and the origin of the hill and stone.

story.title: The Glory of Pretashila in Gaya
story.arc: Description of the sacred stone and the benefits of worshiping Adigadadhara and other deities there.
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; the stone existed from the beginning of creation.
story.comic_potential: The stone as a divine manifestation and the list of boons from worshiping various deities could be depicted as a visual guide.

teachings.teaching: Performing shraddha and pindadana on Pretashila in Gaya leads to Brahmaloka for the performer and ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of ritual action (kriya) performed at a sacred site to purify and elevate the soul, aligning with the principle that disciplined practice yields spiritual results.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Adigadadhara (Vishnu) grants all desires: dharma, artha, kama, moksha, and even kingdom, peace, and progeny.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the culmination of kriya-yoga: by directing all actions towards the divine, one attains the four purusharthas and ultimately liberation.

teachings.teaching: Worshiping various deities (e.g., Ganesha, Kartikeya, Adityas, etc.) grants specific boons like freedom from obstacles, health, wealth, etc.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of 'yathā bhāva tathā siddhi' – as one's intention, so the result. In Kriya-Yoga, focused devotion (bhakti) combined with action (kriya) brings desired outcomes.

chapter_summary: This chapter enumerates the fourteen Manus, their sons, the seven sages, the groups of deities, and the Indras for each Manvantara, along with the avatars of Vishnu who slay the corresponding demons.

story.title: The Fourteen Manvantaras
story.arc: Enumerative cosmology
story.timeline_note: Covers all fourteen Manvantaras from past to future.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic montage of Manus, sages, and Vishnu's avatars slaying demons in each age.

teachings.teaching: The enumeration of Manvantaras illustrates the cyclical nature of time and the recurring role of divine intervention.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this cyclical pattern reflects the inner cycles of spiritual practice: each Manvantara corresponds to a stage of purification and realization, with Vishnu's avatars representing the inner Self that overcomes obstacles.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana lists the sons of various Manus, the sages, and the gods of each Manvantara, along with the avatars of Vishnu who slay the chief demons. It also enumerates the eighteen vidyas (branches of knowledge) established by Vyasa.

story.title: Enumeration of Manvantaras and Avatars
story.arc: Cosmic cycles and divine interventions
story.timeline_note: Covers multiple Manvantaras: Rauchya, Bhautya, etc.
story.comic_potential: Vishnu's avatars as animals (peacock, etc.) slaying demons in each Manvantara.

teachings.teaching: The one deity (Vishnu) manifests in four forms as Vyasa to establish the eighteen vidyas.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the unity of knowledge and the divine source of all disciplines, encouraging the practitioner to see all learning as a path to the same ultimate reality.

chapter_summary: The chapter narrates a dialogue between the sage Ruci and his ancestors (Pitrs). Ruci, a detached ascetic, is questioned by his Pitrs about his refusal to marry and perform householder duties. They argue that marriage and rituals are essential for spiritual progress and to repay debts to gods, ancestors, and beings. Ruci counters that attachment leads to suffering and bondage, advocating for renunciation and knowledge. The Pitrs explain that prescribed actions performed without desire purify the self and lead to liberation, while inaction leads to downfall. Ultimately, the Pitrs disappear, and the sage Markandeya recounts this story to Krauncuki.

story.title: The Dialogue Between Ruci and the Pitrs
story.arc: Ruci, a detached sage, is urged by his ancestors to marry and perform household duties. He argues for renunciation, but the Pitrs explain the necessity of action without attachment. The story illustrates the balance between renunciation and prescribed duties.
story.timeline_note: The story is narrated by Markandeya to Krauncuki, set in a timeless mythological context.
story.comic_potential: The debate between a stubborn ascetic and his ancestors could be portrayed humorously, with the Pitrs vanishing in frustration like blown-out lamps.

teachings.teaching: Householder life is essential for repaying debts to gods, ancestors, and beings; marriage and rituals are prescribed duties.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the necessity of disciplined action (kriya) as a foundation for spiritual purification, not as bondage but as a means to exhaust karma.

teachings.teaching: Actions performed without desire for fruits do not cause bondage; they purify the self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga involves action with detachment, burning past karmas without creating new ones, akin to the Pitrs' advice.

teachings.teaching: Renunciation without fulfilling prescribed duties leads to downfall; knowledge and action are complementary.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework rejects escapism; true liberation comes through engaged practice (kriya) combined with wisdom, not mere renunciation.

teachings.teaching: Even ignorance (avidya) can be beneficial if channeled through proper action, like poison used as medicine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the transformative power of disciplined practice: even flawed beginnings can lead to purification.

chapter_summary: The sage Ruchi, desiring a wife to fulfill his father's command, performs penance to Brahma. Brahma appears and tells him he will become a Prajapati and should propitiate the Pitrs (ancestors) through offerings. Ruchi then performs a tarpaṇa (offering) to the Pitrs on a riverbank and recites a hymn praising them, describing their various forms and the offerings they receive from different beings. The Pitrs appear as a mass of light, and Ruchi continues his hymn.

story.title: Ruchi's Penance and Hymn to the Pitrs
story.arc: Ruchi, urged by his father's command to marry, performs penance to Brahma, who instructs him to propitiate the Pitrs. Ruchi offers tarpaṇa and recites a lengthy hymn praising the Pitrs, who then appear as a mass of light.
story.timeline_note: The story is narrated by Mārkaṇḍeya to Krauñcika, as part of a larger discourse.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic appearance of the Pitrs as a mass of light could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: One should propitiate the Pitrs through offerings (śrāddha, tarpaṇa) to fulfill desires and attain spiritual goals.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of honoring one's ancestors as a form of karma-yoga, aligning with the principle of selfless action and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Pitrs are worshipped by all classes and beings, from gods to humans, and grant various boons including liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the universality of dharma and the interconnectedness of all beings, a key concept in Kriya-Yoga where action is performed for the welfare of all.

teachings.teaching: The Pitrs are protectors and grant blessings when propitiated with faith.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, faith (śraddhā) is essential for any practice; this teaching reinforces that offerings to Pitrs should be done with devotion.

chapter_summary: The sage Ruci praises the Pitrs (ancestors) with a hymn, seeking a wife and progeny. The Pitrs appear, grant his wish, and declare that reciting this hymn during Shraddha rituals ensures their eternal satisfaction, even if the ritual is flawed.

story.title: Ruci's Hymn to the Pitrs
story.arc: Ruci, desiring a wife and progeny, praises the Pitrs. They appear, grant his wish, and declare the power of the hymn in Shraddha rituals.
story.timeline_note: During a conversation between Markandeya and the sages.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic appearance of the Pitrs and the detailed seasonal variations in satisfaction duration.

teachings.teaching: Reciting this Pitr-stotra during Shraddha ensures eternal satisfaction of ancestors, even if the ritual is flawed.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that sincere devotion (bhakti) and correct mantra recitation can purify imperfect actions, aligning with the principle of inner intent over outer form.

teachings.teaching: The Pitrs are the support of the universe, manifest as Agni and Soma, and are to be worshipped with devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views the Pitrs as representations of the cosmic principles of fire (Agni) and moon (Soma), which correspond to the dual energies of purification and nourishment in yogic practice.

chapter_summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates how the apsaras Pramlocā emerged from a river and approached the sage Ruci, offering him a beautiful maiden named Māninī, born from Varuṇa's son Puṣkara. Ruci accepted her as his wife, and from their union a mighty son was born.

story.title: The Birth of Ruci's Son through Māninī
story.arc: A divine maiden is given to sage Ruci, leading to the birth of a powerful son.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during the narration of Mārkaṇḍeya to the sages.
story.comic_potential: The emergence of Pramlocā from the river and the offering of Māninī could be depicted with celestial beauty and divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: The union of a sage and a celestial maiden produces a son of great valor and splendor.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the integration of divine energy (apsaras) with disciplined practice (sage) to generate spiritual power.

chapter_summary: Suta narrates the meditation on Hari (Vishnu) as described by the sages. The meditation focuses on the formless, attributeless, supreme Brahman, beyond the five elements, senses, mind, intellect, and ego. It describes the supreme self as devoid of all dualities, the witness of the three states (waking, dream, deep sleep), and the fourth (turiya). The chapter concludes that one who meditates thus becomes Brahman.

story.title: Meditation on Hari
story.arc: Instruction on the nature of Brahman and meditation leading to liberation
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana discourse; Suta recounts to the sages.
story.comic_potential: A meditative sequence visualizing the formless supreme being, with cosmic imagery of the five elements dissolving.

teachings.teaching: The supreme reality is devoid of body, senses, mind, intellect, prana, ego, and the five elements. It is beyond the three gunas, without desire, free from birth and death, and is the witness of waking, dream, and deep sleep states. It is the fourth (turiya), the supreme Brahman, known through Vedanta.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this describes the state of pure consciousness (turiya) attained through disciplined meditation and pranayama, where the practitioner realizes 'Aham Brahmasmi' (I am Brahman).

teachings.teaching: One who meditates in this manner becomes Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that consistent practice of meditation on the formless leads to identity with the supreme self, not just intellectual understanding.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the meditation (dhyāna) on Lord Vishnu, detailing his divine form, attributes, and the benefits of such meditation. It emphasizes that meditating on Vishnu leads to liberation and cites the sage Yājñavalkya as an example.

story.title: Meditation on Vishnu
story.arc: Instruction on dhyāna for liberation
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a dialogue where Hari (Viṣṇu) instructs Śaṅkara (Rudra) on the meditation on Viṣṇu, citing the example of Yājñavalkya who attained liberation through this practice.
story.comic_potential: A visual depiction of Vishnu's divine form with all attributes, and the sage Yājñavalkya meditating and attaining liberation.

teachings.teaching: Meditation on Vishnu's form, both with attributes (mūrta) and without (amūrta), leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of dhyāna where the meditator first visualizes the divine form (saguna) and then transcends to the formless (nirguna) realization, culminating in the identity 'Vāsudevo 'ham' (I am Vāsudeva).

teachings.teaching: The meditator should contemplate 'I am Vāsudeva' (Vāsudevo 'ham asmīti).
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga: realizing the self as the universal consciousness, which is the goal of meditation.

teachings.teaching: By meditating on Vishnu as described, one attains the supreme goal (paramāṃ gatim).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga path where consistent practice leads to moksha.

chapter_summary: Yājñavalkya, having meditated on Viṣṇu, explains to the sages in Mithilā the essence of dharma, including the fourteen seats of knowledge, the dharmaśāstra authors, the definition of dharma as charity to a worthy recipient, the supreme dharma of self-realization through yoga, the four varṇas, and the saṃskāras from conception to cremation.

story.title: Yājñavalkya's Discourse on Varṇa Dharma
story.arc: Sages approach Yājñavalkya in Mithilā to learn about dharma; he teaches them the foundations of dharma, the authors of dharmaśāstras, the definition of dharma, the supreme dharma of self-realization, and the saṃskāras for the three twice-born varṇas.
story.timeline_note: The discourse occurs after Yājñavalkya has settled in Mithilā and is approached by sages.
story.comic_potential: A sage listing the great lawgivers and then detailing the rituals from conception to cremation could be depicted as a solemn teaching scene.

teachings.teaching: The fourteen seats of knowledge are the Purāṇas, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra, Aṅgas, and the four Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that true knowledge integrates multiple disciplines, not just scripture, to realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: Dharma is defined as giving to a worthy recipient with faith, at the right place and time, using appropriate means.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on right action (kriya) performed with devotion and discernment.

teachings.teaching: The supreme dharma is self-realization through yoga.
teachings.lens_note: This directly echoes Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, where the ultimate goal is ātmadarśana through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The four varṇas are Brāhmaṇa, Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, and Śūdra; the first three are twice-born and undergo saṃskāras from garbhādhāna to cremation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework would interpret these rites as purifying the body and mind for higher yoga, not mere social status.

teachings.teaching: For women, the saṃskāras are performed silently, and marriage is with mantras.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might note that Kriya-Yoga transcends gender distinctions, focusing on inner practice.

chapter_summary: Yājñavalkya describes the rules for the upanayana (sacred thread ceremony) and the duties of a brahmacārin (celibate student), including timing of initiation, daily rituals (sandhyā, agnikārya), begging for alms, study of Vedas, and the importance of the guru. The chapter emphasizes that the Veda is the supreme means to liberation for the twice-born.

relationships.rel: is supreme means to liberation for

story.title: Yājñavalkya on the Duties of the Twice-Born
story.arc: Instruction on upanayana and brahmacarya
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; prescriptive discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text.

teachings.teaching: The upanayana ceremony should be performed at the appropriate age for each varṇa: 8th year from conception for Brāhmaṇa, 11th for Kṣatriya, 12th for Vaiśya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of timely initiation into spiritual practice, aligning with the natural stages of life.

teachings.teaching: Daily rituals include sandhyā (twilight worship), agnikārya (fire offering), and recitation of Gāyatrī.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as foundational kriyās that purify the mind and align the practitioner with cosmic rhythms.

teachings.teaching: The brahmacārin should beg for alms from specified households and eat only after offering to the fire and with the guru's permission.
teachings.lens_note: This cultivates humility and detachment, key aspects of Kriya-Yoga's path of self-discipline.

teachings.teaching: The Veda is the supreme means to liberation for the twice-born; studying it daily brings the fruit of all sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret 'Veda' as the inner knowledge attained through kriya, not just scripture.

teachings.teaching: One who follows this discipline with controlled senses attains Brahmaloka and is not reborn.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of liberation through disciplined practice and inner purification.

chapter_summary: Yajnavalkya instructs sages on the duties of a householder, including marriage rules, types of marriages, treatment of wives, and conduct of women.

story.title: Householder Duties
story.arc: Instruction on dharma for householders
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger discourse on dharma.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text.

teachings.teaching: A man should marry a woman of good lineage, not a sapinda, and from a family of learned Brahmins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes purity of lineage and environment for spiritual practice; marriage is a support for dharma.

teachings.teaching: The eight types of marriages: Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, and Paishacha.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views marriage as a kriya-yoga practice; higher types align with sattvic action, lower with tamasic.

teachings.teaching: A woman should be protected by father, husband, and son; she should never be independent.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a framework for discipline in household life, essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: A wife who is devoted to her husband attains fame and goes to heaven.
teachings.lens_note: Devotion to husband is a form of bhakti yoga, channeling love towards the divine through service.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, narrated by Yajnavalkya, details the duties and rules for householders (grihastha), including mixed caste origins, daily rituals, hospitality, and prohibitions. It emphasizes dharma for all varnas and the importance of self-discipline.

story.title: Duties of Householders and Mixed Castes
story.arc: Instruction on dharma for grihastha ashrama
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana discourse
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with lists of rules.

teachings.teaching: Mixed castes arise from anuloma and pratiloma marriages; their status is determined by birth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that external caste is secondary to inner purification through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: A householder should perform daily rituals: sandhya, agnihotra, study of Vedas, and offerings to beings.
teachings.lens_note: These external rites are aids to internalize the presence of the divine; Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees them as preparatory for meditation.

teachings.teaching: Hospitality to guests (atithi) and feeding of dependents is essential; one should not cook only for oneself.
teachings.lens_note: Selfless service is a form of karma yoga; Sharma teaches that such acts dissolve ego and purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: Common dharma for all: non-violence, truth, non-stealing, purity, sense control, restraint, forgiveness, straightforwardness, and charity.
teachings.lens_note: These are the foundation of Kriya-Yoga practice; they prepare the practitioner for higher sadhana.

teachings.teaching: One should avoid improper occupations and accept only what is earned righteously.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that purity of means is crucial; the mind becomes tainted by dishonest livelihood.

teachings.teaching: Numerous prohibitions regarding bodily functions, behavior, and study times are given to maintain purity.
teachings.lens_note: These external rules train the mind in awareness and discipline, essential for internal yoga.

teachings.teaching: Times for Vedic study and suspension (anadhyaya) are specified, including during eclipses, natural phenomena, and after certain events.
teachings.lens_note: Observing these cycles aligns the practitioner with cosmic rhythms; Sharma notes that such discipline enhances receptivity to higher knowledge.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 96, part 2) provides detailed instructions on proper conduct (ācāra), including prohibitions against stepping on certain substances, rules for bathing, types of food to avoid, and expiations for eating forbidden items. It also lists classes of people from whom food should not be accepted and prescribes penances for violations.

story.title: Ācāra and Food Prohibitions
story.arc: Instructional discourse on dharma
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger dialogue between Yājñavalkya and sages.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily prescriptive rules.

teachings.teaching: One should not step on blood, feces, urine, sputum, or ointments; nor on the shadow of a king, a woman, or a teacher.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes purity of action and awareness of subtle energies in one's environment.

teachings.teaching: Brahmins, snakes, and Kshatriyas should never be disrespected.
teachings.lens_note: Respect for all beings aligns with the yogic principle of ahimsa and reverence for life.

teachings.teaching: One should follow the conduct prescribed by Śruti and Smṛti, avoid hurting others, and not strike a son or disciple.
teachings.lens_note: Non-violence and discipline are foundational to Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Always practice dharma; avoid what is opposed to it. Do not quarrel with those who are wealthy or with parents, teachers, and guests.
teachings.lens_note: Harmonious living supports inner stillness necessary for Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Do not bathe in others' water without first offering five balls (piṇḍa). Bathe in rivers, waterfalls, temples, tanks, and wells.
teachings.lens_note: Ritual purity prepares the body for subtle energy practices.

teachings.teaching: Avoid using others' beds, etc., and do not eat without need from the houses of misers, outcasts, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Guarding the mind from impure associations supports yogic discipline.

teachings.teaching: Do not eat food from certain persons: those who are cruel, fallen, etc. Avoid meat obtained without purpose, food with hair or insects, stale food, food touched by dogs or a menstruating woman, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Dietary purity is essential for sattvic mind and energy flow in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Food from certain low-caste persons is permissible if they offer themselves; stale food is acceptable if oiled and kept long; but avoid camel milk, one-hoofed animals, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Discernment in food choices supports the yogic path.

teachings.teaching: Avoid meat of carnivorous birds, certain birds like cranes, etc. Eating forbidden meat requires penance: three-day fast for ballūra, Cāndrāyaṇa for onions and garlic.
teachings.lens_note: Penance purifies karma and supports spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Eating meat at śrāddha after offering to gods and ancestors is not sinful; but one who kills animals improperly goes to hell.
teachings.lens_note: Intent and context matter; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes conscious action.

teachings.teaching: By renouncing meat and seeking Hari, one attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Abstinence from meat is a means to purify the mind for higher yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter discusses the purification of various materials and objects used in rituals, including metals, wood, cloth, and food, as well as rules for sipping water (ācamana) after certain actions. It also introduces the next chapter on the rules of giving gifts (dāna).

story.title: Purification of Materials and Ācamana Rules
story.arc: Instruction on ritual purity
story.timeline_note: Part of the Ācārakāṇḍa section of the Garuḍa Purāṇa
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily instructional text.

teachings.teaching: Gold, silver, and copper vessels are purified by water; wooden and horn vessels by scraping; woolen and silk cloth by hot water, cow urine, etc.
Food contaminated by hair, insects, etc., is purified by sprinkling ash; ground is purified by sweeping and sprinkling water.
Metals like tin, lead, and copper are purified with alkaline water or sour liquids; iron and bronze with ash and water; unknown contact is always pure.
Water from the ground is pure and satisfies the ancestors; meat fallen from a dog, caṇḍāla, or carnivore is impure.
Sun rays, fire, dust, shadow, cow, earth, wind, horse, goat, and drops from sipping are purifying.
After bathing, drinking, sneezing, sleeping, eating, or walking on a road, one should sip water again; after changing clothes, also sip.
In five cases (sneezing, spitting, sleeping, changing clothes, shedding tears), one should not sip but touch the right ear, where gods like Agni reside.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Yajnavalkya, details the rules and merits of charity (dana), especially the gift of a cow (godana), and the importance of giving to worthy recipients. It also discusses the sin of accepting gifts from unworthy persons and the merit of copying scriptures.

story.title: The Merits of Charity
story.arc: Instruction on dana (charity) and its rewards
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana, a post-Vedic text; no specific historical timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic content.

teachings.teaching: Charity should be given to a worthy recipient (brahmana with knowledge and austerity).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need for purity and discipline in the giver and receiver to ensure the act is spiritually effective.

teachings.teaching: One should not accept gifts from a person lacking knowledge and austerity, as it degrades both giver and receiver.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a warning against associating with those who are not spiritually advanced, as it can hinder one's own progress.

teachings.teaching: The gift of a cow with golden horns, silver hooves, and a calf brings immense merit, freeing seven generations.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the cow symbolizes the earth and nurturing energy; giving such a gift aligns one with dharma and purifies karma.

teachings.teaching: Copying scriptures (Vedas, Puranas) and giving them as gifts is equal to the gift of Brahman (brahmadana) and leads to Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight the importance of preserving and disseminating spiritual knowledge as a form of service (seva) that accelerates spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: One should not listen to atheistic or heretical teachings (lokayata, kutarka) as they lead to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on focusing on practices that purify the mind and avoid distractions that reinforce ego.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, attributed to Yajnavalkya, provides a detailed manual for performing Śrāddha rituals to honor ancestors. It specifies auspicious times, eligible and ineligible Brahmins, step-by-step procedures including offerings, mantras, and the handling of piṇḍas, along with the fruits of performing Śrāddha on various lunar days and asterisms.

story.title: Śrāddha Vidhi
story.arc: Ritual instruction
story.timeline_note: Timeless; attributed to Yajnavalkya's teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; instructional text with no narrative.

teachings.teaching: Śrāddha should be performed on specific times like amāvāsyā, aṣṭakā, etc., and with proper Brahmins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of precise timing and purity in ritual action to align with cosmic rhythms.

teachings.teaching: Eligible Brahmins for Śrāddha include those who are Veda-knowing, virtuous, and free from defects; ineligible ones include the diseased, deformed, and non-Vaiṣṇavas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a call to cultivate inner purity and devotion (bhakti) as prerequisites for effective ritual.

teachings.teaching: The procedure includes inviting Brahmins, seating them, offering water, invoking Viśvedevas and Pitṛs, making offerings with mantras, and feeding them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets each step as a symbolic act of offering one's actions and ego to the divine, transforming the ritual into a meditative practice.

teachings.teaching: Performing Śrāddha on specific lunar days and asterisms grants various fruits like wealth, progeny, heaven, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would view these fruits as natural consequences of aligning one's actions with dharma and cosmic order, not as transactional rewards.

chapter_summary: Yājñavalkya describes the symptoms of a person afflicted by Vināyaka (Gaṇapati) and prescribes a detailed ritual for propitiation, including bathing, offerings, and worship of Ambikā, to remove obstacles and bestow blessings.

story.title: Ritual for Propitiating Vināyaka
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of Garuda Purana teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; ritualistic and instructional.

teachings.teaching: Symptoms of Vināyaka affliction include dreaming of water and shaven heads, and being despondent with fruitless endeavors.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these symptoms indicate inner obstacles (kleshas) that must be purified through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The ritual includes bathing with cow urine, mustard paste, and ghee; anointing with herbs and fragrances; and offering various items at a crossroads.
teachings.lens_note: The external ritual symbolizes internal purification: the crossroads represents the choice between spiritual and material paths.

teachings.teaching: Worship Ambikā with offerings and prayers for beauty, fame, fortune, sons, and prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: Ambikā represents the divine mother energy (Shakti) that grants spiritual and material boons when approached with devotion.

teachings.teaching: After the ritual, feed Brahmins and worship the planets to obtain the fruits of good deeds.
teachings.lens_note: Feeding Brahmins symbolizes sharing merit; planetary worship aligns one's karma with cosmic order.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the procedure for performing a Graha Shanti (pacification of planets) ritual. It lists the nine planets (Surya, Chandra, Mangala, Budha, Brihaspati, Shukra, Shani, Rahu, Ketu), their associated colors, materials for making images, offerings, mantras, and prescribed donations. The ritual is intended to mitigate the malefic effects of planetary influences.

story.title: Graha Shanti Vidhi
story.arc: Ritual instruction
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; instructional content.

teachings.teaching: One should perform Graha Yajna (planetary sacrifice) to pacify the effects of planets when afflicted by their gaze or black magic.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of karma yoga—using ritual action to purify the mind and align with cosmic order, thereby reducing karmic afflictions.

teachings.teaching: The planets are to be worshipped with specific colors, materials, offerings, and mantras to obtain desired fruits like royalty.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of using external symbols and actions (kriya) to invoke internal states and divine grace, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Yājñavalkya describes the duties of the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) stage of life, including renunciation, ascetic practices, and meditation.

story.title: Duties of the Forest-Dweller
story.arc: Instruction on the vānaprastha āśrama
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's teachings on dharma
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily instructional.

teachings.teaching: A vānaprastha should entrust his wife to his sons or take her along, live as a celibate, maintain the sacred fire, practice forgiveness, and perform oblations to gods, ancestors, and guests using wild grains.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the gradual withdrawal from external duties and cultivation of inner discipline, aligning with the practice of kriya yoga to purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: He should be self-controlled, bathe thrice daily, refrain from accepting gifts, study scriptures, meditate, and be devoted to the welfare of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: These practices correspond to the yamas and niyamas of yoga, essential for stabilizing the mind in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: He may collect food once a day, once a month, or at intervals; use his teeth as a mortar; perform the cāndrāyaṇa vow; sleep on the ground; and engage in austerities like five fires in summer, lying on bare ground in rains, and wearing wet clothes in winter.
teachings.lens_note: These severe austerities are meant to transcend bodily identification, a key goal in Kriya-Yoga where the practitioner learns to remain equanimous in all conditions.

teachings.teaching: One who remains unperturbed whether pierced by thorns or anointed with sandalwood, and is ever content and free from anger, is considered equal to all.
teachings.lens_note: This state of equanimity is the fruit of Kriya-Yoga practice, where the yogi sees the same Self in all.

chapter_summary: Yajnavalkya describes the dharma of a sannyasi (renunciate), including the process of leaving home, taking up the staff and water pot, begging for alms, and the ultimate goal of liberation through yoga.

story.title: The Dharma of a Sannyasi
story.arc: Instruction on renunciation and liberation
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's teachings on dharma.
story.comic_potential: A sage calmly explaining the austere life of a renunciate, with visual contrast between worldly life and asceticism.

teachings.teaching: A sannyasi should be detached, carry a triple staff and water pot, and beg for alms without attachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes disciplined action (kriya) and detachment from results, aligning with the practice of karma yoga.

teachings.teaching: By practicing yoga and abandoning the body, one attains immortality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the culmination of kriya-yoga, where disciplined practice leads to liberation (moksha).

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the karmic consequences of sins, detailing the specific animal or degraded human births that result from various misdeeds such as murder, theft, and adultery. It also explains that after suffering in animal forms, souls may be reborn as virtuous humans in noble families.

story.title: Karmic Consequences and Rebirth
story.arc: Exposition of karmic law
story.timeline_note: Timeless teaching on karma and rebirth
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic enumeration of punishments

teachings.teaching: Sins lead to specific animal births: e.g., a brahmin-killer becomes a dog, camel, or donkey; a gold-thief becomes a worm or insect; a grain-thief becomes a mouse; a fruit-thief becomes a monkey; a meat-thief becomes a vulture; a cloth-thief becomes a leper; a salt-thief becomes a bird called cīrī.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that every action (kriya) has a corresponding reaction (karma-phala) that shapes the soul's journey. The detailed list serves as a warning to purify one's actions through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: After exhausting karmic consequences in animal forms, souls are reborn as virtuous humans in noble families, endowed with good qualities and wealth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the eventual purification of the soul through the cycle of karma, leading to a human birth conducive to spiritual practice (sadhana). The 'lakṣaṇopetā' (endowed with good marks) indicates readiness for yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana enumerates various sins (mahāpāpas and upapāpas) and prescribes corresponding prāyaścittas (atonements). It details the hells that sinners go to if they do not perform atonement, and provides specific penances for crimes such as brahmahatyā, surāpāna, steya, gurutalpaga, and others. The text emphasizes the importance of performing atonement for purification of the inner self and societal acceptance.

story.title: Prāyaścitta: Atonement for Sins
story.arc: Exposition of sins and their remedies
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of sins and penances.

teachings.teaching: Neglect of prescribed duties and indulgence in prohibited acts lead to downfall; atonement is necessary for purification.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of disciplined action (kriya) to avoid karmic bondage and the need for conscious effort to purify past actions.

teachings.teaching: Sinners who do not perform atonement go to various hells such as Mahāraurava, Raurava, Tāmisra, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these hells as symbolic states of consciousness resulting from unrepented actions, emphasizing the need for inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: Specific penances are prescribed for major sins: e.g., brahmahā must live as a beggar for twelve years, surāpa must drink boiling liquids, etc.
teachings.lens_note: These severe penances in Kriya-Yoga represent the intense inner discipline required to burn karmic seeds and realign with dharma.

teachings.teaching: For lesser sins (upapātakas), atonement includes Cāndrāyaṇa vrata, fasting, and donations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as gradual purification practices that align the practitioner with the rhythm of nature and cosmic law.

teachings.teaching: The severity of penance should be adjusted according to the sinner's caste, age, strength, and the nature of the sin.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of individualized practice (svadharma) for effective spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: For sins committed in secret, one should perform atonement secretly; for known sins, with the guru's approval.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the importance of humility and guidance in the purification process, avoiding hypocrisy.

teachings.teaching: Recitation of specific mantras (e.g., Aghamarṣaṇa, Rudra, Sahasraśīrṣā) and prāṇāyāma can purify even great sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, mantra and breath control are powerful tools to cleanse subtle impressions and elevate consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, attributed to Yājñavalkya, details various prāyaścitta (atonement) practices including kṛcchra, cāndrāyaṇa, and other penances, emphasizing purification from sins through rituals, fasting, and recitation.

story.title: Prāyaścitta Viveka (Discernment of Atonement)
story.arc: Yājñavalkya expounds various penances for purification, including kṛcchra, cāndrāyaṇa, and others, detailing their procedures and benefits.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: Chanting the Rudraikādaśa (eleven Rudra mantras) destroys sins for the twice-born.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of sound vibration (nāda) to purify subtle impurities, aligning with the practice of mantra japa as a kriya.

teachings.teaching: The ten yamas (restraints) include brahmacarya, dayā, kṣānti, dhyāna, satya, akalkatā, ahiṃsā, steyamādhurye, dama.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees yamas as foundational ethical disciplines that prepare the practitioner for higher kriya-yoga, purifying the mind and actions.

teachings.teaching: The ten niyamas (observances) include snāna, mauna, upavāsa, svādhyāya, upasthanigraha, tapas, akrodha, gurorbhakti, śauca.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, niyamas are daily practices that build inner discipline and devotion, essential for the kriya path.

teachings.teaching: Various kṛcchra penances (e.g., sāntapana, parṇa, taptakṛcchra, pādakṛcchra, prajāpatya, atikṛcchra, parāka, saumya, tulāpuruṣa) are prescribed for purification.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views these as physical kriyas that detoxify the body and mind, aligning with the yogic principle of tapas to burn karmic seeds.

teachings.teaching: The cāndrāyaṇa penance involves regulated eating of piṇḍas (morsels) according to lunar phases, with bathing and mantra recitation.
teachings.lens_note: This lunar-aligned practice in Kriya-Yoga symbolizes harmonizing with cosmic rhythms, a form of kriya aligning microcosm with macrocosm.

teachings.teaching: Performing cāndrāyaṇa with dharmārtha (righteous intent) leads to attaining the world of Candra (moon).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as achieving a state of lunar consciousness—calm, reflective, and blissful—through disciplined kriya practice.

teachings.teaching: One who performs kṛcchra penances with desire for dharma attains great prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this underscores that sincere practice of purificatory kriyas yields both spiritual and material benefits, as inner purity attracts grace.

chapter_summary: Yājñavalkya explains the rules of śauca (purification) for the deceased, including funeral rites, periods of impurity (āśauca) for various relations and circumstances, and the duties of a brāhmaṇa in times of distress, such as permissible occupations and items not to be sold.

story.title: Rules of Purification and Conduct in Distress
story.arc: Instruction on dharma for funeral rites and brāhmaṇa livelihood
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuḍa Purāṇa, likely post-Vedic period.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily prescriptive and legalistic.

teachings.teaching: For a child under two years, bury without water rites; for others, cremate with rituals.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of proper ritual action (kriya) for the deceased's journey.

teachings.teaching: Impurity periods vary: three days for sapinda relations, ten days for others, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees these as external disciplines that support inner purification through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: In emergencies (e.g., war, disaster), immediate purification is prescribed.
teachings.lens_note: Reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of adapting spiritual practice to circumstances while maintaining inner focus.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa in distress may adopt kṣatriya or vaiśya occupations, but must avoid selling certain items like salt, honey, etc.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on righteous livelihood (dharma) as a foundation for spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: Parashara explains dharma for different varnas and ashramas, including duties, purification rules after birth/death (sutaka), penances for sins, and funeral rites. The chapter emphasizes the importance of rituals, charity in Kali Yuga, and consequences of abandoning a virtuous wife.

story.title: Parashara's Discourse on Dharma
story.arc: Parashara instructs Vyasa on dharma, covering varna duties, purification, penances, and funeral rites.
story.timeline_note: Set in a dialogue between Parashara and Vyasa, likely during the Dvapara Yuga.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a didactic exposition of rules.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, charity (dana) is the primary dharma, and it may abandon the doer if done improperly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that in Kali Yuga, action (kriya) must be aligned with inner purity; charity without selflessness is ineffective.

teachings.teaching: A brahmana should perform six daily duties: sandhya, snana, japa, homa, and worship of deities and guests.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views these as foundational kriyas that purify the mind and body, preparing for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: A woman who abandons a virtuous husband will be reborn as a woman for seven births and suffer widowhood repeatedly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a karmic consequence of violating dharma in relationships, emphasizing fidelity as a kriya-yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Purification periods after birth or death (sutaka) vary by varna: 10 days for brahmana, 12 for kshatriya, 15 for vaishya, 1 month for shudra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees these as external observances that support internal purification, aligning with the kriya-yoga principle of discipline.

teachings.teaching: One who kills a brahmana must perform a severe penance (dvavimshatriṃsham aha ret).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such penances are not punitive but transformative, purifying the mind through sustained effort.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents the essence of statecraft (nītisāra) as taught by Bṛhaspati to Indra. It emphasizes the importance of associating with the wise, avoiding the wicked, and understanding the role of time (kāla) in all affairs. It also provides guidelines on choosing a good wife and the qualities of a virtuous spouse, contrasting them with harmful ones.

story.title: Nītisāra: The Essence of Statecraft
story.arc: Sūta narrates the teachings of Bṛhaspati to Indra on the principles of polity and righteous living.
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; Bṛhaspati's instruction to Indra.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily didactic and philosophical.

teachings.teaching: One should always associate with the wise and avoid the wicked; association with the good leads to success in this world and the next.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the importance of satsaṅga (good company) for purifying the mind and advancing in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Avoid disputes with friends, friendship with enemies, instructing foolish disciples, supporting wicked women, and associating with the depraved; even a wise man declines through such actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a warning against distractions and negative influences that disrupt inner discipline and yogic focus.

teachings.teaching: Time (kāla) ripens beings, destroys them, and is hard to overcome; it governs all actions and creation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, kāla represents the cosmic rhythm; aligning one's practices with the natural flow of time is essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should worship gods and brahmins, perform Aśvamedha to destroy great sins, and associate with the excellent, wise, and non-greedy.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the need for ritual and ethical conduct to purify the mind and create a foundation for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: Avoid slander, others' wealth, mockery, others' wives, and living in others' houses.
teachings.lens_note: These are practical ethical precepts (yama) that Kriya-Yoga considers essential for inner peace and spiritual advancement.

teachings.teaching: A relative who is beneficial is a true relative; a harmful relative is an enemy. A good wife is devoted, speaks kindly, and is chaste.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that relationships should support one's spiritual growth; a virtuous spouse is a partner in dharma.

teachings.teaching: A bad wife is like old age, a deceitful friend, a rebellious servant, and a snake in the house; all bring death.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against negative influences that drain energy and distract from spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Abandon the company of the wicked, associate with the good, perform meritorious deeds day and night, and always remember the impermanent nature of things.
teachings.lens_note: This is a core Kriya-Yoga teaching: constant awareness of impermanence (anityatā) fuels the urgency for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: A woman with a cruel face, harsh speech, fiery anger, and restless mind should not be served; attachment to such women arises only by fate and should be avoided.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a caution against karmic entanglements that hinder yoga.

teachings.teaching: Who can find peace when faced with a snake in the house, a disease that returns after treatment, and a body that ages from childhood?
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the urgency of spiritual practice in the face of inevitable suffering and death.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana offers practical wisdom on life, emphasizing the protection of wealth, family, and self. It discusses when to abandon people and places, how to deal with different types of individuals, the dangers of trusting certain entities, the futility of hoarding wealth, the nature of women, and the importance of learning and discernment.

story.title: Practical Wisdom for Life
story.arc: Didactic discourse on worldly and spiritual conduct
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger dialogue between Sūta and the sages at Naimiṣāraṇya.
story.comic_potential: Low; it's a series of aphorisms rather than a narrative.

teachings.teaching: Protect wealth for emergencies, protect family with wealth, and protect oneself with both.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the principle of self-preservation as the foundation for spiritual practice; one must secure basic needs to pursue higher goals.

teachings.teaching: Sacrifice one for the family, family for the village, village for the country, and the earth for the self.
teachings.lens_note: This hierarchy reflects the yogic principle of prioritizing the Self (Ātman) above all, aligning with the ultimate goal of self-realization.

teachings.teaching: Better to live in hell than in a house of bad conduct; sin diminishes in hell but not in a bad house.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as emphasizing the importance of inner purity over external circumstances; a disciplined mind is essential for yoga.

teachings.teaching: A wise person moves with one foot and stands with the other; do not leave a previous place without examining the next.
teachings.lens_note: This cautions against impulsive decisions, mirroring the yogic virtue of discernment (viveka) in all actions.

teachings.teaching: Abandon a country with bad conduct, a residence with troubles, a miserly king, and a deceitful friend.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this advises avoiding environments and associations that hinder spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: What use is wealth in a miser's hand, knowledge full of deceit, beauty without virtue, or a friend who turns away in adversity?
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on inner qualities over external possessions.

teachings.teaching: Many unseen friends appear when one is prosperous; when fallen, even relatives become enemies.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the transient nature of worldly relationships, encouraging detachment (vairāgya).

teachings.teaching: Know a friend in adversity, a hero in battle, a pure person in solitude, and a dear guest in famine.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches discernment in evaluating character, a key skill for a yogi navigating the world.

teachings.teaching: Birds leave a fruitless tree, cranes a dry lake, courtesans a poor man, ministers a fallen king, bees a withered flower, deer a burnt forest; all act according to need.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of expediency in worldly interactions, reminding the yogi not to take things personally.

teachings.teaching: Please a greedy person with gifts, a proud person with folded hands, a fool by following his whims, and a wise person with truth.
teachings.lens_note: This is a practical application of the yogic principle of adapting one's approach to different temperaments (adhiṣṭhāna).

teachings.teaching: Gods and good people are pleased by sincerity; others by food and drink; the wise by respect.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on inner sincerity (bhāva) as the highest offering.

teachings.teaching: Win over the noble by prostration, the wicked by division, the low by small gifts, and equals by equal prowess.
teachings.lens_note: This strategic advice reflects the yogi's need to skillfully navigate different personalities without compromising integrity.

teachings.teaching: A wise person, by understanding another's nature and speaking what is beneficial, quickly brings them under control.
teachings.lens_none: This echoes the yogic practice of empathy and right speech (satya) to influence others positively.

teachings.teaching: Never trust rivers, those with nails, those with horns, those with weapons, women, or royal families.
teachings.lens_note: This cautions against misplaced trust, a practical wisdom for maintaining safety and focus in spiritual life.

teachings.teaching: A wise person should not reveal loss of wealth, mental distress, bad conduct at home, deception, or dishonor.
teachings.lens_note: This advises discretion, which in Kriya-Yoga supports inner stability and avoids unnecessary distractions.

teachings.teaching: Association with low and wicked people, excessive separation from loved ones, living in another's house, and women destroy good character.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against influences that weaken one's moral and spiritual foundation.

teachings.teaching: Who has no fault in their family? Who is not afflicted by disease? Who has not faced calamity? Whose prosperity is constant?
teachings.lens_note: This universalizes suffering, encouraging equanimity (samatva) as taught in the Bhagavad Gita.

teachings.teaching: Who has not become proud after gaining wealth? Who has not faced adversity? Whose mind has not been broken by women? Who is dear to kings? Who has escaped time? What wealth has not lost its glory? Who has escaped the trap of the wicked?
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the impermanence of worldly achievements, a core teaching for developing detachment.

teachings.teaching: One who has no intelligence regarding friends, relatives, and self, and whose actions yield no result even when accomplished, but bring great suffering in failure—how can a wise person engage in such actions?
teachings.lens_note: This advises against futile endeavors, promoting wise action (kriyā) that leads to spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Abandon a place where there is no respect, no affection, no relatives, and no opportunity for learning.
teachings.lens_note: This encourages seeking environments conducive to spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Acquire wealth that is not feared from the king or thieves, and that does not leave you at death.
teachings.lens_note: This points to spiritual wealth (jñāna, bhakti) which is eternal and secure.

teachings.teaching: Wealth earned with great effort is divided among heirs after death; the sins committed for it become the dowry of the deceased.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against sinful accumulation, urging the yogi to earn righteously.

teachings.teaching: The hoarded wealth of a miser, like that of a rat, only causes suffering.
teachings.lens_note: This criticizes miserliness, advocating for generosity as a spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Naked, wretched, rough, with skulls in hand—such is the visible result of not giving.
teachings.lens_note: This warns of karmic consequences of stinginess.

teachings.teaching: Miserly people teach by begging, saying 'Give'—may you never be in such a state.
teachings.lens_note: This is a cautionary tale to cultivate charity.

teachings.teaching: Wealth hoarded by a miser is not used for sacrifices, not given to the virtuous, and ends up in the houses of thieves or kings.
