teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the futility of hoarding.

teachings.teaching: The wealth of a miser goes to gods, brahmins, relatives, or self? No—it goes to fire, thieves, or kings.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the need for proper utilization of wealth.

teachings.teaching: May one never acquire wealth through excessive toil, transgression of dharma, or servility to an enemy.
teachings.lens_note: This advises righteous earning.

teachings.teaching: Lack of practice destroys knowledge, bad clothes destroy women, digested food destroys diseases, and deceit destroys an enemy.
teachings.lens_note: This offers practical insights into cause and effect.

teachings.teaching: Punishment kills a thief, harsh words kill a bad friend, separate beds kill women, and lack of invitation kills a brahmin.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights subtle ways of destruction.

teachings.teaching: Wicked people, artisans, servants, bad drummers, and women become soft when beaten, but are not worthy of honor.
teachings.lens_note: This is a harsh observation on human nature.

teachings.teaching: Know servants by their work, relatives in times of distress, friends in adversity, and a wife when wealth is gone.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches discernment through testing.

teachings.teaching: Women have twice the appetite, four times the intelligence, six times the determination, and eight times the passion.
teachings.lens_note: This is a traditional view on feminine nature.

teachings.teaching: One cannot conquer sleep by sleeping, a woman by lust, fire by fuel, or thirst by wine.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against trying to satisfy desires through indulgence.

teachings.teaching: Passion in women is aroused by rich food, wine, perfumes, fine clothes, and garlands.
teachings.lens_note: This describes external triggers of desire.

teachings.teaching: Even in celibacy, when a desirable man is seen, a woman's yoni becomes moist.
teachings.lens_note: This acknowledges involuntary physical responses.

teachings.teaching: Seeing a well-dressed man, even a brother or son, a woman's yoni moistens—this is true, O Śaunaka.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the power of appearance.

teachings.teaching: Rivers and women have the same nature: independent movement, and they destroy banks and families respectively.
teachings.lens_note: This is a metaphorical warning.

teachings.teaching: A river destroys its bank, a woman destroys her family; both have a whimsical, graceful gait.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the previous metaphor.

teachings.teaching: Fire is not satisfied with wood, the ocean with rivers, death with all beings, nor men with beautiful women.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates insatiable desire.

teachings.teaching: There is no satisfaction for the learned in scriptures, for the beloved in sweet words, for pleasures, children, life, or boons.
teachings.lens_note: This points to the endless nature of worldly desires.

teachings.teaching: A king is not satisfied by amassing wealth, the ocean by water, a scholar by speaking, nor the eye by seeing kings.
teachings.lens_note: This continues the theme of insatiability.

teachings.teaching: For those who live by righteous work, delight in scriptures and wives, have controlled senses, and honor guests—even at home, liberation is possible.
teachings.lens_note: This affirms that liberation can be attained in household life through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Beautiful, adorned women with agreeable minds, living in palaces—that is heaven for the virtuous.
teachings.lens_note: This describes a conventional view of heavenly reward.

teachings.teaching: Not by charity, honor, honesty, friendship, weapons, or scriptures—women are always fickle.
teachings.lens_note: This expresses a traditional skepticism.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge, wealth, mountains, desire, and dharma—these five should be approached slowly.
teachings.lens_note: This advises patience in all endeavors.

teachings.teaching: Eternal are worship of gods, gifts to brahmins, knowledge with good qualities, and good friends.
teachings.lens_note: This identifies what is truly lasting.

teachings.teaching: Those who do not learn in childhood, and are poor and married in youth—they are pitiable, roaming as beasts in human form.
teachings.lens_note: This stresses the importance of education.

teachings.teaching: A student of scripture should not be distracted by eating; he should go far for knowledge with the speed of Garuda.
teachings.lens_note: This encourages dedication to learning.

teachings.teaching: Those who do not learn in childhood, lose wealth in youth due to lust, are humiliated in old age, burning like lotuses in winter.
teachings.lens_note: This warns of the consequences of neglecting education.

teachings.teaching: Logic is uncertain, scriptures are conflicting, and there is no sage whose opinion is not different. The truth of dharma is hidden in a cave; the path is that which the great have trodden.
teachings.lens_note: This advocates following the example of realized beings rather than relying solely on intellectual debate.

teachings.teaching: The inner mind is revealed by appearance, gestures, gait, actions, speech, and changes in eyes and face.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches observation as a means to understand others.

teachings.teaching: A wise person understands even what is unsaid; intelligence is fruitful in knowing others' intentions. Spoken words are understood even by animals; horses and elephants respond to signals.
teachings.lens_note: This extols the value of perceptiveness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the consequences of falling from various paths: if one falls from wealth, they should go on a pilgrimage; if from truth, they should go to Raurava hell; if from yoga, they go to Satyaghṛti; if from kingdom, they go to hunting. It concludes with the colophon marking the end of the chapter.

story.title: Consequences of Falling from Paths
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's teachings on dharma and consequences.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a didactic passage.

teachings.teaching: If one falls from wealth, they should go on a pilgrimage; if from truth, they go to Raurava hell; if from yoga, they go to Satyaghṛti; if from kingdom, they go to hunting.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, 'falling from yoga' refers to losing the continuity of yogic practice; the prescribed remedy (Satyaghṛti) may symbolize a place of truth or a practice to regain alignment.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a collection of moral and practical teachings on various topics such as the importance of steadfastness, the value of knowledge and virtue over birth, the proper treatment of servants and enemies, and the qualities of a wise person. It emphasizes the transient nature of wealth and the importance of associating with virtuous people.

story.title: Teachings on Dharma and Practical Wisdom
story.arc: Suta imparts wisdom to the sages, covering various aspects of righteous living.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Garuda Purana, a later Purana, and does not follow a specific narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a collection of aphorisms and teachings without a narrative plot.

teachings.teaching: One who abandons the permanent and pursues the impermanent loses both.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to focus on the eternal Self (Atman) rather than transient worldly objects.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge without proper expression is like a weapon in the hands of a coward; it brings no satisfaction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the importance of integrating knowledge with action (kriya) for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The fruits of the Vedas are the agnihotra; the fruit of good conduct is auspiciousness; the fruit of a wife is children and pleasure; the fruit of wealth is charity and enjoyment.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that actions (karma) yield specific results, and one should perform them with detachment.

teachings.teaching: A wise man should marry a girl of good family even if she is ugly, but never a beautiful girl of low family.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as emphasizing the importance of inner qualities (sattva) over external appearance.

teachings.teaching: Even from poison, one may take nectar; even from impure things, gold; even from a low person, supreme knowledge; and a gem of a woman even from a bad family.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches discrimination (viveka) and the ability to extract good from any source, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Do not befriend a king, a snake without venom, or a family where women are born; no family is pure where women are not respected.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would caution against attachments that lead to bondage, advocating for inner freedom.

teachings.teaching: Employ servants and ornaments in their proper places; a crest jewel does not shine on the foot.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of right action (kriya) in accordance with one's nature and role.

teachings.teaching: A gem set in tin does not shine; it becomes a reproach to the setter.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the need for proper alignment of one's actions with their inherent qualities.

teachings.teaching: Even when oppressed, a person of steady nature cannot be deprived of all virtues; fire, even when placed under a wick, does not send its flame downward.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the indomitable nature of the inner Self, which remains unaffected by external circumstances.

teachings.teaching: A good horse does not endure the whip; a lion does not endure the roar of an elephant; a hero does not endure the insult of another.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as a call to maintain one's dignity and not tolerate actions that degrade the Self.

teachings.teaching: Even if fallen from fortune, one should not seek the service of the wicked; a hungry lion eats grass? No, it drinks the hot blood of elephants.
teachings.lens_note: This encourages self-reliance and maintaining one's integrity, even in adversity.

teachings.teaching: One who seeks to reconcile with a once-betrayed friend embraces death, like a mule conceiving.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would advise against revisiting past attachments that cause suffering, focusing on the present.

teachings.teaching: The sweet-speaking children of an enemy should not be ignored; they become dangerous in time, like vessels of poison.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches vigilance and discernment in relationships, a practical aspect of dharma.

teachings.teaching: Remove an enemy who has been helped by you, as a thorn with a thorn.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as the need to use appropriate means to overcome obstacles on the spiritual path.

teachings.teaching: Do not worry about those always intent on harming you; they will fall like trees on a riverbank.
teachings.lens_note: This encourages trust in divine justice and detachment from outcomes.

teachings.teaching: Wealth appears as adversity and adversity as wealth; they lead to destruction when dependent on fate.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize the impermanence of material conditions and the need to rely on the eternal Self.

teachings.teaching: When fate is favorable, a man's mind becomes appropriate for the task and free from sin.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of aligning one's will with divine will.

teachings.teaching: In matters of money, learning, food, and business, one should be shameless.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as a practical advice to overcome hesitation in pursuing necessary actions.

teachings.teaching: Do not settle where there is no rich man, no Vedic scholar, no king, no river, and no physician.
teachings.lens_note: This is practical wisdom for worldly life, but Sharma might relate it to the need for a supportive environment for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Do not stay even for a day where there is no fear, no shame, no courtesy, no charity, and no good conduct.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the importance of moral values in a community.

teachings.teaching: Do not reside where there is no time, no Vedic scholar, no king, no river, and no saint.
teachings.lens_note: Similar to above, highlighting essential elements for a balanced life.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge does not reside in one place; no one knows everything; no one is omniscient.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would use this to encourage humility and continuous learning on the spiritual path.

teachings.teaching: No one is all-knowing, nor is anyone completely ignorant; one is considered wise by the knowledge they possess.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the relative nature of knowledge and the importance of practical wisdom.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides advice to a king (pārthiva) on righteous governance, emphasizing dharma, protection of subjects, restraint of senses, and the importance of wisdom, effort, and proper treatment of servants. It includes metaphors and warnings about the transient nature of life and wealth.

story.title: Advice to a King on Righteous Governance
story.arc: Didactic discourse on kingly duties and ethics.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; general advice.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily moral instruction.

teachings.teaching: A king should protect the earth righteously, conquer enemies, and rule with dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle of righteous action (dharma) as a foundation for spiritual practice, emphasizing that external governance mirrors internal self-discipline.

teachings.teaching: Collect taxes like a gardener picks flowers without cutting the root; do not destroy the source.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga teaching of moderation and non-exploitation, akin to the middle path in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: A king should not harm another kingdom but enjoy it like milk from a cow without cutting the udder.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the yogic principle of ahimsa (non-violence) and sustainable enjoyment.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and life are transient; one should focus on dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Echoes the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on detachment (vairagya) and the impermanence of material things.

teachings.teaching: Treat others' wives as mothers, others' wealth as clods, and all beings as oneself.
teachings.lens_note: This is a core ethical teaching in Kriya-Yoga, promoting universal compassion and equality.

teachings.teaching: A king without scripture is blind; he must rely on spies and wisdom.
teachings.lens_note: Highlights the need for knowledge (jnana) and vigilance in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve in adversity; remain equanimous like the moon entering Rahu's mouth.
teachings.lens_note: Cultivating equanimity (samatva) is a key Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Effort (udyoga) is crucial; even gods fear a person with six-fold vigor.
teachings.lens_note: Emphasizes purushartha (human effort) over fatalism, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's active spiritual discipline.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, attributed to Bṛhaspati, provides a detailed classification of servants (bhṛtya) based on their qualities and duties. It describes the characteristics of various officials such as the treasurer, gem expert, army commander, gatekeeper, scribe, messenger, judge, cook, physician, and royal priest. It also warns against employing wicked persons, comparing them to a snake adorned with a gem, and advises the king to appoint wise and virtuous servants for the welfare of cows and Brahmins. The chapter concludes with a note on the king's responsibility for the actions of his servants.

story.title: Classification of Servants
story.arc: Instruction on governance and servant management
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's Ācārakāṇḍa, attributed to Bṛhaspati's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Servants should be examined by their lineage, character, time, and work, just as gold is tested by rubbing, cutting, heating, and beating.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the need for inner purification and self-examination before engaging in action.

teachings.teaching: A wicked person, even if learned, should be avoided like a snake adorned with a gem.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the danger of associating with those who have knowledge but lack purity of heart.

teachings.teaching: The king is responsible for the actions of his servants; therefore, he should appoint wise and virtuous men for the welfare of cows and Brahmins.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on righteous action (dharma) and the leader's duty to ensure the moral integrity of their subordinates.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the importance of associating with virtuous people, the inevitability of karma, and the futility of lamenting over fate. It emphasizes that one's actions determine their destiny, and that true purity lies in truth, self-control, and compassion.

story.title: Discourse on Karma and Virtue
story.arc: Didactic
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; general teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily philosophical.

teachings.teaching: Associate with the virtuous and avoid the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this means cultivating sattvic company to stabilize the mind for inner practice.

teachings.teaching: Perform actions with foresight, leaving some reserve.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes moderation and conservation of energy for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Collect wealth gradually like a bee collecting honey without harming the flower.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the yogic principle of non-violence (ahimsa) and sustainable effort.

teachings.teaching: One's own actions determine happiness and suffering; karma cannot be escaped.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that past karmas can be purified through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Even great beings like Ravana are destroyed by time; fate is powerful.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the need for spiritual effort beyond mere intellect or power.

teachings.teaching: True purity is truth, mental purity, sense control, and compassion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these are prerequisites for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: A wise person remains equanimous in honor and dishonor.
teachings.lens_note: This is a key attitude for a yogi, as taught in the Bhagavad Gita.

teachings.teaching: Nothing can be obtained without destiny; lamentation is futile.
teachings.lens_note: Acceptance of divine will is a step toward inner peace in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: One does not die before the appointed time, nor live after.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the importance of using time wisely for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Past karma bears fruit in due time, like trees.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga aims to accelerate spiritual evolution by burning karmic seeds.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the nature of karma, the relationship between action and its results, the futility of attachment, and the cyclical nature of happiness and suffering. It emphasizes that past actions follow the doer like a calf finds its mother among cows, and that one should not grieve over what is done. It also highlights that lowly people see others' faults while ignoring their own, and that attachment leads to fear and suffering, while detachment brings great happiness. The body is the seat of both pleasure and pain, and everything under another's control is suffering, while self-control is happiness. Happiness and suffering alternate like a wheel.

story.title: Discourse on Karma and Detachment
story.arc: Philosophical teaching
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana, likely narrated by Sūta to the sages.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a didactic philosophical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Past actions follow the doer just as a calf finds its mother among thousands of cows.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that every action (kriya) creates a samskara that will manifest in due time, reinforcing the need for mindful action.

teachings.teaching: One should enjoy the fruits of one's own actions and not grieve.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as accepting the results of past karmas as a means to purify the mind and reduce ego.

teachings.teaching: Past good or bad actions determine one's next birth.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the law of karma; in Kriya-Yoga, conscious action can break the cycle.

teachings.teaching: Lowly people see others' faults as big as mustard seeds but ignore their own faults as big as a bilva fruit.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would stress self-awareness and introspection as essential for spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Those afflicted with attachment and aversion find no happiness anywhere; true happiness is in inner peace (nirvṛti).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the state of inner stillness achieved through meditation.

teachings.teaching: Where there is affection, there is fear; affection is the source of suffering; renouncing it leads to great happiness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a call to cultivate detachment (vairāgya) through Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: The body is the abode of both pleasure and pain; life and body are born together.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the embodied nature of experience; Kriya-Yoga works with the body to transcend it.

teachings.teaching: Everything under another's control is suffering; everything under self-control is happiness. This is the mark of happiness and suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize self-mastery through Kriya-Yoga as the path to true happiness.

teachings.teaching: Happiness is followed by suffering, and suffering by happiness; they revolve like a wheel for humans.
teachings.lens_note: This cyclical nature is transcended through the steady practice of Kriya-Yoga, leading to equanimity.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve over what is past or distant; living in the present, one is not afflicted by sorrow.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would advocate for mindfulness and living in the present moment, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the nature of friendship, enmity, and trust, emphasizing that relationships are based on circumstances. It warns against excessive trust, especially in women, and provides advice on health, conduct, and the avoidance of certain behaviors that lead to ruin. The text also lists actions that bring prosperity or destroy it, and highlights the importance of discretion and self-control.

story.title: On Friendship, Trust, and Right Conduct
story.arc: Didactic discourse on worldly wisdom and spiritual principles
story.timeline_note: Not specified; likely a timeless teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a series of proverbs and moral instructions without narrative action.

teachings.teaching: No one is inherently a friend or enemy; relationships arise from circumstances.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of detachment (vairagya) and understanding that all relationships are karmic interactions.

teachings.teaching: Uttering 'Hari' (the name of Vishnu) even once binds one to the path of liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on nama-japa (chanting divine names) as a powerful tool for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should avoid excessive trust in women, kings, snakes, and fire, as they can be dangerous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary might interpret this as a warning against attachment to sense objects and worldly powers, which distract from inner yoga.

teachings.teaching: A wise person conceals their own faults like a tortoise hides its limbs, and observes others' faults.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the yogic practice of self-restraint (yama) and introspection (svadhyaya).

teachings.teaching: Six things destroy life: dry meat, old women, young curd, morning sun, early morning sex, and sleep.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these are seen as imbalances that disturb the pranic flow; moderation is key.

teachings.teaching: Six things bring back lost prosperity: clean head, clean feet, service to a virtuous woman, moderate eating, sleeping not naked, and avoiding sex on forbidden days.
teachings.lens_note: These practices promote sattvic living and energy conservation, central to Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: One should not be too straight (like a tree) or too crooked; the straight get cut, the crooked survive.
teachings.lens_note: This advises pragmatic wisdom in worldly dealings, balancing truthfulness with discretion.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a series of moral and practical teachings on family, ethics, and self-discipline. It discusses the qualities of a good son, the importance of forgiveness, the dangers of ingratitude, and the need to guard one's mind. The text also warns against associating with evil persons and emphasizes the value of unity among the weak.

story.title: Teachings on Dharma and Family
story.arc: Didactic discourse on ethical conduct
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a larger narrative in the Garuda Purana.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily moral instruction with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: A single virtuous son illuminates the family like the moon illuminates the sky.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformative power of one disciplined individual whose inner purity radiates outward, uplifting the entire lineage.

teachings.teaching: One should treat a son as a friend after the age of sixteen.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of gradual spiritual maturation: as the child develops discernment, the relationship shifts from discipline to mutual respect, mirroring the guru-disciple bond.

teachings.teaching: Forgiveness is a virtue, but excessive forgiveness may be mistaken for weakness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework teaches that forgiveness must be balanced with wisdom (viveka); passive tolerance of evil enables adharma, so one must act firmly when necessary.

teachings.teaching: The mind is the master of the body; if the mind is destroyed, the senses perish.
teachings.lens_note: This is a core Kriya-Yoga teaching: control of the mind through meditation (kriya) is essential for health and spiritual progress. A stable mind ensures the senses function harmoniously.

teachings.teaching: One should not underestimate a small enemy, just as a small fire can burn the world.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this warns against neglecting subtle negative tendencies (vasanas) which, if unchecked, can overwhelm the mind. Vigilance in practice is necessary.

teachings.teaching: There is no atonement for an ungrateful person.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that gratitude (kritajnata) is foundational for spiritual growth; ingratitude blocks the flow of grace and cannot be remedied by external rituals alone.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 115) is a collection of moral and practical teachings, emphasizing the importance of avoiding bad company, the transient nature of life, the value of honor and self-reliance, and the need for righteous conduct. It contrasts the fleeting nature of wealth and youth with the permanence of dharma and fame, and warns against vices like greed, deceit, and dependency.

story.title: Moral Teachings on Life and Conduct
story.arc: Didactic
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a collection of aphorisms.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a series of moral maxims without a plot.

teachings.teaching: Avoid bad company, bad king, bad son, bad daughter, bad country, and bad friend from a distance.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to purify one's environment and associations to support inner discipline and spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, dharma has fled, truth is distant, earth is barren, people are deceitful, and life is miserable; blessed are those who die.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a call to intensify one's spiritual practice (kriya-yoga) to transcend the degeneracy of the age.

teachings.teaching: There is no happiness with a bad son, no pleasure with a bad wife, no trust in a bad friend, no life in a bad kingdom.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of cultivating virtuous relationships as a foundation for a stable spiritual life.

teachings.teaching: Dependence on others' food, wealth, bed, women, and residence can destroy even Indra's prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would stress self-reliance and detachment from material dependencies to maintain inner freedom.

teachings.teaching: Sin transfers through contact, conversation, eating together, sitting, and lying together.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the subtle influence of association; in Kriya-Yoga, one must guard the senses and choose companions wisely.

teachings.teaching: Women are ruined by beauty, ascetics by anger, cows by grazing in bad places, and brahmins by food from shudras.
teachings.lens_note: Each has a specific vulnerability; spiritual aspirants must identify and guard their own weak points.

teachings.teaching: Sin spreads like water from pot to pot through sitting, sleeping, eating, and mixing in a row.
teachings.lens_note: Reinforces the need for purity in social interactions and rituals.

teachings.teaching: There are many faults in pampering, many virtues in discipline; therefore, a student or son should be disciplined, not pampered.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as aligning with the disciplined practice of kriya-yoga, where strict adherence is necessary.

teachings.teaching: Old age comes to bodies from travel, to mountains from water, to women from lack of enjoyment, to clothes from sun.
teachings.lens_note: Impermanence is a key theme; one should not be attached to transient things.

teachings.teaching: Low people desire conflict, mediocre desire peace, and the best desire honor; honor is the wealth of the great.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that true honor comes from inner integrity and spiritual attainment, not external recognition.

teachings.teaching: Lions in the forest do not bow even when hungry; those born in good families do not engage in low deeds even when poor.
teachings.lens_note: Innate nobility and self-respect are crucial; one should maintain dignity regardless of circumstances.

teachings.teaching: A lion's kingship is natural, not conferred by ritual; similarly, true greatness is inherent.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would relate this to the innate divinity within, which kriya-yoga helps realize.

teachings.teaching: A negligent merchant, a greedy servant, a proud beggar, a lustful poor man, and a harsh-speaking woman do not succeed.
teachings.lens_note: These are obstacles to spiritual and worldly progress; one must cultivate opposite virtues.

teachings.teaching: Five evils: a generous poor man, a miser with wealth, a disobedient son, service to a wicked person, and death while doing good deeds.
teachings.lens_note: These paradoxes highlight the complexities of life; one should strive for balance and wisdom.

teachings.teaching: Five things burn without fire: separation from beloved, insult from relatives, remaining debt, service to wicked, and friends turning away due to poverty.
teachings.lens_note: These are sources of intense suffering; spiritual practice can provide equanimity.

teachings.teaching: Four worries are like sword's edge: insult from a low person, a hungry wife, a disaffected wife, and obstruction from a relative.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would advise using kriya-yoga to develop inner strength to bear these.

teachings.teaching: Five things uproot suffering: a submissive son, learning, health, association with good people, and a devoted wife.
teachings.lens_note: These are supports for a peaceful life conducive to spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Five beings (deer, elephant, moth, fish, bee) are destroyed by five senses; how can one who indulges in all five not be destroyed?
teachings.lens_note: A classic warning against sense indulgence; kriya-yoga teaches control of the senses.

teachings.teaching: Five types of brahmins are not to be honored even if equal to Brihaspati: impatient, harsh, proud, poorly dressed, and self-invited.
teachings.lens_note: External qualifications are not enough; character matters.

teachings.teaching: Five things are determined at birth: lifespan, karma, wealth, learning, and death.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the concept of destiny; but effort (purushartha) can modify it.

teachings.teaching: Five virtues are praised: climbing mountains, being in water, staying in cowherd, restraining wicked, and lifting the fallen.
teachings.lens_note: These are acts of courage and compassion.

teachings.teaching: Five things are unstable: cloud's shadow, friendship with a rogue, association with another's wife, youth, and wealth.
teachings.lens_note: Impermanence reminder; focus on the eternal.

teachings.teaching: Life, wealth, youth, sons, wives are unstable; dharma, fame, and glory are stable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that only spiritual achievements endure.

teachings.teaching: A hundred years is short; night takes half; disease, sorrow, old age, and exertion make half of that useless.
teachings.lens_note: Urgency to practice dharma and yoga.

teachings.teaching: Human life is a hundred years; half is night; half of that is childhood; the rest is consumed by sorrow, service, etc.; what pride is there?
teachings.lens_note: Vanity of worldly life; turn to spiritual pursuits.

teachings.teaching: Day and night move like old age; death devours beings like a snake devours the wind.
teachings.lens_note: Inevitable death; prepare through spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Whether moving or still, awake or asleep, all beings' actions are for the benefit of others, like an animal's.
teachings.lens_note: Selflessness is the natural order; one should act for the good of all.

teachings.teaching: What difference between a human animal and an animal if one's intellect is devoid of discrimination and only concerned with filling the belly?
teachings.lens_note: Human birth is for higher purpose; kriya-yoga awakens discrimination.

teachings.teaching: One who has no fame in valor, austerity, charity, learning, or wealth is like mother's excrement.
teachings.lens_note: Harsh but emphasizes the need to achieve something worthwhile.

teachings.teaching: Even a moment of life lived with fame, knowledge, valor, and unbroken honor is true life; a crow lives long eating offerings.
teachings.lens_note: Quality over quantity; live meaningfully.

teachings.teaching: What is life without wealth and honor? What is a friend who is fearful? Follow the lion's vow; do not despair; a crow lives long eating offerings.
teachings.lens_note: Courage and self-respect are essential.

teachings.teaching: One who does not show compassion to self, guru, servants, or friends, what is the use of his life? A crow lives long eating offerings.
teachings.lens_note: Compassion is a key virtue.

teachings.teaching: One whose days pass without dharma, artha, and kama is like a blacksmith's bellows, breathing but not alive.
teachings.lens_note: Life without purpose is mere existence.

teachings.teaching: Self-reliance is fruitful; dependence is death; those dependent on others are dead while living.
teachings.lens_note: Independence is crucial for spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Low people are self-praising; a handful of mice is self-praising; a discontented low person is satisfied with little.
teachings.lens_note: Contentment is a virtue, but not complacency.

teachings.teaching: Six things are like bubbles: cloud's shadow, fire from straw, service to a fool, water on path, a prostitute's love, and friendship with a rogue.
teachings.lens_note: Unreliable; do not invest in them.

teachings.teaching: The world is not satisfied by sweet words alone; life is rooted in honor; when honor fades, where is happiness?
teachings.lens_note: Honor is essential for well-being.

teachings.teaching: The strength of the weak is the king; of a child, crying; of a fool, silence; of a thief, lying.
teachings.lens_note: Each has a characteristic defense; one should use appropriate means.

teachings.teaching: As a person studies scripture, so his intellect and knowledge shine.
teachings.lens_note: Learning is transformative.

teachings.teaching: As a person engages in good deeds, he becomes beloved everywhere.
teachings.lens_note: Action shapes character and reputation.

teachings.teaching: A person is destroyed by greed, negligence, and trust; therefore, avoid greed, negligence, and do not trust blindly.
teachings.lens_note: Three key vices to overcome.

teachings.teaching: Fear danger before it comes; when it arrives, face it fearlessly.
teachings.lens_note: Practical wisdom for dealing with adversity.

teachings.teaching: Remaining debt, fire, and disease grow again; do not leave remnants.
teachings.lens_note: Finish what you start; avoid half-measures.

teachings.teaching: Return a deed for a deed, injury for injury; I see no fault in that; treat the wicked accordingly.
teachings.lens_note: Reciprocal justice is acceptable against evil.

teachings.teaching: Avoid a friend who harms behind the back but speaks sweetly in front; such a deceitful friend is like an enemy.
teachings.lens_note: Beware of hypocrisy.

teachings.teaching: Even a good person is ruined by association with the wicked, as clear water is muddied by clay.
teachings.lens_note: Importance of good company.

teachings.teaching: A brahmin should be honored with all effort; one eats the remnants of a brahmin's meal; the wise do not commit sin.
teachings.lens_note: Respect for the priestly class and their role.

teachings.teaching: That is friendship which is done in absence; that is dharma which is done without deceit.
teachings.lens_note: Sincerity and selflessness define true virtue.

teachings.teaching: That is not an assembly where there are no elders; they are not elders who do not speak dharma; that is not dharma where there is no truth; that is not truth which is mixed with deceit.
teachings.lens_note: Definitions of true assembly, elder, dharma, and truth.

teachings.teaching: A brahmin among men is like the sun among lights; the head among limbs; truth is the best among vows.
teachings.lens_note: Hierarchy of values; truth is supreme.

teachings.teaching: That is auspicious where the mind is pleased; that is life which is not service to others; that is earned which is enjoyed by relatives; that is victory which is over enemies in battle.
teachings.lens_note: Definitions of true auspiciousness, life, wealth, and victory.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 115) presents a collection of moral and practical teachings on life, relationships, and conduct. It covers topics such as the transience of wealth and power, the importance of self-control, the duties of women, the value of education, and the dangers of greed and attachment. The chapter concludes with a note on the transmission of this wisdom from Vishnu to Shiva to Vyasa.

story.title: Teachings on Life and Conduct
story.arc: Didactic discourse
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of a larger dialogue where Shaunaka recounts the teachings of Vyasa, which originated from Vishnu via Shiva.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a series of aphorisms and moral instructions with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Wealth earned by one's own people and enjoyed, and the roar of enemies in battle, should not make a woman proud; one who is free from desire is happy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes detachment from external validation and the cultivation of inner contentment through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: A friend is one in whom there is trust; a person is one who has conquered the senses.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight that true friendship and self-mastery are fruits of sustained yogic discipline, where trust arises from inner purity.

teachings.teaching: Where affection and attachment are abandoned, and friendship is lost, only that which is praised by the self is commendable.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on self-reliance and inner discernment over external approval.

teachings.teaching: One should not investigate the origins of rivers, fire-sacrifices, the Bharata war, and time; the fault does not diminish from the root.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as a caution against intellectual speculation that distracts from direct spiritual experience.

teachings.teaching: Rivers end in salt water, sexual union ends in separation, slander ends in gossip, wealth ends in the threefold misery.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the impermanent nature of worldly phenomena, a core teaching in Kriya-Yoga for cultivating detachment.

teachings.teaching: Royal glory ends with a brahmin's curse, sin ends with brahminical splendor, a hermitage ends with noise, and a family ends with a woman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a reminder of the fragility of worldly status and the need for spiritual grounding.

teachings.teaching: All abodes end in destruction, all elevations end in fall, unions end in separation, and life ends in death.
teachings.lens_note: This is a classic statement of impermanence, central to Kriya-Yoga's practice of non-attachment.

teachings.teaching: If one wishes to return, one should not go too far; one should turn back from the water's edge and from a tree with smooth bark.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as a practical guideline for maintaining boundaries in spiritual practice, avoiding extremes.

teachings.teaching: One should not live where there is no leader, nor where there are many leaders, nor where a woman is the leader, nor where a child is the leader.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the importance of proper guidance in spiritual life, akin to the need for a guru in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: A father protects in childhood, a husband protects in youth, and a son protects in old age; a woman is never fit for independence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might contextualize this within traditional social norms, but in Kriya-Yoga, the inner self (Atman) is the ultimate protector.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon a barren wife after eight years, one who has lost her husband after nine, one who gives birth only to daughters after eleven, and one who speaks unpleasantly immediately.
teachings.lens_note: This verse reflects ancient social customs; Sharma would likely emphasize the underlying principle of harmony and duty.

teachings.teaching: Due to lack of desire among men and fear of dependents, three types of people remain with their husbands despite being devoid of means and boundaries.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as a commentary on the complexities of human relationships and the need for spiritual maturity.

teachings.teaching: A wise man should avoid a tired horse, a mad elephant, cows after first calving, and frogs in waterless places.
teachings.lens_note: This is practical wisdom; in Kriya-Yoga, it symbolizes avoiding situations that disturb mental equilibrium.

teachings.teaching: For those afflicted by wealth, there is no friend or relative; for those afflicted by lust, there is no fear or shame; for those afflicted by worry, there is no happiness or sleep; for those afflicted by hunger, there is no strength or vigor.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight how these afflictions (kleshas) are obstacles to yoga, to be overcome through practice.

teachings.teaching: How can there be sleep for the poor, for one who is a servant of others, for one attached to another's wife, or for one who steals another's wealth?
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the restlessness of a mind entangled in desires, a key theme in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: A man who is debt-free, free from disease, and not attached to women sleeps happily and eats with leisure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a description of the peace that comes from simplicity and self-control.

teachings.teaching: As the lotus grows tall according to the depth of water, so a servant becomes proud with a powerful master.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of dependence; in Kriya-Yoga, true strength comes from inner independence.

teachings.teaching: For a lotus in its place, the sun and water are friends; for one displaced, they cause drying and decay.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the importance of staying in one's natural state (dharma) for spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Those who are friends when one is in a position become enemies when one falls; the sun's love for the lotus in water becomes drying when taken to land.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches the fickleness of worldly relationships, encouraging reliance on the eternal Self.

teachings.teaching: Things in their proper place are honored; hair, teeth, nails, and men when displaced are not honored.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize the importance of alignment with one's true nature and purpose.

teachings.teaching: Conduct reveals family, speech reveals region, respect reveals affection, and body reveals food.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the external manifestations of inner qualities; in Kriya-Yoga, inner purity reflects outwardly.

teachings.teaching: Rain on the ocean is useless, food for the full is useless, charity for the rich is useless, and good deeds for a low person are useless.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a call for appropriate action, aligning with the principle of right effort in yoga.

teachings.teaching: One who is distant but in the heart is near; one who has left the heart, though near, is far.
teachings.lens_note: This speaks to the power of emotional connection; in Kriya-Yoga, the heart is the seat of divine love.

teachings.teaching: Facial distortion, weak voice, perspiration, and great fear are signs of death; these same signs appear in a beggar.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as a warning against the spiritual death that comes from dependency and loss of self-respect.

teachings.teaching: For a hunchback, one struck by insects, one driven out by wind, and one living on a peak, it is better not to beg for birth.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the dignity of self-reliance; in Kriya-Yoga, begging for spiritual favors is discouraged.

teachings.teaching: Even the lord of the world, Vishnu, became a dwarf by begging; who else is greater than he? Yet his purpose did not lead to degradation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note that even divine acts of humility are for a higher purpose, not for personal gain.

teachings.teaching: A mother is an enemy, a father is a foe, if they do not educate a child; they do not shine in an assembly, like a crane among swans.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of education; in Kriya-Yoga, spiritual education is paramount.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge is the best form of beauty, a hidden treasure, a source of goodness, pleasing to people, the guru of gurus, a destroyer of family sorrow, the supreme deity, and honored among kings; a man without knowledge is an animal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would wholeheartedly endorse this, as Kriya-Yoga is a path of knowledge (jnana) and self-realization.

teachings.teaching: Wealth kept inside the house is visible and can be stolen entirely, but knowledge cannot be taken by others.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the imperishable nature of knowledge, a key tenet in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the fruits of worshiping various deities on specific lunar days (tithis) and weekdays. It prescribes which deity to worship on each tithi to obtain desired results such as wealth, grain, sons, kingdom, and victory. The chapter also mentions worship of planets, constellations, and yogas.

story.title: Tithi Worship and Its Fruits
story.arc: Instructional discourse by Brahma on the benefits of worshiping specific deities on specific lunar days.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: Worshiping Hari (Vishnu) with one meal, fasting, or other observances on specific tithis grants wealth, grain, sons, kingdom, and victory.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that disciplined ritual practice (kriya) aligned with cosmic rhythms yields tangible spiritual and material results, integrating action with devotion.

teachings.teaching: Each tithi is associated with a specific deity; worshiping that deity on that day fulfills desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a map of the subtle energies of time, where attuning one's actions to the divine presence in each moment accelerates spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Anaṅgatrayodaśīvrata, a thirteen-day vow dedicated to Śiva, detailing the specific flowers, toothsticks, and offerings for each month from Mārgaśīrṣa to Kārttika, along with the concluding rites and benefits.

story.title: Anaṅgatrayodaśīvrata
story.arc: Description of a vow dedicated to Śiva with monthly variations
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes a ritual cycle spanning the twelve months from Mārgaśīrṣa to Kārttika.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a ritual manual with no narrative.

teachings.teaching: The Anaṅgatrayodaśīvrata involves worshiping Śiva with specific flowers, toothsticks, and offerings each month, culminating in a concluding ceremony with donations and a homa.
Performing this vow with devotion brings benefits such as wealth, sons, health, auspiciousness, and attainment of heaven.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī vow, its procedure, and the benefits of observing it. The vow involves fasting on the twelfth day of the bright fortnight in the month of Mārgaśīrṣa, worshipping Viṣṇu, and donating a pot filled with five grains to a brāhmaṇa. The devotee prays for the vow to remain unbroken across seven births, and the chapter also mentions other similar vows like the Agastya Arghya vow.

story.title: Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī Vrata
story.arc: Instruction on the vow and its benefits
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Ācārakāṇḍa in the Pūrvakhaṇḍa of the Garuḍa Purāṇa.
story.comic_potential: A devotee performing the vow with offerings and prayers, with Viṣṇu appearing to grant blessings.

teachings.teaching: Observing the Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī vow with fasting and worship of Viṣṇu leads to liberation and fulfillment of desires.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this vow represents a disciplined practice (kriya) that aligns the devotee's actions with divine will, purifying the mind and leading to spiritual wholeness (akhaṇḍa).

teachings.teaching: Donating a pot filled with five grains to a brāhmaṇa is part of the vow.
teachings.lens_note: This act of charity (dāna) is a kriya that cultivates detachment and generosity, essential for inner purification.

teachings.teaching: The vow is to be kept unbroken across seven births, symbolizing continuity of spiritual effort.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that consistent practice (abhyāsa) over lifetimes is necessary to attain the unbroken state of union with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Agastya Arghya Vrata, a ritual offering to the sage Agastya during the transition of the sun into Virgo, which is said to bestow both worldly enjoyment and liberation. The procedure involves preparing a pot with various items, worshipping an image of Agastya made of kasha flowers, and offering arghya with a specific mantra. The chapter also mentions the exclusion of certain castes from the offering and the donation of the pot to a Brahmin.

story.title: The Agastya Arghya Vrata
story.arc: Ritual instruction
story.timeline_note: The ritual is to be performed when the sun is about to enter Virgo, within three days of that transition.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a straightforward ritual manual.

teachings.teaching: The Agastya Arghya Vrata, performed at the specified time, grants bhukti (worldly enjoyment) and mukti (liberation).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this ritual can be seen as a kriya (purificatory action) that aligns the practitioner with cosmic rhythms, facilitating the flow of prana and leading to spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The offering should be made with a pot containing seven grains, gold, silver, curd, sandalwood, and kasha flowers, accompanied by a specific mantra.
teachings.lens_note: The use of specific materials and mantra corresponds to the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on precise technique to channel energy and invoke divine presence.

teachings.teaching: The mantra honors Agastya as born from Mitra and Varuna, and as Kumbhayoni (pot-born).
teachings.lens_note: This mantra invokes the lineage and potency of Agastya, reinforcing the practitioner's connection to the sage's transformative energy.

teachings.teaching: The pot should be given to a Brahmin along with gold and dakshina, and seven Brahmins should be fed; the practitioner then enjoys the fruits of the vrata for a year.
teachings.lens_note: The act of giving (dana) is a key kriya that purifies the practitioner and generates merit, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga principle of selfless action.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the 'Rambha Tritiya' vow, detailing the specific flowers, food offerings, and toothwood types to be used for worshipping various forms of the goddess (Gauri, Girisuta, Subhadra, Gomati, Vishalakshi, Shri Mukhi, Narayani, Madhavi, Shri, Uttama, Rajaputri, Padmaja) in each month from Margashirsha to Kartika, along with the associated observances and donations.

story.title: Rambha Tritiya Vrata
story.arc: Description of a monthly vow cycle
story.timeline_note: The vow is observed over the twelve months from Margashirsha to Kartika, on the third day of the bright fortnight.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a ritual manual with no narrative.

teachings.teaching: Worship Gauri with bilva leaves and kuśa water on the third day of the bright fortnight in Margashirsha.
Worship Girisuta with marubaka flowers in Pausha, and use karpura and krisara as offerings.
Worship Subhadra with kalhara flowers in Magha, and use ghee and mandaka as offerings.
Worship Gomati with kunda flowers in Phalguna, and offer shashkuli.
Worship Vishalakshi with damanaka flowers in Chaitra, and offer krisara.
Worship Shri Mukhi with tagara flowers in Vaishakha, and offer ashoka and vata.
Worship Narayani with shatapatra flowers in Jyeshtha, and offer khanda.
Worship Madhavi with bilva leaves in Ashadha, and offer kshiranna and vataka.
Worship Shri with audumbara toothwood in Shravana.
Worship Uttama with mallika toothwood and kshira offerings in Bhadrapada.
Worship Rajaputri with japa flowers in Ashvayuja, and offer jiraka.
Worship Padmaja with jati flowers in Kartika, and offer panchagavya.
At the end of the year, worship Umamaheshvara and donate cloth, umbrella, gold, etc., with night vigil and music.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Cāturmāsya vows, including observances on Ekādaśī, starting from Āṣāḍha full moon, and the benefits of various fasting and dietary practices leading to liberation and attainment of Viṣṇu's abode.

story.title: Cāturmāsya Vrata Nirūpaṇa
story.arc: Description of the four-month vow, its observances, and the spiritual rewards including attainment of Viṣṇu's world and liberation.
story.timeline_note: The vow begins on the full moon of Āṣāḍha and continues for four months.
story.comic_potential: A visual sequence showing a devotee performing the vow, culminating in a celestial journey to Viṣṇu's abode.

teachings.teaching: Observing Cāturmāsya vows, including Ekādaśī fasting and worship of Viṣṇu, destroys all sins and leads to Viṣṇu's realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes disciplined practice (kriya) over time, aligning with the gradual purification of the mind and body.

teachings.teaching: Specific dietary restrictions (e.g., abstaining from wine, meat, oil) and fasting periods (one night, three nights, etc.) yield progressively higher spiritual rewards, culminating in liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as practical steps to reduce tamasic influences and increase sattva, essential for yogic progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Māsopavāsa vrata, a month-long fast observed from the eleventh day of the bright fortnight of Āśvina until the awakening of Viṣṇu in Kārtika. It details the rules, including daily worship, avoidance of body oil and perfumes, and the breaking of the fast on the twelfth day with a feast for Brahmins. The chapter emphasizes that even if the devotee becomes unconscious, consuming milk or similar items does not break the vow and grants both enjoyment and liberation.

story.title: The Māsopavāsa Vrata
story.arc: Instruction on a month-long fast vow dedicated to Viṣṇu
story.timeline_note: The vrata spans from the eleventh day of the bright fortnight of Āśvina to the awakening of Viṣṇu in Kārtika.
story.comic_potential: A devotee's determination to complete the fast despite fainting, with milk as a saving grace.

teachings.teaching: The Māsopavāsa vrata is the highest among all vows, suitable for forest-dwellers, ascetics, and women.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the highest practice is accessible to all, regardless of station, and involves disciplined inner purification.

teachings.teaching: The vow is taken on the eleventh day of the bright fortnight of Āśvina and lasts for thirty days until Viṣṇu's awakening.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on precise timing and cyclic rhythms for spiritual practices.

teachings.teaching: The devotee should worship Viṣṇu daily, bathe thrice, avoid oil massage and perfumes, and not enter temples.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets these as practices to reduce sensory indulgence and maintain purity of body and mind.

teachings.teaching: On the twelfth day, after worship, one should feed Brahmins and then break the fast.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of integrating service (seva) with personal practice.

teachings.teaching: If the devotee becomes unconscious during the fast, consuming milk or similar items does not break the vow and grants both enjoyment and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as a compassionate allowance, emphasizing that the inner intention (bhāva) is more important than rigid external rules.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Bhishmapanchaka vow and other Kartika observances, detailing rituals such as bathing, worship of Vishnu, fasting, and offerings. It emphasizes the importance of Ekadashi and specific tithis for spiritual merit and liberation.

story.title: Bhishmapanchaka and Kartika Vows
story.arc: Instructional discourse on Kartika observances, culminating in the Bhishmapanchaka vow and Ekadashi importance.
story.timeline_note: Set during Kartika month, with specific tithis mentioned.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: Observing vows in Kartika, especially Bhishmapanchaka, purifies sins and grants desires.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes disciplined action (kriya) as a means to purify karma and align with divine will.

teachings.teaching: Ekadashi fasting is a supreme vow; Hari resides where Ekadashi is observed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the power of rhythmic discipline (kriya) to invoke divine presence.

chapter_summary: Lord Brahma narrates the glory of the Shivaratri vrata, including the story of a sinful hunter who unknowingly performed the vrata and attained liberation. The chapter details the procedure for observing the vrata, including fasting, worship, and vigil on the night of Krishna Chaturdashi in the month of Magha-Phalguna.

story.title: The Glory of Shivaratri Vrata
story.arc: Brahma narrates the story of a hunter who unknowingly performed Shivaratri and was liberated, followed by the detailed procedure for observing the vrata.
story.timeline_note: The story is set in the context of Brahma recounting the conversation between Gauri and Shiva.
story.comic_potential: The hunter's accidental worship—throwing water and leaves on a linga, and a flower falling from his hand—has a humorous, ironic twist.

teachings.teaching: Even unintentional worship of Shiva on Shivaratri yields great merit and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this illustrates that sincere action (kriya) performed without ego can purify karma, even if done unknowingly.

teachings.teaching: The procedure for Shivaratri vrata includes fasting, worship with panchagavya and amrita, offering of sesame, rice, and ghee, chanting of the mantra 'Om Namah Shivaya', and keeping vigil at night.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize the disciplined practice (kriya) of these rituals as a means to channel energy and focus the mind on the divine.

teachings.teaching: The vrata culminates with seeking forgiveness from Shiva and performing acts of charity, leading to worldly prosperity and ultimately liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see the forgiveness prayer as a surrender of the ego, a key step in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter discusses the rules and importance of observing Ekadashi fast, including which lunar days to combine or avoid, and the story of King Rukmangada who attained liberation through Ekadashi observance.

story.title: Ekadashi Mahatmya
story.arc: Instruction on Ekadashi observance and its fruits
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; references to King Mandhata and King Rukmangada
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily didactic with brief narrative examples

teachings.teaching: One should not eat on Ekadashi of both fortnights.
Avoid Ekadashi mixed with Dashami; Gandhari observed such and lost her hundred sons.
When Ekadashi coincides with Dwadashi, Hari is present; when with Dashami, a demon is present.
If doubt arises due to conflicting statements, take Dwadashi and break fast on Trayodashi.
Ekadashi, Dwadashi, and especially Trayodashi combined are auspicious and destroy all sins.
One should observe fast on Ekadashi or Dwadashi, or the mixed three-day fast, but never with Dashami.
By keeping vigil at night, listening to Puranas, worshipping Gadadhara, and fasting on Ekadashi and Dwadashi, King Rukmangada attained liberation.

chapter_summary: Brahma describes the arcane worship (arcana) that leads to liberation and enjoyment. He details the construction of a general mandala, placement of deities and powers, and the sequence of worship including the lotus, energies, and the four Vyuhas.

story.title: The Arcane Worship for Liberation
story.arc: Brahma teaches the method of worship that grants both enjoyment and liberation.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; instructional discourse.
story.comic_potential: A detailed ritual manual; low narrative drama.

teachings.teaching: The worship (arcana) described leads to the supreme goal (paramāṃ gatim) and grants both enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that ritual action (kriya) when performed with inner awareness becomes a means to unite bhukti and mukti, integrating worldly life with spiritual liberation.

teachings.teaching: The mandala should include Dhata, Vidhata, Ganga, Yamuna, Mahānadī, Dvāraśrī, Daṇḍa, Pracaṇḍa, Vāstupūruṣa, Ādhāraśakti, Kūrma, Ananta, Bhūmi, Dharma, Jñāna, Vairāgya, Aiśvarya, Adharma, the lotus stalk, pericarp, filaments, the three guṇas, solar discs, powers like Vimalā, Durgā, Gaṇa, Sarasvatī, Kṣetrapāla, and then worship the seat and the mūrti of Vāsudeva, Bala, Aniruddha, and Nārāyaṇa.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this elaborate mandala as a map of the inner cosmos: each deity and power corresponds to a layer of consciousness or energy in the practitioner's subtle body. The sequential worship is a kriya-yoga practice of internal purification and ascent.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Bhima Dvadashi, a sacred fast observed on the eleventh day of the bright fortnight in the month of Magha when the sun is in the constellation Shravana. It extols the power of this fast to destroy great sins, including brahminicide, and compares it favorably to holy places and other meritorious acts. The chapter also provides detailed instructions for the ritual worship of Lord Varaha (the boar incarnation of Vishnu) on this day, including the construction of an image, offerings, and the recitation of the Purana. Observing this fast with devotion is said to free one from the three debts and grant all desires.

story.title: The Glory of Bhima Dvadashi
story.arc: Description of the Bhima Dvadashi fast, its benefits, and the ritual worship of Varaha.
story.timeline_note: The fast is observed on the eleventh day of the bright fortnight of Magha when the sun is in Shravana.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic contrast between the power of the fast and the sins it destroys, along with the vivid description of the ritual, could be visually engaging.

teachings.teaching: Observing the Ekadashi fast on the specific day (Bhima Dvadashi) destroys great sins like brahminicide.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of disciplined action (kriya) aligned with cosmic rhythms to purify karma.

teachings.teaching: The fast is more meritorious than holy places, gifts, and other rituals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the superiority of internal discipline over external observances; the fast is a concentrated practice of self-control.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Varaha with specific mantras and offerings leads to liberation from the three debts and fulfillment of desires.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on devotion (bhakti) as a form of inner purification and alignment with the divine will.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, titled 'Vrataparibhāṣā', defines the rules and observances for various vows (vratas). It details the niyamas (restraints), daily practices, forbidden items during fasting, the ten common dharmas for all vows, the procedure for the Brahmakūrca vow, and the different types of months (cāndra, sāvana, saura, nākṣatra). It also discusses the effects of breaking a vow and the remedies, including fasting and shaving the head.

story.title: Vrataparibhāṣā: Rules for Vows
story.arc: Instructional discourse on the proper observance of vows, including their definitions, practices, and consequences.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana, likely narrated by Vyāsa to the assembly.
story.comic_potential: Low; this is a prescriptive text with detailed rules, not a narrative.

teachings.teaching: Vows (vratas) are defined as niyamas (restraints) and are considered a form of austerity (tapas).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the concept of tapas as a disciplined practice that purifies the mind and body, essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Daily practices include bathing three times a day, sleeping on the ground, and controlling the senses.
teachings.lens_note: These external disciplines support internal purification, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on sensory control and regularity.

teachings.teaching: One should avoid speaking with women, Śūdras, and fallen persons during a vow.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the need to maintain a pure environment for spiritual practice, avoiding distractions and negative influences.

teachings.teaching: The ten common dharmas for all vows are: forgiveness, truth, compassion, charity, purity, sense control, worship of gods, fire offerings, contentment, and non-stealing.
teachings.lens_note: These virtues are foundational in Kriya-Yoga for cultivating a sattvic mind and aligning with dharma.

teachings.teaching: If a vow is broken due to anger, carelessness, or greed, one should fast for three days and shave the head as expiation.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the importance of commitment and the need for atonement to restore purity, resonating with the Kriya-Yoga principle of prāyaścitta.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various vratas (vows) and rituals associated with specific tithis (lunar days), including the worship of deities such as Shiva, Parvati, Ganesha, and Nagas. It details the procedures, offerings, and benefits of observing these vows, emphasizing the attainment of worldly desires, health, and liberation.

story.title: Vratas and Rituals for Various Tithis
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a discourse by Brahma, likely set in a timeless mythological context.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a list of ritual instructions with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Observing vows on specific tithis with offerings and worship leads to fulfillment of desires and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of disciplined practice (kriya) and devotion (bhakti) to achieve spiritual goals.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the rituals and observances for the Saptami (seventh lunar day) vratas, including Phala Saptami and Vijaya Saptami, with specific rules for bathing, worship of the Sun god, offerings, fasting, and donations to Brahmins.

story.title: Saptami Vrata Nirupana
story.arc: Description of Saptami vratas and their procedures
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; ritual instructions.

teachings.teaching: On the seventh lunar day of Bhadrapada month, one should worship Kartikeya; all acts of bathing and charity on that day yield inexhaustible results.
On Saptami, one should feed Brahmins and worship the Sun with the mantra 'Om Khakholkaya amritatvam...'.
On Ashtami, one should break the fast by consuming maricha (black pepper) after bathing and worshipping the Sun on Saptami.
Offer fruits to Brahmins with the mantra 'May Martanda be pleased'; specific fruits like dates, coconut, or bilva are recommended.
After worshipping the Sun on Saptami, one should feed Brahmins with payasa (rice pudding) and give dakshina, then drink milk oneself.
For those desiring wealth or sons, they should avoid rice and instead subsist on air or conquer hunger to perform Vijaya Saptami.
One should avoid wheat, beans, barley, shashtika rice, copper vessels, stone-ground flour, honey, meat, oil massage, collyrium, and sesame seeds during the seven Saptamis to achieve desired results.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes two vratas (religious observances): Dūrvāṣṭamī and Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī. Dūrvāṣṭamī is observed on the eighth day of the bright fortnight of Bhādrapada, involving worship of Dūrvā (a grass), Sūrya (the Sun), Gaṇeśa, and Śiva. Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī is observed on the eighth day of the dark fortnight when Rohiṇī nakṣatra is present, involving worship of Kṛṣṇa with various names and mantras, and offering arghya to the Moon and Rohiṇī. The chapter emphasizes the power of these observances to destroy sins and grant liberation.

story.title: Dūrvāṣṭamī and Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī Vratas
story.arc: Ritual Observances
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes two specific days in the Hindu calendar: the eighth day of the bright fortnight of Bhādrapada (Dūrvāṣṭamī) and the eighth day of the dark fortnight when Rohiṇī nakṣatra is present (Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī).
story.comic_potential: The chapter is instructional, detailing rituals and mantras. It could be visualized as a guidebook with illustrations of the deities and the ritual procedures.

teachings.teaching: Observing Dūrvāṣṭamī vrata with offerings of dūrvā grass, fruits, and flowers to Śiva and others destroys the sin of brahmahatyā (killing a Brahmin).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this vrata represents a kriya (purificatory action) that aligns the practitioner with cosmic order, removing karmic impurities through disciplined ritual.

teachings.teaching: Observing Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī vrata with worship of Kṛṣṇa using various names and mantras, and offering arghya to the Moon and Rohiṇī, destroys sins accumulated over three births.
teachings.lens_note: This vrata exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of using mantra and ritual to transcend past karma. The repetition of divine names (nāmānyetāni saṃkīrtya) is a form of japa kriya that purifies the mind and invokes divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The devotee prays to Viṣṇu to be saved from the ocean of saṃsāra (worldly existence) and from sins, acknowledging that without Him there is no protector.
teachings.lens_note: This prayer reflects the Kriya-Yoga goal of liberation (moksha) through surrender to the divine. The repeated 'trāhi mām' (save me) is a cry for divine intervention, which in Kriya-Yoga is complemented by active yogic practices.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Budha Ashtami vrata (Mercury's eighth-day vow), its rules, and a story illustrating its power. The narrative follows a Brahmin named Vira, his wife Rambha, their son Kaushika, and daughter Vijaya. Through observing the vrata, they attain liberation and heavenly rewards.

story.title: Budha Ashtami Vrata and the Story of Kaushika
story.arc: The chapter begins with Brahma describing the vrata rules, then narrates the story of Kaushika and his family who, through performing the vrata, overcome hardships and attain liberation.
story.timeline_note: The story is set in the city of Pataliputra, with events unfolding over a single day and night, culminating in the family's liberation.
story.comic_potential: The scene where Kaushika and Vijaya, after performing the vrata, find a pot of gold given by thieves, and the subsequent family drama, has comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: Observing the Budha Ashtami vrata with devotion and proper rituals leads to liberation and heavenly rewards.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this vrata can be seen as a practice of disciplined action (kriya) that purifies the mind and aligns one with cosmic rhythms, leading to spiritual freedom.

teachings.teaching: The story illustrates that even in difficult circumstances, performing the vrata with faith can bring about miraculous results and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that the vrata is a form of kriya-yoga that transforms karma, as seen when Kaushika and Vijaya's sincere practice leads to unexpected blessings.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the rituals and mantras associated with the Mahānavamīvrata, including the Aśokāṣṭamī vow, the worship of Durgā with specific iconography, and the offering of animal sacrifices. It details the benefits of observing these rites on specific lunar days and asterisms.

story.title: Mahānavamīvrata
story.arc: Ritual instructions for the great ninth-day vow, including the Aśokāṣṭamī vow, worship of Durgā with detailed iconography, and animal sacrifice.
story.timeline_note: The rituals are prescribed for specific lunar days: Caitra śuklāṣṭamī (Aśokāṣṭamī) and Āśvayuja śuklāṣṭamī (Mahānavamī).
story.comic_potential: The vivid description of Durgā's many arms holding weapons and the animal sacrifice could be depicted dramatically.

teachings.teaching: Observing the Aśokāṣṭamī vow on Caitra śuklāṣṭamī with the asterism Punarvasu, by drinking the eight buds of the Aśoka tree, frees one from sorrow.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this ritual symbolizes the internal practice of consuming the 'buds' of negative emotions (śoka) through mindful awareness, transforming them into peace.

teachings.teaching: The Mahānavamī on Āśvayuja śuklāṣṭamī with Uttarāṣāḍhā is highly meritorious; bathing and charity on that day yield inexhaustible results.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as aligning one's actions with cosmic rhythms (ṛta), where the 'inexhaustible' results come from acting in harmony with the natural order.

teachings.teaching: Worship Durgā with the mantra 'durge durge rakṣiṇi svāhā' and meditate on her form with specific weapons and attributes.
teachings.lens_note: The mantra and visualization are tools for Kriya-Yoga practitioners to invoke the protective, transformative energy of the Divine Mother within.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the ten-syllable mantra (daśākṣarī vidyā) makes one invincible.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this mantra corresponds to the ten vital energies (prāṇas); mastering them through recitation grants control over life forces.

teachings.teaching: Offer a buffalo or goat at the end of the night on Mahānavamī.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as symbolic of sacrificing the animalistic tendencies (tamas) within, not literal killing, as the text may be interpreted esoterically.

teachings.teaching: Offer oblations to various demonesses (Pūtanā, etc.) in different directions to appease them.
teachings.lens_note: These represent internal obstacles; the directional offerings are a Kriya-Yoga technique to balance the five elements and subdue negative tendencies.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Mahakaushika mantra and the rituals associated with the Maha Navami festival, including the worship of various goddesses and the offering of a bali (sacrifice).

story.title: Mahakaushika Mantra and Maha Navami Rituals
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; ritual instructions.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily ritualistic.

teachings.teaching: The Mahakaushika mantra is powerful and should be used to consecrate offerings (bali) to Nirṛti.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformative power of sound (mantra) in ritual action, aligning inner intention with outer practice.

teachings.teaching: On Maha Navami, one should worship the goddesses including Brahmāṇī, Māheśī, etc., and perform rituals like bathing the deity with milk and offering food to brahmins.
