teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of devotion (bhakti) as a form of inner purification and alignment with cosmic forces.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with specific tithis (lunar days) in the months of Ashvin and Chaitra. It details the procedures, offerings, and benefits of observing Vira Navami, Damanaka Navami, Dig Dashami, and Rishi Ekadashi. The chapter emphasizes the importance of worshiping deities, offering specific items, and reciting mantras to attain health, wealth, victory, and ultimately, liberation or a high spiritual status.

story.title: Vratas for Specific Tithis
story.arc: The chapter presents a series of vratas (vows) prescribed by Brahma, each associated with a specific lunar day and offering particular benefits. The narrative is instructional, with Brahma as the speaker.
story.timeline_note: The vratas are tied to the lunar calendar, specifically the months of Ashvin and Chaitra.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a straightforward list of rituals without dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: On the bright ninth of Ashvin, one should worship the Devi with a single meal and recite a lakh of mantras to observe Vira Navami.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes disciplined practice (kriya) and focused repetition (japa) as a means to purify the mind and attain spiritual strength.

teachings.teaching: On the bright ninth of Chaitra, worship the Devi with Damanaka flowers to obtain long life, health, prosperity, and victory over enemies.
teachings.lens_note: The use of specific flowers (Damanaka) symbolizes the offering of one's senses and actions to the divine, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga's active surrender.

teachings.teaching: Observing Dig Dashami with one meal and donating ten cows and golden directions at the end makes one the lord of the universe.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching highlights the principle of selfless action (karma yoga) and the symbolic offering of the ten directions, representing mastery over one's environment through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: On Ekadashi, worship the sages (Rishis) to become wealthy, have sons, and attain the world of the sages after death.
teachings.lens_note: Worshiping the sages aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on honoring the lineage of teachers (guru parampara) as a means to receive blessings and spiritual guidance.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the significance of the Vijayā Ekādaśī, which occurs when the eleventh and twelfth lunar days coincide with the Śravaṇa nakṣatra. It prescribes fasting rules, worship procedures including a specific mantra for offering to Vāmana (an avatar of Viṣṇu), and the benefits of observing this vow.

story.title: The Glory of Vijayā Ekādaśī
story.arc: Instruction on the observance of a sacred day
story.timeline_note: Mythological time; the teaching is given by Brahma.
story.comic_potential: A visual depiction of the fasting rules and the worship of Vāmana with specific offerings.

teachings.teaching: The Vijayā Ekādaśī, when the eleventh and twelfth lunar days coincide with Śravaṇa nakṣatra, grants both enjoyment and liberation. Observing it with fasting (ekabhakta, nakta, ayācita, upavāsa) and avoiding certain foods and activities (e.g., meat, honey, lying, exercise, sex, day sleep, collyrium, certain grains) yields great merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching emphasizes the purification of the body and mind through disciplined fasting and abstention, which are preparatory practices for deeper meditation and union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Bathing at the confluence of rivers on this day is highly meritorious. Worship Vāmana with gold, white cloth, umbrella, and sandals, reciting specific mantras to different parts of the body.
teachings.lens_note: The Kriya-Yoga lens sees the ritual bathing and worship as symbolic of inner purification and the offering of one's senses and actions to the divine, aligning with the practice of surrendering the ego.

teachings.teaching: Offer ghee-rice pudding and sweetmeats, and stay awake at night. After bathing and worship, recite a prayer to Govinda (who is identified with Budha and Śravaṇa) for the destruction of sins and bestowal of happiness.
teachings.lens_note: The night vigil (jāgaraṇa) in Kriya-Yoga represents sustained awareness and meditation, while the prayer is a form of mantra repetition that focuses the mind on the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata (the vow of the twelfth lunar day of Śravaṇa month) and its benefits, including the destruction of sins and the attainment of all desires. It also mentions the ritual of giving pots to Brahmins at a riverbank.

story.title: Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata Nirūpaṇa
story.arc: Description of the vow and its merits
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline given
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily ritualistic instruction

teachings.teaching: Performing the Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata destroys sins and grants all happiness.
Giving pots to Brahmins at a riverbank fulfills all desires.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with specific tithis (lunar days), nakshatras (constellations), and days of the week. It details the deities to be worshipped on each tithi, the benefits of fasting and offerings, and concludes with a prayer to Acyuta (Vishnu) for the removal of sins and the attainment of desires.

story.title: Tithi Vrata Nirupana
story.arc: Instruction on Vratas
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the chapter is a prescriptive text on vratas.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a list of rituals and deities without narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Kama on trayodashi with damanaka flowers and offerings to Rati, Priti, and Asoka brings blessings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this external worship of Kama symbolizes the internal cultivation of desire for union with the divine, transforming mundane longing into spiritual aspiration.

teachings.teaching: Fasting on chaturdashi and ashtami for a year, along with Shiva worship, leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets fasting as control of the senses and mind, and Shiva worship as meditation on the formless aspect of the self, leading to moksha.

teachings.teaching: Observing a three-night fast on Kartika and donating a house leads to attaining Surya's world.
teachings.lens_note: The three-night fast represents a period of intense purification; the donation symbolizes detachment from material possessions, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on renunciation.

teachings.teaching: Offerings to ancestors on amavasya are imperishable.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the continuity of karma and the importance of honoring one's lineage, which in Kriya-Yoga is seen as a practice of gratitude and karmic cleansing.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Kesava in Margashirsha with ghee homa and krisara offering for four months brings benefits.
teachings.lens_note: The four-month period (chaturmasya) is a time for intensified spiritual practice; ghee homa symbolizes offering of one's inner essence, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's fire of self-transformation.

teachings.teaching: Naivedya offered before visarjana is pure; after visarjana it becomes nirmalya.
teachings.lens_note: This distinction teaches the importance of timing and intention in ritual; in Kriya-Yoga, it reflects the need to offer actions before ego arises, keeping them pure.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Vishnu on ekadashi, ashtami, chaturdashi, and saptami leads to attainment of their worlds.
teachings.lens_note: These tithis are considered spiritually potent; regular worship on them aligns the practitioner with cosmic rhythms, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga's harmonization with nature.

teachings.teaching: Fasting with ekabhakta, nakta, ayacita, or upavasa and worship of all deities brings bhukti and mukti.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees these fasting types as progressive stages of self-discipline, culminating in upavasa (complete fast) which symbolizes total surrender to the divine.

teachings.teaching: Specific deities are worshipped on each tithi: Dhanada and Agni on pratipada, Nasatya and Dastra on dvitīya, etc.
teachings.lens_note: This mapping of deities to tithis provides a structured path for daily devotion, which in Kriya-Yoga is a method to invoke specific energies for spiritual growth.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana lists the dynasties of kings descended from Manu, starting from Brahma born from Vishnu's navel, through Daksha, and then the solar dynasty (Suryavansha) including Ikshvaku, Mandhata, Sagara, Bhagiratha, and the Rama lineage, among others.

story.title: Genealogy of the Solar Dynasty
story.arc: A continuous lineage from Brahma to Rama and beyond, emphasizing the divine origin and historical continuity of the Ikshvaku kings.
story.timeline_note: The genealogy spans from creation to the Ramayana era and later generations.
story.comic_potential: The long list of names could be visualized as a family tree with notable figures like Rama and Bhagiratha highlighted.

teachings.teaching: The kings of the solar dynasty are descendants of Vishnu, emphasizing the divine nature of righteous rule.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this lineage symbolizes the continuity of dharma through disciplined action (kriya) and devotion, where each king represents a step in spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: The genealogy shows the importance of lineage and tradition in maintaining cosmic order.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the necessity of following the path of one's predecessors (guru parampara) in yoga practice, ensuring the transmission of wisdom.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 138, part 2) continues the description of the Surya Vamsha (Solar Dynasty), listing a genealogy of kings from Suparshva to Janaka, with an emphasis on the lineage's connection to yoga.

story.title: Surya Vamsha Genealogy (continued)
story.arc: Genealogical listing of the Solar Dynasty from Suparshva to Janaka, with a note that the lineage of Janaka is associated with yoga.
story.timeline_note: The chapter presents a linear succession of kings; no specific time periods are given.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a dry genealogical list with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: The lineage of Janaka is associated with the practice of yoga (yogasamashraya).
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga commentary, this indicates that the royal lineage of Janaka embodies the integration of worldly duty (kingship) with the inner path of yoga, exemplifying the Kriya-Yoga ideal of action in awareness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 139, part 1) narrates the lunar dynasty (Somavaṃśa) lineage, starting from Brahmā born from Nārāyaṇa, through Atri, Soma, Budha, Purūravas, and many subsequent kings and sages, including the Yadu and Vṛṣṇi lines leading to Vasudeva and Kṛṣṇa's ancestors.

story.title: Chapter 139 (part 1)
story.arc: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 139, part 1) narrates the lunar dynasty (Somavaṃśa) lineage, starting from Brahmā born from Nārāyaṇa, through Atri, Soma, Budha, Purūravas, and many subsequent kings and sages, including the Yadu and Vṛṣṇi lines leading to Vasudeva and Kṛṣṇa's ancestors.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 139, part 2) details the genealogies of the lunar dynasty (Candravaṃśa), focusing on the descendants of King Kunti and others, including the births of the Pandavas, Kauravas, Yadavas, and various other royal lineages. It lists the wives and children of Vasudeva (including Krishna and Balarama), the lineage of Pradyumna and Aniruddha, and the dynasties of Turvasu, Druhyu, Anu, and others, culminating in the birth of Karna and his son Vrishasena.

story.title: Chapter 139 (part 2)
story.arc: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 139, part 2) details the genealogies of the lunar dynasty (Candravaṃśa), focusing on the descendants of King Kunti and others, including the births of the Pandavas, Kauravas, Yadavas, and various other royal lineages. It lists the wives and children of Vasudeva (including Krishna and Balarama), the lineage of Pradyumna and Aniruddha, and the dynasties of Turvasu, Druhyu, Anu, and others, culminating in the birth of Karna and his son Vrishasena.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana presents a detailed genealogy of the Lunar Dynasty (Candravaṃśa), tracing the lineage from Janamejaya through various kings and sages, including the Kauravas and Pandavas, culminating in Parīkṣit and Janamejaya. It lists numerous rulers and their descendants, with cross-references to figures from the Mahabharata.

story.title: Chapter 140
story.arc: This chapter of the Garuda Purana presents a detailed genealogy of the Lunar Dynasty (Candravaṃśa), tracing the lineage from Janamejaya through various kings and sages, including the Kauravas and Pandavas, culminating in Parīkṣit and Janamejaya. It lists numerous rulers and their descendants, with cross-references to figures from the Mahabharata.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana lists the future kings of the Ikshvaku and Brihadratha dynasties, culminating in a discourse on the nature of creation and dissolution, emphasizing that all beings are subject to destruction and urging the practice of dharma to attain Hari.

story.title: Future Royal Dynasties and Cosmic Dissolution
story.arc: The chapter lists the future kings of the Ikshvaku and Brihadratha lines, then transitions to a philosophical discourse on the cyclical dissolution of the universe, culminating in the teaching that only Vishnu (Narayana) is eternal and one should abandon sin and practice dharma to reach Him.
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes future kings and then the cosmic dissolution, which is a cyclical event.
story.comic_potential: The long list of names could be visualized as a genealogical scroll, and the dissolution sequence as a cosmic implosion.

teachings.teaching: All beings, including kings, are subject to destruction; one should abandon sin and practice dharma to attain Hari.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the impermanence of worldly achievements and the need for disciplined action (kriya) to transcend the cycle of birth and death. The dissolution of the universe mirrors the dissolution of ego through yoga.

teachings.teaching: The universe undergoes three types of dissolution: naimittika (occasional), prakritika (elemental), and atyantika (absolute). Earth dissolves into water, water into fire, fire into air, air into space, space into ego, ego into intellect, intellect into the unmanifest, and the unmanifest into the Self, which is Vishnu.
teachings.lens_note: This maps to the yogic process of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and laya (dissolution) in Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner systematically withdraws consciousness from gross to subtle levels, ultimately merging with the Supreme Self.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ten primary avatars of Vishnu (Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, and Kalki) and their deeds to protect dharma. It also narrates the story of the chaste wife (pativrata) of a leprous Brahmin named Kaushika, who through her devotion prevented the sunrise, and how Anasuya, another pativrata, intervened to restore the sun. The chapter highlights the power of pativrata dharma and compares Sita's devotion to that of Anasuya.

story.title: The Ten Avatars and the Power of Pativrata Dharma
story.arc: Description of Vishnu's avatars and a narrative illustrating the power of a chaste wife's devotion.
story.timeline_note: The chapter covers events from the Matsya avatar to the Rama avatar, and then a story from a later time involving Kaushika and Anasuya.
story.comic_potential: The story of Kaushika's wife preventing sunrise and Anasuya's intervention has dramatic and humorous potential.

teachings.teaching: Vishnu incarnates to protect dharma and destroy adharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that divine intervention is a response to the imbalance of cosmic energies, and the avatars represent the active principle of preservation through righteous action.

teachings.teaching: The power of a pativrata (chaste wife) is immense; her devotion can influence cosmic events.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the power of focused intention and devotion (bhakti) to alter the subtle energies of the universe. The wife's unwavering faith represents the highest form of mental discipline.

teachings.teaching: Sita's devotion to Rama surpasses even that of Anasuya.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the ideal of complete surrender (prapatti) to the divine, which in Kriya-Yoga is the ultimate state of yoga where the individual self merges with the cosmic self.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana narrates the story of the Ramayana, from the lineage of Brahma to Rama's return to Ayodhya and his reign. It covers key events: Rama's birth, his killing of Tataka, marriage to Sita, exile, Sita's abduction by Ravana, alliance with Sugriva, Hanuman's journey to Lanka, the war, Ravana's defeat, and Rama's coronation.

story.title: Ramayana Summary
story.arc: From the lineage of Brahma to Rama's reign and departure to heaven.
story.timeline_note: The chapter covers the entire Ramayana story in a condensed form, from creation to Rama's ascent to heaven.
story.comic_potential: High; the chapter is a rapid-fire sequence of iconic scenes: birth of Rama, breaking the bow, exile, abduction, Hanuman's leap, burning of Lanka, war, and coronation.

teachings.teaching: Hearing the Ramayana destroys sin (pāpavināśanam).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this indicates that the narrative itself is a purifying practice; recitation or listening with devotion is a form of kriya that cleanses the mind.

teachings.teaching: Rama's exile and acceptance of hardship demonstrate dharma and detachment.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as an example of vairagya (dispassion) and karma-yoga: performing one's duty without attachment to outcomes.

teachings.teaching: Rama's alliance with Sugriva and the monkeys shows that divine purpose uses all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of collective action in Kriya-Yoga: the divine works through many instruments; surrender to the divine will brings all resources together.

teachings.teaching: Hanuman's devotion and service to Rama is the ideal of bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: Hanuman exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga ideal of total surrender and selfless service; his actions are spontaneous and powerful because they are aligned with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Rama's victory over Ravana represents the triumph of dharma over adharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the inner battle: the ego (Ravana) is slain by the higher Self (Rama) through disciplined practice and divine grace.

teachings.teaching: Rama's rule as a just king (ramarajya) is the ideal of righteous governance.
teachings.lens_note: This symbolizes the state of inner harmony when the soul rules the senses and mind; external order reflects internal discipline.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana narrates the Harivamsha, focusing on the divine exploits of Krishna, including his birth to Vasudeva and Devaki, his childhood pastimes such as killing Putana, overturning the cart, breaking the Yamala-Arjuna trees, subduing Kaliya, killing Dhenuka, lifting Govardhana, and later slaying Kamsa, Keshi, and other demons. It also mentions his marriages to Rukmini, Satyabhama, and others, his son Pradyumna, grandson Aniruddha, and the battle with Shiva over Bana, as well as the killing of Naraka, Shishupala, and Dvivida. The chapter concludes with Krishna's role as a teacher to Sandipani and his establishment of Ugrasena as king.

story.title: Harivamsha: The Glories of Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's life and deeds from birth to establishment of dharma
story.timeline_note: Covers Krishna's entire life from birth to post-Mahabharata events, but not in strict chronological order; focuses on key exploits.
story.comic_potential: High; includes dramatic battles, divine interventions, and heroic feats suitable for visual storytelling.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's descent is for the protection of dharma and destruction of adharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle that divine action (kriya) is always aligned with cosmic order; the practitioner must act to uphold dharma through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's lifting of Govardhana symbolizes the power of faith and surrender to the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the yogi's ability to transcend material limitations through devotion and the practice of kriya-yoga, which lifts the mind above worldly concerns.

teachings.teaching: The killing of demons represents the destruction of inner vices.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, each demon slain corresponds to a specific impurity or obstacle in the practitioner's mind, such as lust (Putana), anger (Kamsa), etc., which must be overcome through yogic discipline.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana narrates the lineage of the Kuru dynasty leading to the Mahabharata war, summarizing key events: the birth of Bhishma, Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and Vidura; the birth of the Kauravas and Pandavas; the Pandavas' escape from the lacquer house, killing of Baka, marriage to Draupadi, receiving Indraprastha, Arjuna's marriage to Subhadra and acquisition of divine weapons, the Rajasuya sacrifice, the dice game and exile, the war with its major battles (Bhishma, Drona, Karna, Shalya, Duryodhana, and Ashvatthama's night raid), Yudhishthira's rule and Ashvamedha, Parikshit's coronation, the Yadava destruction, and Yudhishthira's ascent to heaven. It concludes with the future Kalki avatar and the merit of hearing the Bharata.

story.title: The Bharata War and Avatars
story.arc: Summary of the Mahabharata from lineage to war to aftermath, concluding with future avatars.
story.timeline_note: Covers from creation to future Kalki avatar; main events are from the Mahabharata era.
story.comic_potential: High; epic battles, divine interventions, and dramatic deaths.

teachings.teaching: Hearing the Bharata and the avatars leads to heaven.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformative power of sacred narrative (śravaṇa) as a form of yoga, purifying the mind and leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The avatar of Kalki will destroy the wicked and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the cyclical manifestation of divine will to restore dharma, mirroring the inner battle between good and evil in the practitioner's consciousness.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari explains the fivefold diagnosis of diseases (nidana, purvarupa, rupa, upashaya, samprapti) and details the causes of dosha aggravation, including dietary and lifestyle factors, leading to various diseases. The chapter emphasizes that the root cause of all diseases is the vitiation of the three doshas (vata, pitta, kapha) due to improper conduct and sinful actions.

story.title: Sarvaroganidana: The Etiology of All Diseases
story.arc: Dhanvantari expounds the fivefold diagnosis and causes of dosha vitiation, culminating in the statement that sinful actions lead to disease.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a discourse from Dhanvantari to Sushruta, following the tradition of Atreya.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical medical exposition with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: The fivefold diagnosis of diseases: nidana (cause), purvarupa (prodromal symptoms), rupa (manifest symptoms), upashaya (therapeutic test), and samprapti (pathogenesis).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the systematic analysis of kleshas (afflictions) and their stages, where nidana is the root ignorance, purvarupa is the subtle tendency, rupa is the manifest suffering, upashaya is the practice that alleviates, and samprapti is the full manifestation of karma.

teachings.teaching: All diseases arise from vitiated doshas (vata, pitta, kapha), which are aggravated by improper diet, lifestyle, and sinful actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the karmic law: adharma (unrighteousness) disturbs the pranic balance, leading to physical and mental disorders. Kriya-Yoga purifies the doshas through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The causes of vata aggravation include bitter, pungent, astringent, sour, dry foods; excessive exertion, grief, worry, and sexual activity; and seasonal factors like summer.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, vata corresponds to the air element and prana vayu. Overstimulation of the senses and erratic lifestyle disturb prana, leading to anxiety and disease. Steady asana and pranayama stabilize vata.

teachings.teaching: Pitta is aggravated by pungent, sour, salty, hot foods; anger; and seasons like autumn and midday.
teachings.lens_note: Pitta relates to the fire element and tejas. Uncontrolled anger and ambition burn the system. Kriya-Yoga teaches cooling practices like sheetali pranayama and meditation to pacify pitta.

teachings.teaching: Kapha is aggravated by sweet, sour, salty, oily, heavy foods; daytime sleep; and lack of exercise.
teachings.lens_note: Kapha corresponds to water and earth elements. Laziness and overindulgence create stagnation. Kriya-Yoga emphasizes dynamic practices like kapalabhati and physical activity to invigorate kapha.

teachings.teaching: Sannipata (tridoshic imbalance) arises from mixed, incompatible, and spoiled foods, as well as sinful actions.
teachings.lens_note: This represents severe karmic imbalance. In Kriya-Yoga, such conditions require intense purification through panchakarma and advanced yogic practices to restore harmony.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 147) provides a detailed classification and description of fever (jvara) from an Ayurvedic perspective. It discusses the origins, types, symptoms, and prognosis of various fevers, including those caused by imbalances of vata, pitta, kapha, and sannipata (all three doshas). It also covers external causes such as injury, curses, and sorcery. The chapter emphasizes the importance of understanding the nature of fever for proper treatment.

story.title: Classification of Fevers
story.arc: Ayurvedic exposition
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a didactic text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical treatise.

teachings.teaching: Fever is classified into eight types based on dosha, external causes, and other factors.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this classification reflects the interplay of prana (vata), tejas (pitta), and ojas (kapha) in disease. Understanding the doshic imbalance is key to restoring harmony through yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: The prognosis of fever depends on the strength of the doshas and the presence of complications.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle that the severity of disease is proportional to the disturbance of the subtle energies. Yogic purification can mitigate even severe imbalances.

teachings.teaching: External causes of fever include injury, curses, and sorcery.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as karmic or pranic disturbances that affect the physical body. Kriya-Yoga practices can cleanse such influences.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 147) details the classification, etiology, and symptomatology of various types of fevers (jvara) based on the imbalance of doshas (vata, pitta, kapha) and their location in the body's tissues (rasa, rakta, mamsa, medas, asthi, majja, shukra). It describes periodic fevers (quotidian, tertian, quartan) and their characteristics, as well as specific types like haridraka. The text also outlines signs of recovery and mentions the next chapter on raktapitta.

story.title: Classification of Fevers
story.arc: Medical treatise on fevers
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: None

teachings.teaching: Fevers arise from dosha imbalances and manifest differently based on the tissue involved.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the purification of subtle channels (nadis) and the balancing of pranic energies. The classification of fevers by tissue depth mirrors the stages of spiritual purification where deeper impurities are cleansed.

teachings.teaching: Periodic fevers (quotidian, tertian, quartan) are linked to dosha cycles and seasonal influences.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees these cycles as reflections of natural rhythms; aligning with them through disciplined practice (kriya) harmonizes the body-mind.

teachings.teaching: Deep-seated fevers (asthi, majja, shukra) are difficult to treat and indicate advanced disease.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these correspond to deep karmic impressions; their resolution requires sustained sadhana and grace.

teachings.teaching: Signs of recovery include lightness of body, clarity of senses, appetite, and absence of pain.
teachings.lens_note: These mirror the signs of spiritual progress: lightness, clarity, and inner peace.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology (nidāna) of raktapitta (bleeding disorders), describing its causes, symptoms, and treatment principles. It explains how aggravated pitta and blood combine, leading to various symptoms like heaviness in the head, anorexia, desire for cold, smoke-like vision, sour taste, vomiting, cough, dyspnea, dizziness, fatigue, and discoloration. The chapter emphasizes the importance of purificatory therapies (virecana and vamana) based on the direction of bleeding and associated doshas.

story.title: Etiology of Raktapitta
story.arc: Medical discourse on bleeding disorders
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a medical treatise.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Raktapitta arises from aggravated pitta and blood due to intake of excessively hot, pungent, sour, salty, and alkaline foods, as well as certain grains like kodrava.
Symptoms include heaviness in the head, anorexia, desire for cold, smoke-like vision, sour taste, vomiting, cough, dyspnea, dizziness, fatigue, and discoloration of eyes and skin.
Bleeding can occur upward (from nose, eyes, ears, mouth) or downward (from penis, anus, vagina), and also through hair follicles.
Upward bleeding is curable and treated with purgation (virecana), while downward bleeding is managed with emesis (vamana).
When kapha is associated, treatments should include astringent and sweet substances; when vata is involved, the condition becomes incurable.
In mixed dosha conditions, emesis is beneficial; complications arise from improper treatment.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari describes the five types of cough (kāsa) based on the doshas: vāta, pitta, kapha, kṣata (trauma), and kṣaya (consumption). Each type's symptoms, pathogenesis, and prognosis are detailed. The chapter emphasizes the importance of prompt treatment to prevent progression to more serious conditions like consumption.

story.title: Kāsa Nidāna: The Etiology of Cough
story.arc: Medical discourse on the diagnosis and prognosis of cough types.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical content.

teachings.teaching: Cough arises from vitiated vāta, pitta, kapha, trauma, or consumption; each has distinct symptoms and requires specific treatment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this reflects the principle that imbalances in the body's energies (doshas) manifest as disease, and diagnosis must be precise to apply corrective action (kriya).

teachings.teaching: Neglected cough can lead to serious conditions like consumption (rājayakṣma), so prompt treatment is essential.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes timely intervention (kriya) to prevent disease progression, aligning with the yogic concept of nirodha (cessation) of harmful patterns.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari describes the etiology, symptoms, and prognosis of respiratory diseases (śvāsa), including five types: kṣudra, tamaka, chinna, mahā, and ūrdhva. The chapter details causes such as aggravated cough, dosha imbalance, and external factors, and outlines fatal signs.

story.title: Śvāsa Nidāna (Etiology of Respiratory Disease)
story.arc: Dhanvantari expounds on the causes and symptoms of breathlessness.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Respiratory disease arises from aggravated cough or dosha imbalance, affecting the prāṇa vāyu and causing various symptoms.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this correlates with prāṇāyāma disturbances; proper breath control can prevent such imbalances.

teachings.teaching: Five types of śvāsa are described: kṣudra, tamaka, chinna, mahā, and ūrdhva, with specific symptoms and prognoses.
teachings.lens_note: These classifications help in diagnosing the stage of prāṇic obstruction, guiding specific yogic breathing techniques.

teachings.teaching: Fatal signs include loss of consciousness, dry mouth, upward gaze, and inability to exhale.
teachings.lens_note: Such signs indicate severe prāṇic blockage; urgent intervention through specific kriyās may be needed.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the diagnosis and classification of hiccups (hikrā), describing various types such as kṣudrā, yamalā, mahatī, and gambhīrā, their causes, symptoms, and prognostic implications. It also briefly introduces the topic of yakṣmaroga (consumption/tuberculosis) as a segue to the next chapter.

story.title: Hiccup Etiology and Classification
story.arc: Medical discourse on hiccups (hikrā) and introduction to yakṣmaroga.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a didactic exposition; no narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Hiccups arise from vitiated vāta (wind) due to improper diet and exertion, and are classified into types based on symptoms and severity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this aligns with the principle that bodily disorders (ādhi) stem from imbalances in the prāṇic flows, and diagnosis requires observing the specific manifestations of vāta.

teachings.teaching: Certain types of hiccups (ādye dve) and those with all symptoms (sarvaliṅgāṃ) and rapid onset (veginīm) should be considered incurable and indicative of impending death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning that when prāṇic disturbances become severe and systemic, they signal the dissolution of the vital force, emphasizing the need for early intervention through yogic practices to regulate prāṇa.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology, symptoms, and prognosis of Rajayakshma (tuberculosis), also known as Kshaya or consumption. It describes the causes (excess exertion, suppression of natural urges, depletion of semen/ojas, and improper diet), the pathogenesis involving vitiated vata and pitta, and a detailed list of prodromal and clinical symptoms including fever, cough, hemoptysis, and wasting. It also covers complications, doshic differentiation, and prognostic indicators.

story.title: Etiology and Symptoms of Rajayakshma
story.arc: Medical discourse on Rajayakshma (tuberculosis) as narrated by Dhanvantari.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Rajayakshma arises from four causes: excess exertion, suppression of natural urges, depletion of semen/ojas, and improper diet.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, suppression of natural urges and depletion of ojas correspond to pranic blockages and loss of vital energy, which can be addressed through yogic practices that regulate prana and build ojas.

teachings.teaching: The disease manifests with symptoms like fever, cough, hemoptysis, wasting, and various psychic disturbances.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as signs of severe pranic imbalance and toxicity, requiring purification (kriya) and restorative practices.

teachings.teaching: Prognosis depends on the doshic involvement and the stage of tissue depletion; some cases are incurable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize early intervention through lifestyle and yogic practices to prevent irreversible tissue damage.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the diagnosis and classification of anorexia (arocaka) based on doshic imbalances. It describes symptoms for vata, pitta, kapha, and sannipata types, as well as complications from worms and other factors. The chapter ends with a transition to the next topic on heart disease.

story.title: Diagnosis of Anorexia
story.arc: Medical teachings
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a medical discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Anorexia arises from doshas located in the tongue and heart, and from sannipata (combined doshas) and mental distress.
The mouth tastes astringent, bitter, or sweet according to vata, pitta, kapha imbalances; in sorrow and anger, everything becomes tasteless.
Vomiting caused by doshas; udana vayu expels doshas, causing distress, loss of taste, and other symptoms.
Pain in navel and back, sides, and vomiting of frothy, salty, or bitter material; symptoms vary by dosha.
Pitta-type anorexia: vomiting of alkaline, smoky, greenish-yellow, sour, bitter, with thirst, fainting, burning; kapha-type: thick, yellow, sweet, salty, with hair-raising, swelling, lethargy.
A patient with all symptoms combined should be rejected; aversion to all sights and sounds indicates severe disease from vitiated vata, worms, or spoiled food.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 154) discusses the diagnosis (nidāna) of heart diseases (hṛdroga) and thirst (tṛṣṇā), classifying them according to the three doshas (vāta, pitta, kapha) and their combinations. It describes symptoms, causes, and subtypes, including those caused by worms (kṛmi) and complications (upasarga). The chapter is attributed to Dhanvantari.

story.title: Diagnosis of Heart Disease and Thirst
story.arc: Medical exposition
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Heart diseases are classified into five types based on doshas and worms.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the need to diagnose the root cause (karma or vāsanā) before applying yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Thirst (tṛṣṇā) arises from imbalances of vāta, pitta, kapha, or complications.
teachings.lens_note: Tṛṣṇā symbolizes desire; its treatment parallels the yogic discipline of reducing cravings through pratyāhāra.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari describes the causes and symptoms of intoxication (madātyaya) from alcohol, classified by doṣa (vāta, pitta, kapha) and their combinations, including stages of intoxication, complications like mūrchā and saṃnyāsa, and the importance of moderation.

story.title: Madātyaya Nidāna
story.arc: Dhanvantari expounds on the etiology and symptomatology of alcohol intoxication.
story.timeline_note: Single discourse; no narrative progression.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Alcohol has ten qualities (tīkṣṇa, amla, rūkṣa, etc.) that disturb the mind and body, leading to intoxication.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to how sensory indulgence (rajas) agitates the mind, disrupting inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: Intoxication progresses through three stages: first excitement, then loss of control, finally unconsciousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a metaphor for the three guṇas: initial rajas, then tamas overwhelming, and finally loss of awareness.

teachings.teaching: Those who are strong, well-nourished, and eat heavily do not get excessively intoxicated.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that a balanced lifestyle (sattvic diet, discipline) builds resilience against vices.

teachings.teaching: One who abstains from alcohol with self-control and wisdom never suffers from mental or physical disorders.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: self-discipline (niyama) leads to freedom from afflictions.

teachings.teaching: Moderate drinking according to one's constitution, season, and strength is like drinking nectar.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the principle of moderation in all things, avoiding extremes.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari to Suśruta, provides a detailed medical treatise on hemorrhoids (arśas). It covers the etiology, classification, symptoms, and complications of the disease, emphasizing the role of vāta, pitta, and kapha doṣas. The text describes the anatomical location, types (dry/moist, congenital/acquired), and associated conditions like grahaṇī (malabsorption). It also outlines the systemic effects and poor prognosis for certain types.

story.title: Treatise on Hemorrhoids
story.arc: Medical exposition on the etiology, classification, and complications of arśas (hemorrhoids) as taught by Dhanvantari to Suśruta.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; this is a didactic medical text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Hemorrhoids arise from vitiated doṣas (vāta, pitta, kapha) and are classified as dry (śuṣka) or moist (ārdra), congenital (sahaja) or acquired (jāntarotthena).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching emphasizes the importance of balancing the doṣas through disciplined action (kriya) and awareness, as imbalance leads to disease. The classification into congenital and acquired reflects the interplay of karma and present habits.

teachings.teaching: The root cause of hemorrhoids includes dietary indiscretions, suppression of natural urges, and trauma, leading to vāta aggravation and formation of growths in the anal region.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would interpret this as a call to mindful living (sādhana) and proper regulation of bodily functions, aligning with the yogic principle of not suppressing natural urges (dhāraṇa).

teachings.teaching: Symptoms of hemorrhoids include pain, bleeding, constipation, and systemic effects like weakness, pallor, and loss of appetite.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the holistic view of health in Ayurveda, where local symptoms reflect systemic imbalance. In Kriya-Yoga, it highlights the interconnectedness of body and mind, and the need for integrated practice.

teachings.teaching: Vāta-type hemorrhoids are dry, rough, and painful; pitta-type are red, bleeding, and burning; kapha-type are pale, smooth, and itchy.
teachings.lens_note: This classification aids in personalized treatment. In Kriya-Yoga, it mirrors the need to tailor spiritual practices to one's constitution (prakṛti) for effective transformation.

teachings.teaching: Complications of hemorrhoids include grahaṇī (malabsorption), gulma (abdominal tumor), and other serious conditions, with some types being incurable.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against neglect of early symptoms. In Kriya-Yoga, it emphasizes the importance of timely action (kriya) to prevent karmic and physical entanglements from worsening.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the classification and treatment of hemorrhoids (arśas). It describes the causes, symptoms, and types based on dosha imbalance, and emphasizes the need for prompt treatment to prevent complications.

story.title: Nidāna of Arśas (Hemorrhoids)
story.arc: Medical discourse on the diagnosis and treatment of hemorrhoids
story.timeline_note: Part of the Ācārakāṇḍa in the Garuda Purāṇa
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Hemorrhoids are classified based on dosha (vata, pitta, kapha) and curability (sādhya, kṛcchrasādhya, asādhya). Prompt treatment is essential to prevent complications like intestinal obstruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of addressing imbalances at their root through disciplined action (kriya) to prevent disease progression.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology (nidāna) of diarrhea (atīsāra) and chronic digestive disorders (grahaṇī). It describes causes such as excessive water intake, improper diet, and emotional factors like fear and grief. The text details symptoms based on the three doshas (vāta, pitta, kapha) and distinguishes between āma (undigested) and nirāma (digested) types. It also lists eight major diseases (mahārogas) including urinary disorders, skin diseases, and hemorrhoids.

story.title: Etiology of Diarrhea and Digestive Disorders
story.arc: Medical discourse between Dhanvantari and Suśruta
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's medical section
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Diarrhea arises from vitiated doshas, improper diet, and emotional disturbances like fear and grief.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, emotional disturbances are seen as pranic imbalances; fear and grief disturb apana vayu, leading to digestive issues.

teachings.teaching: The eight major diseases (mahārogas) include vāta disorders, urinary stones, leprosy, diabetes, abdominal tumors, fistula, hemorrhoids, and grahaṇī.
teachings.lens_note: These diseases are considered manifestations of deep-seated karmic and pranic blockages; Kriya-Yoga practices aim to purify the nadis and balance the doshas.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 158) discusses the causes, symptoms, and types of urinary disorders (mūtrāghāta) and related conditions such as aśmarī (urinary stones), vātabasti, vātakuṇḍalikā, mūtragranthi, mūtraśukra, and others. It describes the role of vāta, pitta, and kapha doshas in these diseases, and provides detailed clinical features.

story.title: Urinary Disorders and Their Causes
story.arc: Medical exposition on urinary diseases
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic text.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: The bladder (basti) is filled from the sides by fine channels carrying urine continuously.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this can be seen as the subtle body's nadis carrying prana; blockage leads to disease.

teachings.teaching: Urinary stones (aśmarī) are caused by vāta drying up urine mixed with pitta and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes balancing the three doshas through pranayama and meditation to prevent such concretions.

teachings.teaching: Suppression of urine leads to vātabasti and other disorders.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, holding natural urges is discouraged as it disturbs prana flow; timely elimination is part of yogic discipline.

teachings.teaching: Semen (śukra) can be displaced by vāta and cause mūtraśukra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that brahmacharya and control of sexual energy are essential for health; misuse leads to disorders.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 159) is a medical treatise on urinary disorders (prameha). It enumerates twenty types of prameha classified by dosha (kapha, pitta, vata), describes their symptoms, complications (including ten types of skin lesions), and etiological factors such as diet and lifestyle. The text also discusses prognosis and the progression to diabetes (madhumeha).

story.title: Treatise on Urinary Disorders
story.arc: Medical exposition on prameha, its classification, symptoms, complications, and treatment principles.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical content.

teachings.teaching: Prameha are twenty in number: ten from kapha, six from pitta, and four from vata. They are caused by vitiated medas (fat), mutra (urine), and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this classification emphasizes the need to balance the doshas through disciplined action (kriya) and purification.

teachings.teaching: If neglected, all prameha eventually become madhumeha (diabetes), which is difficult to cure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against neglecting early imbalances; consistent yogic practice prevents degeneration.

teachings.teaching: The causes include sweet, sour, salty, heavy, unctuous foods, new grains, alcohol, meat, sugarcane products, milk, sedentary lifestyle, and excessive sleep.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga advocates moderation and mindful eating (ahara) as part of kriya; these are obstacles to inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Complications include ten types of skin lesions (pidika) such as sharavika, kacchapika, jvalini, etc.
teachings.lens_note: These physical manifestations reflect deeper doshic imbalances; Kriya-Yoga uses asanas and pranayama to address root causes.

teachings.teaching: Premonitory symptoms include sweetness in mouth, burning in hands and feet, ants attracted to urine, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as early signs of metabolic imbalance; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes early detection through self-awareness (svadhyaya).

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari, describes the etiology, symptoms, and classification of Vidradhi (abscess) and Gulma (abdominal tumor). It details causes such as improper diet, lifestyle, and dosha imbalances, and outlines the characteristics of each type based on vata, pitta, kapha, and sannipata. Special mention is made of Rakta Gulma in women.

story.title: Dhanvantari's Discourse on Vidradhi and Gulma
story.arc: Medical exposition
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Vidradhi (abscess) arises from vitiated doshas and blood, with symptoms like swelling, pain, and fever.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the importance of balancing the doshas through disciplined action (kriya) to prevent disease.

teachings.teaching: Gulma (abdominal tumor) is caused by vata, pitta, kapha, or combined doshas, with specific symptoms for each type.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that such imbalances are corrected by aligning one's actions with dharma and practicing pranayama.

teachings.teaching: Rakta Gulma in women is associated with menstrual irregularities and is considered incurable.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the need for preventive care and purity in lifestyle, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the medical conditions of vidradhi (abscess) and gulma (abdominal tumor), including their causes, symptoms, and progression. It explains how vitiated vata and pitta lead to the formation of these diseases in the body, particularly in the uterus and abdomen.

story.title: Vidradhi and Gulma Nidana
story.arc: Medical discourse on abdominal diseases
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a medical treatise.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Vata and pitta combine to form gulma (abdominal tumor) and vidradhi (abscess) in the body.
Symptoms of gulma include abdominal distension, pain, and obstruction of vata.
Vidradhi is characterized by rapid suppuration and severe pain due to vitiated blood.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari describes the causes, symptoms, and classification of abdominal diseases (udara roga), including eight types: vataja, pittaja, kaphaja, sannipataja, plihodara, yakridodara, baddhagudodara, and chidrodara. He explains the role of digestive fire (agni) and dosha imbalance, and notes that all diseases arise from weak digestion.

story.title: Dhanvantari's Discourse on Abdominal Diseases
story.arc: Medical teachings on udara roga
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's medical section
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: All diseases arise from weak digestive fire (mandagni), especially abdominal disorders.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, mandagni corresponds to lack of inner fire (tapas) and pranic stagnation; strengthening agni through disciplined practice is key.

teachings.teaching: Abdominal diseases are caused by vitiated vata, pitta, kapha, or their combinations, and by accumulation of malas (waste products).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret dosha imbalance as disturbance in the five pranas; cleansing practices (kriyas) restore balance.

teachings.teaching: Eight types of udara roga: vataja, pittaja, kaphaja, sannipataja, plihodara, yakridodara, baddhagudodara, chidrodara.
teachings.lens_note: Classification reflects holistic diagnosis; in Kriya-Yoga, each type corresponds to specific energetic blockages.

teachings.teaching: Symptoms include loss of appetite, emaciation, abdominal distension, pain, and discoloration.
teachings.lens_note: These signs indicate prana vayu obstruction; practices like nauli and agnisara kriya are recommended.

teachings.teaching: Prognosis: all udara rogas are difficult to cure, especially congenital ones.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes prevention through daily sadhana and proper diet (mitahara).

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari explains the etiology and symptoms of pāṇḍu (jaundice) and śotha (edema), including their types, causes, and complications, as part of Ayurvedic medical knowledge.

story.title: Etiology of Pāṇḍu and Śotha
story.arc: Medical discourse
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of Garuda Purana medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Pāṇḍu (jaundice) arises from vitiated pitta (bile) aggravated by causative factors, leading to discoloration and systemic symptoms.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects how internal imbalances (doṣas) manifest as disease, emphasizing the need for purification and balance.

teachings.teaching: Śotha (edema) is classified into five types based on doṣa involvement and causes, including trauma and poison.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a teaching on how external factors (karma, diet) disturb the subtle body, leading to physical swelling.

teachings.teaching: Improper diet and lifestyle (e.g., heavy, cold foods, suppression of urges) aggravate doṣas and lead to disease.
teachings.lens_note: Aligns with Kriya-Yoga emphasis on disciplined living (niyama) to maintain pranic balance.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology, classification, symptoms, and prognosis of visarpa (erysipelas or spreading skin infection). It describes various types based on dosha involvement (vata, pitta, kapha, sannipata), as well as traumatic and systemic forms. The text also outlines curable and incurable conditions.

story.title: The Etiology and Classification of Visarpa
story.arc: Medical discourse on skin diseases
story.timeline_note: Not specified; part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical exposition.

teachings.teaching: Visarpa arises from trauma, vitiated doshas, and improper diet and activity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the importance of balanced lifestyle (mitahara and vihara) to prevent disease, aligning with the yogic principle of moderation.

teachings.teaching: Different doshic types of visarpa present with distinct symptoms and require specific treatments.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes individualized practice (svadhyaya) and self-awareness to recognize one's constitution and imbalances, mirroring the diagnostic approach here.

teachings.teaching: Granthivisarpa and kardamakhyavisarpa are severe forms with poor prognosis, especially when marma (vital points) are affected.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the need for early intervention and the concept of 'kriya' (action) in yoga to prevent disease from reaching incurable stages.

teachings.teaching: Visarpa caused by external injury (kshata) involves vitiated pitta and vata, presenting with specific skin lesions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views external injuries as opportunities for purification (shatkarma) and healing through pranayama and asana.

teachings.teaching: Curable visarpa includes those with single dosha or dual dosha without complications; incurable types involve all doshas, marma involvement, or tissue decay.
teachings.lens_note: This classification parallels the yogic concept of 'adhyatmika' (spiritual) diseases being curable through self-effort, while advanced stages require grace.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 164) provides a detailed classification and description of skin diseases (kuṣṭha), including their causes, symptoms, and prognosis. It categorizes seven major types (mahākuṣṭha) and several minor types, linking them to imbalances of the three doṣas (vāta, pitta, kapha). The text also discusses the progression of disease through bodily tissues (dhātus) and the contagious nature of these conditions.

story.title: Classification of Skin Diseases (Kuṣṭha Nidāna)
story.arc: Medical discourse on dermatology
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; highly technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Skin diseases arise from imbalances of vāta, pitta, and kapha, and are classified into seven major types (mahākuṣṭha) and several minor types.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this reflects the principle that physical ailments are manifestations of deeper energetic imbalances. The detailed classification serves as a diagnostic tool to identify the root doṣa imbalance, which can be corrected through disciplined practice (kriya) and lifestyle adjustments.

teachings.teaching: The progression of disease through tissues (tvak, rakta, māṃsa, medas, asthi, majjā, śukra) indicates severity; deeper involvement is more difficult to treat.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that purification practices (shatkarmas) and pranayama can help cleanse deeper tissues, preventing disease progression. This verse underscores the importance of early intervention.

teachings.teaching: Skin diseases can be contagious through contact, sharing food, bedding, etc.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the need for purity (śauca) in daily life, a key tenet in Kriya-Yoga. Maintaining cleanliness and mindful interactions prevents the spread of impurities, both physical and subtle.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various types of internal and external parasites (krimi), their origins, characteristics, and the diseases they cause. It categorizes them based on location (external/internal), source (mucus, blood, feces), and symptoms.

story.title: Kriminidāna: Diagnosis of Parasites
story.arc: Medical discourse on parasitic diseases
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Parasites are of two types: external and internal. External ones arise from dirt, mucus, phlegm, blood, and feces, and are of four kinds. They are named twenty-fold, are of the size of a sesame seed, and reside in hair and clothes.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of external and internal purity. External parasites symbolize impurities in action and environment; internal parasites represent mental and emotional toxins that must be cleansed through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Internal parasites born from mucus in the stomach cause various diseases like heartburn, vomiting, fever, and anorexia.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the result of improper digestion of experiences (mental and physical). Kriya-Yoga practices like pranayama and meditation help 'digest' karmic impressions, preventing such imbalances.

teachings.teaching: Blood-borne parasites are minute, round, copper-colored, and cause hair loss and skin diseases like leprosy.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the need to purify the blood (life force) through ethical living and yogic practices. In Kriya-Yoga, blood purity is linked to sattvic diet and pranayama.

teachings.teaching: Intestinal parasites from feces cause abdominal pain, constipation, and pallor.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a metaphor for accumulated karmic waste. Regular cleansing through fasting and meditation is recommended to maintain health.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, narrated by Dhanvantari, details the etiology, symptoms, and classification of vata (wind) disorders. It describes how vitiated vayu causes various diseases affecting different body parts, including the head, joints, limbs, and sensory organs, and outlines specific conditions such as pakshaghata (hemiplegia), hanustambha (jaw stiffness), and urustambha (thigh stiffness).

story.title: Etiology and Classification of Vata Disorders
story.arc: Medical exposition on vata diseases
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Vata disorders arise from vitiated vayu and affect various body parts; proper diagnosis requires understanding of dosha imbalances.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this aligns with the principle that prana (vital energy) must be balanced through disciplined action (kriya) to prevent disease. The detailed symptoms reflect how disturbed prana manifests in the body.

teachings.teaching: Specific vata disorders include pakshaghata (hemiplegia), hanustambha (jaw stiffness), and urustambha (thigh stiffness), each with distinct causes and symptoms.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as blockages in the nadis (energy channels) due to improper kriya practices or lifestyle. The emphasis on treatment difficulty for certain conditions underscores the need for consistent yogic discipline.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari begins teaching Suśruta about the diagnosis and treatment of Vātarakta (gout), describing its causes, symptoms, and remedies.

story.title: Dhanvantari's Discourse on Vātarakta
story.arc: Dhanvantari explains the etiology and management of Vātarakta to Suśruta.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's medical teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic medical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Vātarakta is caused by vitiated vāta (wind) and rakta (blood), leading to painful swelling in joints.
teachings.lens_note: Ayurvedic medical teaching.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the etiology, pathogenesis, classification, and clinical features of Vatarakta (gout), including its subtypes based on dosha involvement and dhatu affliction. It also details the effects of vitiated Vata on the five subtypes of Prana and the concept of Avarana (occlusion).

story.title: Etiology and Classification of Vatarakta
story.arc: Medical exposition on Vatarakta (gout) in the Garuda Purana
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; didactic text.
story.comic_potential: Low; technical medical description.

teachings.teaching: Vatarakta arises from vitiated Vata and Rakta due to causes like incompatible diet, anger, day sleep, etc.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this mirrors how imbalance in pranic energies (Vata) and blood (Rakta) disrupts the subtle body's harmony, requiring purification and balancing practices.

teachings.teaching: The disease progresses from superficial to deep tissues, eventually affecting all dhatus.
teachings.lens_note: This parallels the Kriya-Yoga view that unaddressed subtle imbalances gradually manifest as gross physical disorders, emphasizing early intervention.

teachings.teaching: Vatarakta is classified by dosha predominance: Vata, Pitta, Kapha, or combined; single-dosha is curable, double-dosha manageable, triple-dosha incurable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees dosha classification as a diagnostic tool to tailor Kriya practices (e.g., pranayama for Vata, cooling techniques for Pitta).

teachings.teaching: The five subtypes of Vata (Prana, Udana, Vyana, Samana, Apana) when vitiated cause specific disorders.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these correspond to different pranic functions; their imbalance can be corrected through specific asanas and breath control.

teachings.teaching: Avarana (occlusion) of Vata by other doshas or dhatus leads to complex symptoms; diagnosis requires careful observation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Avarana reflects blockages in energy channels (nadis), which Kriya-Yoga aims to clear through meditation and pranayama.

chapter_summary: This chapter continues the discourse on medical diagnosis and treatment, focusing on the effects of obstructed bodily winds (vata) and the use of triphala and other herbal formulations to cure various diseases. It concludes with the colophon indicating it is from the Garuda Purana, chapter 168, and the speaker is Dhanvantari.

story.title: Medical Discourse on Vata Disorders and Herbal Remedies
story.arc: Continuation of medical teachings from Dhanvantari to Sushruta, focusing on diagnosis and treatment of diseases caused by obstructed winds.
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger medical dialogue within the Garuda Purana.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily instructional text with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Obstruction of the two vital winds leads to loss of strength, lifespan, and health. When winds are covered and unknown, or known but displaced, they are difficult to cure even with effort, unless complications arise.
If obstructed winds are neglected, complications such as abscess, splenic disease, heart disease, abdominal tumors, and gastritis develop.
The etiology (nidana) spoken by Atreya is explained for understanding all diseases and promoting longevity.

After diagnosing diseases, one should practice treatment. Triphala combined with honey, ghee, and jaggery destroys all diseases. Triphala with the three pungents (vyosha) also suppresses all diseases. Alternatively, triphala with shatavari, guduchi, agni (chitraka), and vidanga is effective.

A formulation of shatavari, guduchi, chitraka, shunthi, mushali, bala, punarnava, brihati, nirgundi, neem leaves, bhringaraja, amalaka, and vasaka, prepared by triturating with their own juice seven times, can be used as powder, pills, tablets, ghee, oil, or decoction to cure wasting diseases.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 168) is a discourse by Dhanvantari on Ayurvedic principles, focusing on the causes, symptoms, and treatments of diseases arising from imbalances of the three doshas: vata, pitta, and kapha. It describes the characteristics of each dosha, their locations in the body, the effects of diet and lifestyle, and methods for diagnosis and treatment, including herbal remedies and therapies.

story.title: Dhanvantari's Discourse on Ayurveda
story.arc: Exposition of medical knowledge
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; timeless teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic medical text.

teachings.teaching: Health is maintained by balance of doshas (vata, pitta, kapha), dhatus, and malas; imbalance causes disease.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this balance corresponds to the harmonious flow of prana in the nadis, achieved through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Vata is cold, light, subtle, and mobile; its imbalance causes various disorders.
teachings.lens_note: Vata imbalance reflects erratic prana; Kriya-Yoga stabilizes vata through breath control and asanas.

teachings.teaching: Pitta is hot, sharp, and sour; its imbalance leads to inflammatory conditions.
teachings.lens_note: Pitta imbalance indicates excess tapas; cooling pranayama and meditation restore balance.

teachings.teaching: Kapha is sweet, heavy, and sticky; its imbalance causes congestion and lethargy.
teachings.lens_note: Kapha imbalance reflects stagnation; Kriya-Yoga uses dynamic practices to stimulate energy flow.

teachings.teaching: The five elements (panchamula) are used in herbal remedies to balance doshas.
teachings.lens_note: Herbs correspond to subtle energies; in Kriya-Yoga, internal cleansing through diet complements yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Improper diet and lifestyle cause ama (toxins), leading to disease.
teachings.lens_note: Ama is undigested karma; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes proper digestion of experiences through awareness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various bodily omens and signs that indicate impending death, such as displacement of body parts, discoloration, and other abnormalities. It is part of the medical section (vaidyakaśāstra) and is spoken by Dhanvantari.

story.title: Omens of Death
story.arc: Medical teachings on fatal signs
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's medical discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; instructional text.

teachings.teaching: If a person's ankle, knee, forehead, chin, or cheek become displaced from their normal position, they will soon die.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, bodily signs reflect pranic disturbances; displacement indicates severe energy blockages.

teachings.teaching: If the left eye twitches, the tongue is dark, the nose is deformed, or the lips become black and displaced, death is imminent.
teachings.lens_note: These signs show vata and kapha imbalance leading to prana withdrawal.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari, provides a detailed classification of various foods and substances according to their effects on the three doshas (vata, pitta, kapha). It covers grains, pulses, vegetables, fruits, spices, salts, waters, dairy products, oils, and sweeteners, describing their properties and therapeutic uses.

story.title: Classification of Foods and Their Effects on Doshas
story.arc: Didactic exposition on dietetics and Ayurveda
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; a standalone teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely informational.

teachings.teaching: Raktaśāli (red rice) is tridoṣaghna (balances all doshas) and quenches thirst and obesity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, balancing doshas is essential for preparing the body for higher practices.

teachings.teaching: Mahāśāli (great rice) is highly vṛṣya (aphrodisiac), and kalama (a type of rice) alleviates kapha and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Aphrodisiacs support ojas, vital for spiritual energy.

teachings.teaching: Śyāmāka (barnyard millet) is drying, rough, vātala (increases vata), and alleviates kapha and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Understanding food qualities helps in dietary discipline for yoga.

teachings.teaching: Yava (barley) is cooling, alleviates kapha and pitta, and is vṛṣya.
teachings.lens_note: Barley is sattvic and recommended for meditation.

teachings.teaching: Godhūma (wheat) is vṛṣya, cooling, heavy, sweet, and destroys vata.
teachings.lens_note: Wheat nourishes the body, supporting physical stability for asanas.

teachings.teaching: Mudga (green gram) is astringent, sweet, light, and destroys kapha and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Light foods are preferred for pranayama and meditation.

teachings.teaching: Māṣa (black gram) is heavy, vṛṣya, and alleviates pitta and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Heavy foods may be used in moderation for grounding.

teachings.teaching: Rājamāṣa (cowpea) is not vṛṣya, alleviates kapha and pitta, and relieves vata disorders.
teachings.lens_note: Balancing vata is crucial for nervous system health in yoga.

teachings.teaching: Kulattha (horse gram) is useful for asthma, hiccup, kapha, gulma, and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Herbal remedies support cleansing practices.

teachings.teaching: Makuṣṭhaka (moth bean) is cooling, absorbent, and alleviates raktapitta and fever.
teachings.lens_note: Cooling foods help in pitta pacification for mental calm.

teachings.teaching: Caṇaka (chickpea) is vātala, and destroys masculinity, blood, kapha, and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Excess vata can disturb meditation; moderation advised.

teachings.teaching: Masūra (lentil) is sweet, cooling, absorbent, and alleviates kapha and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Lentils are sattvic and easy to digest.

teachings.teaching: Kalāya (pea) is similar to masūra but very vātala.
teachings.lens_note: Vata-increasing foods should be balanced with oils.

teachings.teaching: Āḍhakī (pigeon pea) alleviates kapha and pitta, and is śukralā (semen-promoting).
teachings.lens_note: Semen preservation is important for ojas.

teachings.teaching: Atasī (flax) is pittala (increases pitta), and siddhārtha (mustard) conquers kapha and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Mustard is heating; used in moderation for kapha conditions.

teachings.teaching: Tila (sesame) is salty, sweet, unctuous, heating, and increases pitta and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Sesame oil is used in abhyanga for vata balance.

teachings.teaching: Various leafy vegetables like citraka, iṅgudī, nālīkā, pippalī, madhuśigru, cavya, caraṇa, nirguṇḍī, tarkārī, kāśamarda, and bilva are kapha-pitta destroying, anti-helminthic, light, and digestive.
teachings.lens_note: Bitter greens cleanse the liver, aiding detoxification.

teachings.teaching: Varṣābhū (a plant) and mārkara (?) destroy vata and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Seasonal foods align with nature's rhythms.

teachings.teaching: Eraṇḍa (castor) is bitter, and kākamācī (?) removes tridoṣa.
teachings.lens_note: Castor oil is used for purification.

teachings.teaching: Cāṅgerī (sorrel) destroys kapha and vata; sarṣapa (mustard) aggravates all doshas.
teachings.lens_note: Mustard in excess is rajasic.

teachings.teaching: Kausmasumbha (?) and rājikā (?) are vata-pitta aggravating.
teachings.lens_note: Avoid in pitta conditions.

teachings.teaching: Nāḍīca (?) destroys kapha and pitta; cucu (?) is sweet and cooling.
teachings.lens_note: Cooling foods calm the mind.

teachings.teaching: Padmapatra (lotus leaf) is doṣaghna; tripuṭa (?) greatly increases vata.
teachings.lens_note: Lotus is sattvic.

teachings.teaching: Vāstuka (lamb's quarters) is alkaline, destroys all doshas, and is highly appetizing.
teachings.lens_note: Alkaline foods balance pH.

teachings.teaching: Taṇḍukīya (?) and pālaṅkyā (spinach) are similar.
teachings.lens_note: Spinach is iron-rich, good for blood.

teachings.teaching: Mūlaka (radish) raw causes doshas; cooked destroys vata and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Cooking transforms properties.

teachings.teaching: Karkoṭaka (bitter gourd), vārtāka (brinjal), padola (snake gourd), kāravellaka (bitter melon) destroy kuṣṭha, meha, fever, asthma, cough, pitta, and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Bitter gourds are detoxifying.

teachings.teaching: Kūṣmāṇḍa (ash gourd) destroys all doshas, is heart-friendly, and cleanses the bladder.
teachings.lens_note: Ash gourd is cooling and sattvic.

teachings.teaching: Kaliṅga (?) and ālābu (bottle gourd) destroy pitta and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Bottle gourd is light and easy to digest.

teachings.teaching: Trapuṣa (cucumber) and urvāruka (?) counteract vata, kapha, and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Cucumber hydrates and cools.

teachings.teaching: Vṛkṣāmla (?) destroys kapha and vata; jambīra (lemon) reduces kapha and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Lemon is cleansing.

teachings.teaching: Dāḍima (pomegranate) destroys vata, is absorbent; nāgaraṅga (orange) fruit is heavy.
teachings.lens_note: Pomegranate is astringent and balancing.

teachings.teaching: Keśara (saffron) and mātuluṅga (citron) are digestive and reduce kapha and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Saffron is sattvic and uplifting.

teachings.teaching: Māṣa (black gram) alleviates vata and pitta; its skin is unctuous, heating, and removes vata.
teachings.lens_note: Black gram is heavy; use sparingly.

teachings.teaching: Āmalaka (amla) is vṛṣya, sweet, heart-friendly, and sour.
teachings.lens_note: Amla is rich in vitamin C and rejuvenating.

teachings.teaching: Harītakī (haritaki) is appetizing, sacred, and comparable to nectar.
teachings.lens_note: Haritaki is a key herb in triphala for cleansing.

teachings.teaching: Akṣa (?) destroys kapha and vata, and is tridoṣajit.
teachings.lens_note: Balancing all doshas is ideal.

teachings.teaching: Tintiḍī (tamarind) fruit is sour, laxative, and alleviates vata and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Tamarind is heating; use in moderation.

teachings.teaching: Lakuca (?) is doṣala (aggravates doshas); bakula (?) is sweet and destroys kapha and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Bakula flowers are fragrant and calming.

teachings.teaching: Bījapūraka (?) destroys gulma, vata, kapha, asthma, and cough.
teachings.lens_note: Respiratory health supports pranayama.

teachings.teaching: Kapittha (wood apple) is absorbent, doṣaghna; ripe is heavy and anti-poison; unripe increases kapha and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Ripe wood apple is cooling.

teachings.teaching: Pakvāmra (ripe mango) increases vata, gives muscle, semen, color, and strength.
teachings.lens_note: Mango is nourishing but can aggravate vata.

teachings.teaching: Jāmbava (jamun) destroys vata, kapha, pitta, is absorbent, and causes constipation.
teachings.lens_note: Jamun is astringent and good for diabetes.

teachings.teaching: Tinduka (?) destroys kapha and vata; badara (jujube) destroys vata and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Jujube is calming.

teachings.teaching: Bilva (bael) causes constipation and increases vata; priyāla (?) removes vata.
teachings.lens_note: Bael is used for digestive issues.

teachings.teaching: Rājādana (?), moca (banana flower), panasa (jackfruit), nārikela (coconut) increase semen and muscle; they are sweet, unctuous, and heavy.
teachings.lens_note: Coconut is cooling and sattvic.

teachings.teaching: Drākṣā (grapes), madhūka (mahua), kharjūra (dates), kuṅkuma (saffron) destroy vata and rakta (blood disorders).
teachings.lens_note: Grapes are rejuvenating.

teachings.teaching: Māgadhī (?) ripe is sweet and destroys asthma and pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Sweet taste pacifies pitta.

teachings.teaching: Ārdraka (ginger) is appetizing, vṛṣya, digestive, and destroys kapha and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Ginger is a universal medicine.

teachings.teaching: Śuṇṭhī (dry ginger), marica (black pepper), pippalī (long pepper) are considered kapha-vata destroyers.
