teachings.lens_note: Trikatu is a classic digestive formula.

teachings.teaching: Marica is not vṛṣya according to Ayurveda.
teachings.lens_note: Pepper is heating; may reduce semen.

teachings.teaching: Hiṅgu (asafoetida) destroys gulma, colic, constipation, vata, and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Asafoetida aids digestion.

teachings.teaching: Yavānī (thyme), dhanyaka (coriander), ājājī (cumin) are excellent vata-kapha destroyers.
teachings.lens_note: Cumin and coriander are cooling.

teachings.teaching: Saindhava (rock salt) is good for eyes, vṛṣya, and tridoṣaśamana.
teachings.lens_note: Rock salt is pure and balancing.

teachings.teaching: Sauvarcala (black salt) destroys constipation, is heating, and relieves heart pain.
teachings.lens_note: Black salt is used in digestive aids.

teachings.teaching: Viḍaṅga (embelia) is heating, relieves colic, sharp, and destroys vata.
teachings.lens_note: Viḍaṅga is anti-helminthic.

teachings.teaching: Romaka (?) is vātala, sweet, appetizing, moistening, and heavy.
teachings.lens_note: Moderation needed.

teachings.teaching: Yavakṣāra (barley alkali) destroys heart, spleen, and throat diseases, and stimulates digestion.
teachings.lens_note: Alkalis balance acidity.

teachings.teaching: Sarjikṣāra (soda ash) is burning, digestive, sharp, and tearing.
teachings.lens_note: Used in small quantities.

teachings.teaching: Nābhasa (rain water) is doṣaghna, light, heart-friendly, and anti-poison.
teachings.lens_note: Rain water is purest.

teachings.teaching: Nādeya (river water) is vātala, rough; sārasa (lake water) is sweet and light.
teachings.lens_note: Water sources affect doshas.

teachings.teaching: Tāḍāga (tank water) is vātala; raucya (?) is appetizing, rough, kapha-destroying, light.
teachings.lens_note: Tank water may be heavy.

teachings.teaching: Nairjhara (spring water) is digestive, pittala; kaupa (well water) and audbhida (?) destroy pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Spring water is energizing.

teachings.teaching: Water exposed to sun by day and moon by night is free from all doshas, equal to sky water.
teachings.lens_note: Solar and lunar energies purify.

teachings.teaching: Hot water destroys fever, asthma, obesity, vata, and kapha; boiled and cooled water is tridoṣaghna; water kept overnight is doṣala.
teachings.lens_note: Warm water aids digestion.

teachings.teaching: Cow's milk destroys vata and pitta, is unctuous, heavy, and rejuvenating.
teachings.lens_note: Cow milk is sattvic.

teachings.teaching: Buffalo milk is heavier than cow's milk, unctuous, and destroys vata.
teachings.lens_note: Buffalo milk is cooling.

teachings.teaching: Goat's milk destroys bleeding diarrhea, cough, asthma, and kapha; is good for eyes, vitalizing, and for women's raktapitta.
teachings.lens_note: Goat milk is lighter and easily digestible.

teachings.teaching: Dadhi (yogurt) is excellent for vata, vṛṣya, but increases pitta and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Yogurt is heavy; best taken with honey or spices.

teachings.teaching: Mantha (buttermilk) and mastu (whey) are doṣaghna and cleanse channels.
teachings.lens_note: Buttermilk is probiotic.

teachings.teaching: Fresh navanīta (butter) destroys grahaṇī (sprue), piles, and colic; kilāṭa (?) etc. are heavy and cause skin diseases.
teachings.lens_note: Fresh butter is nourishing.

teachings.teaching: Takra (buttermilk) is excellent for grahaṇī, swelling, piles, spleen, diarrhea, and gulma; it is tridoṣaśamana.
teachings.lens_note: Buttermilk is a digestive tonic.

teachings.teaching: Sarpis (ghee) is vṛṣya, sweet, destroys vata, pitta, kapha; cow ghee is medhya, good for eyes, and through processing becomes tridoṣajit.
teachings.lens_note: Ghee is sattvic and enhances ojas.

teachings.teaching: Processed ghee destroys epilepsy, insanity, fainting; ghee from goat etc. has similar qualities to cow ghee.
teachings.lens_note: Medicated ghee is used in therapies.

teachings.teaching: Cow's urine destroys kapha and vata, all worms and poison; it cures jaundice, abdominal diseases, skin diseases, piles, swelling, gulma, and urinary disorders.
teachings.lens_note: Gomutra is used in panchagavya.

teachings.teaching: Tila taila (sesame oil) destroys vata and kapha, is strengthening, and good for hair.
teachings.lens_note: Sesame oil is warming.

teachings.teaching: Sārṣapa (mustard oil) destroys worms, jaundice, kapha, fat, and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Mustard oil is heating.

teachings.teaching: Kṣauma (linseed oil) is not good for eyes, destroys pitta and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Linseed oil is for external use.

teachings.teaching: Akṣaja (?) destroys kapha and pitta, is good for hair, skin, and ears.
teachings.lens_note: Nourishes sense organs.

teachings.teaching: Madhu (honey) is tridoṣaghna but also vātala.
teachings.lens_note: Honey is sattvic but heating in excess.

teachings.teaching: Honey destroys hiccup, asthma, worms, vomiting, diabetes, thirst, and poison.
teachings.lens_note: Honey is a natural healer.

teachings.teaching: Ikṣu (sugarcane) and its products destroy raktapitta, are strengthening, vṛṣya, and increase kapha.
teachings.lens_note: Sugarcane juice is cooling.

teachings.teaching: Phāṇita (jaggery syrup) is pittala; taḍi (toddy) and surā (liquor) are light; matsyaṇḍikā (?) is light.
teachings.lens_note: Fermented drinks are tamasic.

teachings.teaching: Khaṇḍa (crystal sugar) is vṛṣya, unctuous, sweet, and destroys blood, pitta, and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Sugar in moderation.

teachings.teaching: Guḍa (jaggery) destroys vata and pitta, is rough, vātaghna, and increases kapha; old jaggery is pittaghna, wholesome, and purifies blood.
teachings.lens_note: Old jaggery is medicinal.

teachings.teaching: Gaḍaśarkarā (?) destroys raktapitta, is vṛṣya, and unctuous.
teachings.lens_note: Sweet taste is nourishing.

teachings.teaching: All alcoholic drinks increase pitta due to sourness, but destroy kapha and vata.
teachings.lens_note: Alcohol is rajasic and tamasic.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses dietary guidelines, including the properties of various foods and drinks (peya, yusha, etc.) and their effects on doshas (vata, pitta, kapha). It also describes symptoms of poisoning and concludes with Dhanvantari beginning to explain the eight types of fever.

story.title: Dietary and Medicinal Instructions
story.arc: Dhanvantari imparts knowledge on food properties and fever classification.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a larger discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; instructional content.

teachings.teaching: Different types of peya (gruel) have specific effects: e.g., vata-anulomana, laghu, vasti-shodhani; peya with satakrada, dadima, vyosha, guda, madhu, pippali is beneficial for cough and diarrhea.
Payasa (rice pudding) increases kapha and strength; krishara (a dish) destroys vata.
Properly prepared soup (supa) is light; vegetables cooked with ghee etc. are beneficial.
Yusha (soup) made with dadima and amalaka stimulates digestion and alleviates vata and pitta; with malaka it is good for cough, asthma, and cold.
Yusha from yava, kola, kulattha is throat-soothing and anti-vata; from mudga and amalaka is absorbent and destroys kapha and pitta.
Curd with guda is anti-vata; saktu (flour) is dry and vata-aggravating; ghrita-purna (ghee-filled) stimulates digestion; shashkuli is heavy and aphrodisiac.
Bhakshya (snacks) with meat are nourishing; those fried in oil harm eyesight; those boiled in water are hard to digest.
Very hot mandaka (a type of food) is beneficial; cold ones are considered heavy. Water as anupana (after-drink) destroys fatigue and thirst.

Protection of food and drink prevents diseases. Poison symptoms: not hot, peacock-throat colored, discoloring, strong smell/taste/touch, mental distress, eye disease on inhalation, trembling, yawning, etc.
Fever is of eight types: separate, dual, combined, and exogenous.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a comprehensive list of Ayurvedic remedies for various diseases including fever (jvara), diarrhea (atisara), digestive issues (grahani), hemorrhoids (arsha), anemia (pandu), bleeding disorders (raktapitta), cough (kasa), hiccup (hikka), vomiting (chardi), epilepsy (apasmara), and others. It describes herbal decoctions, powders, medicated ghees, and other formulations.

story.title: Chapter 170 (part 1)
story.arc: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a comprehensive list of Ayurvedic remedies for various diseases including fever (jvara), diarrhea (atisara), digestive issues (grahani), hemorrhoids (arsha), anemia (pandu), bleeding disorders (raktapitta), cough (kasa), hiccup (hikka), vomiting (chardi), epilepsy (apasmara), and others. It describes herbal decoctions, powders, medicated ghees, and other formulations.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides medical remedies for various ailments including urinary disorders, swelling, tumors, wounds, and skin conditions. It prescribes herbal formulations, dietary recommendations, and treatments such as decoctions, pastes, and oils.

story.title: Medical Remedies in Garuda Purana
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a medical treatise.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical text.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal remedies for urinary disorders, swellings, tumors, and wounds are prescribed.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, these remedies can be seen as practical applications of balancing bodily humors (doshas) through natural substances, aligning with the principle of using external means to support internal purification and health.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari, provides medical treatments for various diseases including sinus fistulas, fractures, skin diseases (kushtha), leucoderma, hyperacidity, eruptions, ear ailments, and oral diseases. It prescribes herbal formulations, oils, decoctions, and surgical procedures.

story.title: Medical Treatments for Various Diseases
story.arc: Dhanvantari instructs Suśruta on treatments for sinus fistulas, fractures, skin diseases, leucoderma, hyperacidity, eruptions, ear and oral diseases.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; instructional discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily medical prescriptions.

teachings.teaching: For sinus fistulas, incise with a knife and treat like a wound; use guggulu, triphala, and other herbs with ghee.
For fractures, first cool with cold water, then apply paste and bandage with kuśa grass; diet includes māṣa, meat, ghee, milk, and soup.
For skin diseases, use emetics, purgatives, bloodletting; various herbal pastes and oils are prescribed.
For leucoderma (śvitra), use decoctions of dhātrī and khadira with vāgajī; also apply aparājitā paste.
For hyperacidity (amlapitta), use decoctions of triphalā, nimba, etc. with honey; also pippalī with honey.
For ear diseases, use warm goat urine with rock salt for severe pain; jātī leaf oil for pus; śuṇṭhī and mustard oil for earache.
For oral diseases, use triphalā mouthwash; a powder of tejoda, triphalā, lodhra, citraka with honey for gum and throat ailments.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 171) provides a detailed medical treatise on the treatment of eye diseases and other ailments using herbal remedies, including formulations for netraroga (eye diseases), timira (cataract), and various other conditions. It also includes treatments for headaches, menstrual disorders, and other ailments, emphasizing the use of natural ingredients like triphala, honey, and ghee.

entities.name: trikaṭu
aliases: three pungents: ginger, black pepper, long pepper

story.title: Medical Treatise on Eye Diseases and Other Ailments
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical chapter.

teachings.teaching: Diseases originating from the eyes and abdomen (akṣikukṣibhavā rogāḥ) such as cold, wounds, and fever are pacified within five days by fasting (laṅghana).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, fasting (laṅghana) is a purificatory practice that reduces tamas and rajas, preparing the body for higher yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal collyriums and medicated ghees are prescribed for eye diseases, emphasizing the use of natural substances like triphala, honey, and ghee.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as external aids to cleanse the subtle channels (nāḍīs) related to vision, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga focus on purification.

teachings.teaching: Nasal administration (nasya) of medicated oils prepared from daśamūlī etc. alleviates headaches and diseases of the upper body.
teachings.lens_note: Nasya is a panchakarma therapy that in Kriya-Yoga is seen as a method to clear the pranic channels in the head, facilitating deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: Specific herbal drinks and pastes are recommended for menstrual disorders (asṛgdara) and other gynecological issues.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would view these as balancing the doshas, particularly vata and pitta, to maintain health for spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari, provides medical treatments for women's diseases (strīroga) and general ailments. It includes herbal remedies for gynecological issues, childbirth, lactation, and pediatric care, as well as treatments for poisoning, fever, and skin diseases. The chapter also discusses seasonal regimens, panchakarma therapies, and rejuvenative formulas (rasāyana).

story.title: Medical Treatments for Women's Diseases and General Ailments
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a medical treatise.

teachings.teaching: For gynecological disorders, treatments should be vata-pacifying.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with balancing the vata dosha through specific actions and remedies.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal formulations are prescribed for specific conditions like yoni disorders, infertility, and postpartum care.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize the practical application of these remedies as part of a disciplined lifestyle (kriya) to maintain health.

teachings.teaching: Seasonal regimens and panchakarma are recommended for detoxification and rejuvenation.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of aligning with natural cycles and purifying the body-mind system.

teachings.teaching: Mantras and rituals are used for protection of children from grahas (planetary influences).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a means to harmonize subtle energies through sound and intention.

chapter_summary: Dhanvantari describes the six tastes (sweet, sour, salty, pungent, bitter, astringent) and their effects on the body, along with specific herbs and substances that balance the doshas (vata, pitta, kapha). He also discusses the use of ghee, oil, and other snehas, and provides guidelines for diet and treatment based on humoral imbalances.

story.title: Discourse on the Six Tastes and Humoral Balance
story.arc: Dhanvantari expounds on the properties of tastes and herbs for health and healing.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana's medical teachings.
story.comic_potential: A detailed medical treatise; low narrative drama.

teachings.teaching: Sweet taste (madhura) alleviates fainting and burning, pleases the senses, but in excess causes worms and kapha.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the sweet taste corresponds to sattvic nourishment that calms the mind, but overindulgence leads to tamasic inertia.

teachings.teaching: Sour taste (amla) is digestive and carminative, but excessive use causes tooth sensitivity and body looseness.
teachings.lens_note: Sour taste stimulates agni (digestive fire) but can disturb mental equanimity if overused, reflecting rajasic agitation.

teachings.teaching: Salty taste (lavaṇa) cleanses and softens, but excess leads to skin diseases and blood disorders.
teachings.lens_note: Salt represents rajasic qualities that can bind the soul to sensory cravings; moderation is key for spiritual clarity.

teachings.teaching: Pungent taste (kaṭuka) stimulates digestion and clears kapha, but in excess causes burning and dizziness.
teachings.lens_note: Pungent taste ignites tapas (austerity) but can lead to mental agitation; it must be balanced with sattvic practices.

teachings.teaching: Bitter taste (tikta) purifies and reduces fever, but excessive use dries up moisture and causes stiffness.
teachings.lens_note: Bitter taste embodies vairagya (detachment), cleansing the mind of attachments, but overuse leads to dryness of spirit.

teachings.teaching: Astringent taste (kaṣāya) is healing and absorbent, but excess causes heart pain and constipation.
teachings.lens_note: Astringent taste promotes stability and control, but excessive restraint can suppress natural energies, hindering spiritual flow.

teachings.teaching: Ghee (ghṛta) is best for intelligence, memory, and digestion; it is recommended for pitta disorders.
teachings.lens_note: Ghee symbolizes sattvic clarity and is used in Kriya-Yoga to lubricate the nadis and enhance meditation.

teachings.teaching: Oil (taila) is beneficial for vata disorders and for those with strong digestive fire or emaciation.
teachings.lens_note: Oil represents the nurturing aspect of prana, grounding the mind for deeper yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Sneha (oleation) dosage varies by constitution: one pala for strong, three aksha for medium, half pala for weak.
teachings.lens_note: Proper dosage mirrors the yogic principle of moderation in all things to maintain balance in body and mind.

teachings.teaching: Warm water is recommended for sneha and pitta conditions; it promotes vata anuloma and digestive fire.
teachings.lens_note: Warm water symbolizes the internal heat of tapas, aiding purification and spiritual awakening.

teachings.teaching: Svedana (sudation) is indicated for vata and kapha disorders, but not for the very obese, dry, weak, or unconscious.
teachings.lens_note: Svedana represents the sweat of effort in yoga; it must be applied judiciously to avoid exhausting the practitioner.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various medicinal oils and ghee preparations, including Brāhmi Ghṛta, Nārāyaṇa Taila, and others, for treating a range of diseases. It lists ingredients, preparation methods, and therapeutic uses, emphasizing their efficacy in curing vata disorders, skin diseases, and rejuvenation.

story.title: Medicinal Oils and Ghee Preparations
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a continuation of the Garuda Purana's medical section, with Rudra speaking after the previous chapter.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical recipe list.

teachings.teaching: Brāhmi Ghṛta enhances memory and intellect.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the purification of the mind and sharpening of the intellect through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Nārāyaṇa Taila cures all vata disorders and is beneficial for rejuvenation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on balancing the vital energies (vata) for physical and spiritual health.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal preparations can cure skin diseases, paralysis, and other ailments.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees these as methods to purify the body's channels (nadis) for unobstructed prana flow.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 175) presents a dialogue between Lord Shiva (Śaṅkara) and Lord Hari (Viṣṇu) on the treatment of fevers (jvara) using various herbal decoctions and remedies. It categorizes fevers by dosha (vāta, pitta, kapha) and provides specific formulations for each, including single and combined dosha imbalances. The chapter emphasizes the importance of langhana (lightening therapy) and specific herbs like guḍūcī, musta, parpaṭa, and others.

story.title: Remedies for Fevers
story.arc: Instruction on Ayurvedic treatment of fevers
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana, a post-Vedic text; the dialogue is between Viṣṇu and Śiva.
story.comic_potential: A divine consultation on herbal medicine, with Viṣṇu as the teacher and Śiva as the student, listing remedies in a systematic manner.

teachings.teaching: Langhana (lightening therapy) is the primary treatment for all fevers, along with drinking boiled water and avoiding drafts.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, langhana corresponds to reducing mental and physical impurities, preparing the body for deeper yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Specific herbal decoctions are prescribed for vāta, pitta, and kapha fevers, and for combined dosha imbalances.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of balancing the three doshas, akin to balancing the three gunas in Kriya-Yoga for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides recipes for hair growth, blackening, and treatment of scalp and ear ailments using herbal and mineral preparations.

story.title: Hair and Ear Treatments
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely technical.

teachings.teaching: Hair grows after seven nights from application of burnt elephant tusk paste mixed with goat milk and collyrium.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the rapid manifestation of inner purity when external practices align with inner discipline.

teachings.teaching: Oil prepared with Bhṛṅgarāja juice and guñjā powder promotes hair growth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a metaphor for nurturing the subtle body through consistent practice (abhyāsa).

teachings.teaching: Application of mango stone powder makes hair fine.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes refinement of character through tapas (austerity).

teachings.teaching: A paste of mango pith and āmalaka makes hair firm, thick, long, and glossy.
teachings.lens_note: Represents the strengthening of dhāraṇā (concentration) leading to stable spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Oil with viḍaṅga, gandhapāṣāṇa, and cow urine cures scalp diseases.
teachings.lens_note: Purification of the mind (citta) through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Burnt conch powder with lead paste makes hair smooth and black.
teachings.lens_note: Transformation of base instincts into refined qualities.

teachings.teaching: A decoction of Bhṛṅgarāja, iron powder, triphalā, etc., blackens grey hair.
teachings.lens_note: Reversal of aging symbolizes rejuvenation of spiritual vigor.

teachings.teaching: Paste of cakramardaka seeds, kuṣṭha, etc., in sour gruel cures scalp diseases.
teachings.lens_note: Healing of mental afflictions (kleśas) through appropriate remedies.

teachings.teaching: Oil with saindhava, vacā, hiṅgu, etc., cures severe ear pain.
teachings.lens_note: Alleviation of sensory disturbances through balanced practice.

teachings.teaching: Filling ear with sheep urine and saindhava removes pus and worms.
teachings.lens_note: Elimination of impurities from the sense organs.

teachings.teaching: Mālatī flower juice with cow urine cures pus discharge from ear.
teachings.lens_note: Purification of auditory perception.

teachings.teaching: A powder of various herbs for massage increases size of limbs and ears.
teachings.lens_note: Enhancement of physical and subtle capacities through herbal sādhana.

teachings.teaching: Paste of kaṭutaila, bhallātaka, etc., applied to the penis enlarges it.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes strengthening of creative energy (ojas) for spiritual purposes.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a comprehensive list of herbal and mineral remedies for various ailments, primarily eye diseases, but also covering mouth, dental, skin, and systemic conditions. It includes recipes for collyriums, nasal drops, decoctions, and pastes, often addressing Lord Shiva as the listener.

story.title: Medical Remedies from the Garuda Purana
story.arc: A discourse on herbal and mineral treatments for various diseases, addressed to Shiva.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the chapter is a didactic exposition.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical text.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal and mineral preparations can cure eye diseases such as night blindness, timira (cataract), and pterygium.
Nasal drops and decoctions can treat headaches, sinus issues, and oral diseases.
Decoctions and pastes can treat fevers, digestive issues, and body pains.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 177) provides a detailed compendium of medicinal and magical recipes, including treatments for wounds, fractures, eye ailments, digestive issues, and various ailments, as well as rituals for subjugation, enmity, and pest control. It is presented as a dialogue between Lord Shiva (Rudra) and Lord Vishnu (Hari), with instructions for using herbs, animal products, and mantras.

story.title: Netrañjanādinirūpaṇa (Treatise on Eye Ointments and Other Remedies)
story.arc: Instructional dialogue between Vishnu and Shiva on medicinal and magical practices.
story.timeline_note: Undetermined; part of the Garuda Purana's first section.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal and animal-based remedies for wounds, fractures, and ailments are prescribed, emphasizing the use of natural substances.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this reflects the principle of using material elements (dravya) as aids in purification and healing, aligning with the yogic practice of śarīra-śuddhi (body purification).

teachings.teaching: Magical rituals for subjugating enemies, causing enmity, and controlling pests are described, often involving mantras and specific substances.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets such practices as external applications of will (saṅkalpa) and mantra, which can influence the subtle energies of others, but warns against misuse for selfish ends.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various mantras and herbal recipes for vaśīkaraṇa (subjugation/attraction), including methods to control others, enhance beauty, ensure pregnancy, and cause infertility. It also describes the 64 kalās (arts) related to love and attraction.

story.title: Vaśīkaraṇa and Related Practices
story.arc: Instructional manual on mantras and herbal remedies for attraction, subjugation, fertility, and infertility.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: Offering betel nut with mantra to Nārāyaṇī or Hari can bring a person under control.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of using ritual action (kriya) to harmonize energies and influence others through divine connection.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal mixtures and applications (e.g., tilaka, anointing) can attract or subjugate others.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as practical applications of prana and subtle energy manipulation through natural substances, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on direct experience.

teachings.teaching: Mantras like 'oṃ rudraṃ hrāṃ hrīṃ varaśaktā tvaritāvidyā' and 'oṃ mātaraḥ stambhayasvāhā' are used for protection and control.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as sonic vibrations that align the practitioner's energy with cosmic forces, a core Kriya-Yoga technique.

teachings.teaching: The 64 kalās (arts) from Kāmaśāstra are described as means of attraction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might view these as refined expressions of life force (kriya) in interpersonal dynamics, emphasizing mastery over one's own energy.

teachings.teaching: Recipes for pregnancy and infertility are provided.
teachings.lens_note: These align with Kriya-Yoga's holistic approach to health, using natural substances to balance bodily energies.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various medicinal recipes and treatments for dental, ear, and digestive ailments, using herbal and mineral ingredients.

story.title: Medicinal Recipes and Treatments
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a collection of medical prescriptions.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely instructional content.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal and mineral preparations can cure dental discoloration, ear discharge, ear pain, and digestive issues.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, these remedies can be seen as practical applications of purifying the physical body to support spiritual practice, aligning with the principle of bodily purity as a foundation for higher yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various magical recipes and rituals for controlling women, curing fevers, repelling insects, and other practical purposes. It includes instructions for making incense, ointments, and pills using herbs and animal products.

story.title: Magical Recipes and Rituals
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a list of instructions.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal and magical preparations can be used to influence women, cure diseases, and repel pests.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, these practices might be seen as lower-level tantric techniques that manipulate external energies, but the true yoga is internal control of prana and mind.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various medicinal recipes and treatments for ailments such as cough, asthma, hiccup, skin disorders, hair removal, and teeth whitening. It includes ingredients like betel leaf, ghee, honey, salt, copper vessel, milk, and various herbs.

story.title: Medicinal Recipes and Treatments
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of remedies.

teachings.teaching: A mixture of betel leaf, ghee, honey, salt in a copper vessel with milk is a remedy for eye diseases and pain.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this may symbolize the purification of the senses (eyes) through the combination of elements (earth, water, fire, air, ether) and the practice of pranayama.

teachings.teaching: Haritaki, vaca, kustha, vyosha, hinga, and manasila mixed with honey and ghee are licked for cough, asthma, and hiccup.
teachings.lens_note: This remedy can be seen as a metaphor for balancing the doshas through disciplined practice (kriya), where the herbs represent different aspects of the mind-body complex.

teachings.teaching: Pippali and triphala powder with honey is taken to cure chronic cold, cough, and severe asthma.
teachings.lens_note: The combination of pippali (long pepper) and triphala (three fruits) may represent the harmonization of the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) through yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Root of citraka, bhasma, and pippali powder mixed with honey is licked for asthma, cough, and hiccup.
teachings.lens_note: Citraka (plumbago) is a heating herb; in Kriya-Yoga, this may correspond to the awakening of kundalini and the purification of nadis.

teachings.teaching: Blue lotus, sugar, madhuka, and padmaka mixed with rice water stops bleeding disorders.
teachings.lens_note: This cooling remedy may symbolize the calming of the mind and emotions through meditation and pranayama.

teachings.teaching: Ginger and sugar with honey makes the voice like a cuckoo when taken as a pill after meals.
teachings.lens_note: Improving the voice can be linked to the purification of the throat chakra (vishuddhi) through mantra and breath control.

teachings.teaching: Haritala, conch powder, and banana leaf ash are used for hair removal.
teachings.lens_note: Hair removal may symbolize the removal of impurities and the cultivation of detachment (vairagya) in the yogic path.

teachings.teaching: Salt, haritala, tumbini fruits, and lac juice are used for hair removal.
teachings.lens_note: This combination may represent the dissolution of ego and attachments through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Lime, haritala, conch ash, manasila, and rock salt ground with goat's milk is an excellent hair remover.
teachings.lens_note: The use of goat's milk (a sattvic substance) may indicate the need for purity in the practice of kriya.

teachings.teaching: Conch, amalaka, dhātakī flowers ground with milk and held in the mouth for a week makes teeth white and shiny.
teachings.lens_note: Teeth whitening can be seen as a metaphor for purifying speech and thoughts, essential in the yogic path.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various medicinal and magical recipes for health, beauty, strength, and supernatural powers such as fire and water stasis, subjugation, and poison removal. It includes mantras and herbal preparations.

story.title: Medicinal and Magical Formulations
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of a dialogue between Shiva and Garuda.
story.comic_potential: Low; it's a list of recipes.

teachings.teaching: Curd is generally condemned in summer, spring, and rains, but recommended in winter, autumn, and the rainy season.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of adapting practices to seasons and bodily humors for balance.

teachings.teaching: Consuming old jaggery and sugar with butter gives strength and virility.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a practical application of balancing energies (ojas) through diet.

teachings.teaching: A paste of powdered kuṣṭha with ghee and honey eaten at bedtime removes baldness and grey hair.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on rejuvenation (rasayana) and maintaining vitality.

teachings.teaching: A mixture of flax, māṣa, wheat flour, and pippalī applied with ghee makes one handsome like Kandarpa.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a method to enhance personal magnetism and sattvic appearance.

teachings.teaching: Consuming a preparation of barley, sesame, ashwagandha, musali, sarala, and jaggery makes one young and strong.
teachings.lens_note: This is a classic rejuvenative formula in Ayurveda, which Kriya-Yoga incorporates for physical health.

teachings.teaching: Drinking a decoction of asafoetida, rock salt, and ginger cures colic and indigestion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note this as a practical remedy for digestive issues, essential for maintaining prana flow.

teachings.teaching: Paste of dhātakī and somarājī with milk makes a weak person stout.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of using specific herbs to build tissue (dhatu).

teachings.teaching: Licking butter mixed with sugar and honey, and drinking milk, gives strength, nourishment, and intelligence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a simple sattvic diet to enhance ojas and medha (intellect).

teachings.teaching: Drinking powdered crab with milk cures consumption.
teachings.lens_note: This is an example of using animal products therapeutically, which Kriya-Yoga may adapt with caution.

teachings.teaching: A paste of bhallātaka, viḍaṅga, yavakṣāra, rock salt, realgar, conch shell, and oil removes hair.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would classify this as a depilatory, possibly for ritual or hygiene purposes.

teachings.teaching: Paste of mālūra juice and leech applied to hands stops fire (burns).
teachings.lens_note: This is a folk remedy; Kriya-Yoga might interpret 'fire' metaphorically as inner heat or anger.

teachings.teaching: Paste of śālmalī juice and donkey urine thrown into fire stops fire.
teachings.lens_note: Again, a literal fire-stopping charm; Sharma might see it as a symbolic control of agni.

teachings.teaching: A pill made from crow's stomach and frog fat thrown into fire stops fire.
teachings.lens_note: Similar to above; Kriya-Yoga may view this as a practice to master the element of fire.

teachings.teaching: Chewing muṇḍī bark, vacā, musta, marica, and tagara allows one to lick fire with the tongue.
teachings.lens_note: This suggests a siddhi (power) over fire; Sharma would relate it to advanced pranayama and tapas.

teachings.teaching: A paste of gorocanā and bhṛṅgarāja with ghee, along with a mantra, stops water.
teachings.lens_note: Water stasis mantra; Kriya-Yoga might use this as a concentration technique to control the water element.

teachings.teaching: Mantra 'oṃ namo bhagavate jalaṃ stambhaya...' stops water.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize the power of sound vibrations to influence elements.

teachings.teaching: Burying vulture bone, cow bone, and discarded garlands at an enemy's door causes their death.
teachings.lens_note: This is black magic; Kriya-Yoga generally avoids harmful practices, focusing on self-purification.

teachings.teaching: A tilaka made from five red flowers, kumkum, and one's own blood is a subjugating charm.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would caution against using such methods for control; true yoga is about self-mastery.

teachings.teaching: Brahmadaṇḍī taken with puṣya (nakshatra) in food or drink is subjugating.
teachings.lens_note: Again, a subjugation practice; Kriya-Yoga might reinterpret as a way to subdue one's own negative traits.

teachings.teaching: Drinking a decoction of yaṣṭi madhu (licorice) and honey cures constipation and heart pain.
teachings.lens_note: A simple Ayurvedic remedy; Sharma would appreciate its balancing effect on vata.

teachings.teaching: Mantra 'oṃ hrūṃ jaḥ' removes scorpion venom.
teachings.lens_note: Mantra therapy; Kriya-Yoga uses bija mantras for healing.

teachings.teaching: A nasal or oral preparation of pippalī, butter, ginger, rock salt, marica, curd, and kuṣṭha removes poison.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a practical antidote, possibly with symbolic meaning of transforming toxins.

teachings.teaching: Drinking or applying a paste of triphalā, ārdraka, kuṣṭha, sandalwood, and ghee removes poison.
teachings.lens_note: Another antidote; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes purification of the body to eliminate toxins.

teachings.teaching: A mixture of pigeon eyes, orpiment, and realgar kills poison like Garuda kills snakes.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note the symbolic association with Garuda, representing the power to overcome poison.

teachings.teaching: A paste of rock salt, three spices, curd, honey, and ghee removes scorpion venom.
teachings.lens_note: Again, a practical remedy; Kriya-Yoga might see it as balancing the three doshas.

teachings.teaching: Drinking a decoction of brahmadaṇḍī and sesame with trikaṭuka powder cures abdominal tumors and blood obstruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a remedy for serious digestive disorders, aligning with cleansing practices.

teachings.teaching: Drinking milk with honey stops bleeding; applying paste of āṭarūpaka root on vagina and navel ensures easy childbirth.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga values such practical knowledge for women's health and safe delivery.

teachings.teaching: Drinking sugar and honey with rice water stops dysentery with blood.
teachings.lens_note: A simple remedy for a common ailment; Sharma would approve of its simplicity and effectiveness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a series of medicinal recipes and mantras for curing various ailments such as diarrhea, jaundice, fever, skin diseases, and spleen disorders. It includes herbal preparations, dietary recommendations, and a mantra for fever. The chapter is addressed by Hari (Vishnu) to Rudra (Shiva).

story.title: Medicinal Recipes and Mantras
story.arc: Hari instructs Rudra on various remedies for diseases.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; didactic discourse.
story.comic_potential: Low; instructional content.

teachings.teaching: A mixture of marica, śṛṅgavera, kuṭajatvac, etc., cures grahaṇī (sprue).
Pippalī, pippalīmūla, marica, tagara, vacā, devadāru, pāṭhā with milk cure atisāra (diarrhea).
Collyrium from marīca and tilapuṣpa removes kāmalā (jaundice).
Harītakī with guḍa and madhu acts as a purgative.
Triphalā, citraka, citra, kaṭukarohiṇī cure ūrustambha (thigh stiffness) and are excellent purgatives.
Decoction of harītakī, śṛṅgavera, devadāru, candana with goat milk and apāmārga root, or jayantī, cures ūrustambha in seven days.
Fine powder of anantā and śṛṅgavera with guggulu and guḍa as pills destroys vāyu in tendons and agnimāndya (digestive weakness).
Saṅkhapuṣpī with flowers, leaves, and root, taken with goat milk, cures apasmāra (epilepsy).
Aśvagandhā and abhayā with water cure raktapitta (bleeding disorders).
Harītakī and kuṣṭha powder placed in mouth, then drinking cold water, stops vomiting.
Gaḍūcī, padma, kāriṣṭha, dhānyāka, raktacandana cure pitta-śleṣma fever, vomiting, thirst, and increase digestive fire.
Mantra 'oṃ jambhinī stambhinī mohaya...' with śaṅkhapuṣpī tied to ear removes fever.
Reciting the mantra 108 times over a flower, touching the nail, cures cāturthika (quartan) fever and other fevers.
Fumigation with jambūphala, haridrā, and snake slough cures all fevers including cāturthika.
Oil boiled with karavīra, bhṛṅgapatra, lavaṇa, kuṣṭha, karkaṭa, and four times urine cures pāmā, vicarcikā, kuṣṭha, and wounds by anointment.
Drinking pippalī with honey and eating sweet foods, along with sūraṇa, destroys plīhā (spleen disorder).
Pippalī and haridrā with cow urine applied to the anus cures arśas (hemorrhoids).
Drinking goat milk with fresh ginger destroys plīhā.
Paste of saindhava, viḍaṅga, somarājī, sarṣapa, rajanī (two), viṣa with cow urine, and application of nimba leaves cures kuṣṭha (leprosy).

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various medicinal and alchemical recipes, including treatments for diseases like piles, fistula, urinary disorders, skin diseases, and fever, as well as procedures for making gold, attracting others, and achieving supernatural abilities.

story.title: Medicinal and Alchemical Recipes
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; a collection of recipes.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily technical instructions.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal and mineral preparations can cure diseases like piles, fistula, urinary disorders, skin diseases, and fever.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these remedies may be seen as practical applications of yogic principles to purify the body and balance doshas, supporting the practitioner's physical health for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Alchemical processes can transform base metals into gold and silver.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, alchemy symbolizes the transformation of the base human nature into divine consciousness; these recipes may be interpreted as metaphors for inner purification and transmutation of energies.

teachings.teaching: Mantras and herbal applications can provide protection, invisibility, and attraction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views such practices as preliminary siddhis (powers) that arise from mastery of prana and concentration; they are not to be sought as ends but as signs of progress.

teachings.teaching: Specific treatments for burns, wounds, and snakebite are provided.
teachings.lens_note: These practical remedies align with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on maintaining a healthy physical body as a foundation for higher practices.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 185) is a practical manual of mantras, rituals, and herbal remedies for achieving various worldly goals such as wealth, victory, control over others (especially women), and curing diseases. It includes a mantra for Ganesha, instructions for japa and homa, and numerous recipes for tilakas, ointments, and medicines for ailments like colic, diarrhea, and eye diseases.

story.title: Mantras and Remedies for Worldly Success
story.arc: Instructional dialogue between Hari (Vishnu) and Shiva, detailing practical techniques for power, health, and control.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; timeless teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual with no narrative events.

teachings.teaching: The mantra 'āṃ gaṇapataye' is a source of wealth and knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this mantra is a bija for activating the root chakra, grounding energy for material stability.

teachings.teaching: Japa of the mantra 8,000 times and tying a śikhā (topknot) brings victory in business.
teachings.lens_note: The śikhā represents focused intention; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes pranayama and concentration to achieve success.

teachings.teaching: Homa with black sesame and ghee (108 offerings) can subdue a king within three days.
teachings.lens_note: This ritual aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of using fire as a transformative element to purify desires.

teachings.teaching: Fasting on aṣṭamī and caturdaśī, worshiping Vighnarāṭ, and offering 108 oblations of sesame and rice ensures invincibility in battle.
teachings.lens_note: Fasting and worship are Kriya-Yoga tools for mental discipline and invoking divine protection.

teachings.teaching: Applying a tilaka made from specific herbs (e.g., gorocana, kuṅkuma) can attract women.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, external tilakas symbolize internal energy centers; this practice is a lower-level application of pranic control.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal remedies cure diseases like colic, diarrhea, eye ailments, and respiratory issues.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga views health as a prerequisite for spiritual practice; these remedies support the physical body.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a series of herbal and Ayurvedic remedies for various ailments including urinary disorders, goiter, fistula, hemorrhoids, and digestive issues. It is presented as a dialogue between Lord Shiva (Rudra) and the sage, with instructions for preparing and administering medicines.

entities.name: Trikatuka
aliases: three pungents: ginger, black pepper, long pepper

story.title: Ayurvedic Remedies for Various Diseases
story.arc: The chapter is a compilation of medicinal recipes without a narrative arc. It is a didactic discourse on herbal treatments.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the chapter is a timeless set of instructions.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical medical text with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal formulations can cure urinary disorders, goiter, fistula, hemorrhoids, and digestive issues.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, these remedies represent practical applications of purifying the body (kriya) to support spiritual practice. The emphasis on natural substances aligns with the yogic principle of using the material world to transcend it.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various herbal remedies and dietary practices, primarily using the leaves and seeds of the Palasha tree (Butea monosperma), combined with milk, honey, ghee, curd, and other substances, to achieve longevity, freedom from disease, strong physique, good eyesight, and prevention of gray hair and wrinkles. The instructions are given by Lord Hari (Vishnu) to Rudra (Shiva).

story.title: Ayushkara Yoga: Remedies for Longevity and Health
story.arc: Instructional dialogue between Hari and Rudra on herbal preparations.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a list of recipes and benefits.

teachings.teaching: Consuming powdered Palasha leaves with milk for seven days makes one free from all diseases and grants longevity and strength.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the purification of the body through natural substances, aligning with the practice of shuddhi (cleansing) to prepare for higher yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Regular consumption of milk with honey and ghee extends life to ten thousand years.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching highlights the importance of sattvic diet in Kriya-Yoga for maintaining a long, healthy life conducive to spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Eating Palasha powder with curd daily makes the body as hard as diamond (vajradeha).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, a strong body is essential for enduring the rigors of meditation and pranayama; this remedy supports physical resilience.

teachings.teaching: Application of Palasha powder mixed with buffalo milk restores black hair to bald men.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of rejuvenation (kaya kalpa) to maintain youthfulness for prolonged spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Anointing with Palasha powder mixed with oil removes wrinkles and gray hair.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views external appearance as a reflection of inner vitality; such practices support the yogi's ability to maintain a youthful body for sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Consuming Palasha seeds with butter for a month, after bowing to Hari, grants a lifespan of five hundred years, free from gray hair and wrinkles, with the strength of an elephant.
teachings.lens_note: This combines devotion (bhakti) with herbal remedy, illustrating the Kriya-Yoga integration of physical health and spiritual surrender.

teachings.teaching: Taking root of Bhringaraja (Eclipta alba) with sour gruel for a month grants a lifespan of five hundred years, free from gray hair and wrinkles, with elephant-like strength.
teachings.lens_note: Bhringaraja is known in Ayurveda for rejuvenation; in Kriya-Yoga, such herbs are used to support the body's energy systems for meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various herbal and medicinal remedies for wounds, abscesses, bleeding, fistulas, and ailments caused by supernatural entities. It includes treatments using plants like apāmārga, rudra lāṅgalikā, bālamūla, meṣaśṛṅgī, brahmayaṣṭi, and others, as well as formulations with guggulu, owl feathers, and other ingredients. The remedies are presented as instructions from Rudra/Śiva to Śaṅkara, emphasizing practical healing.

story.title: Remedies for Wounds and Supernatural Afflictions
story.arc: Instructional dialogue between Rudra and Śaṅkara on medicinal treatments.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; timeless teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a list of remedies.

teachings.teaching: Use apāmārga root paste to stop bleeding from wounds.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes using natural, grounded practices to purify and heal the physical body, aligning with the principle of tapas (austerity) to remove impurities.

teachings.teaching: Apply rudra lāṅgalikā and darbha paste to extract foreign objects from wounds.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga concept of removing internal obstacles (kleshas) through disciplined practice, akin to extracting a splinter.

teachings.teaching: Use bālamūla or meṣaśṛṅgī paste for chronic fistulas.
teachings.lens_note: Chronic ailments represent deep-seated samskaras; persistent herbal treatment mirrors sustained yogic practice to uproot them.

teachings.teaching: Brahmayaṣṭi fruit paste with water cures blood disorders.
teachings.lens_note: Blood disorders symbolize emotional imbalances; this remedy parallels pranayama to purify the nadis and stabilize the mind.

teachings.teaching: Guggulu and owl feather smoke drives away evil spirits.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents using subtle vibrations (mantra, pranayama) to dispel negative energies and protect the practitioner's aura.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various herbal and ritual remedies for ailments such as dental pain, fever, poisoning, gynecological issues, and fear of wild animals. It includes instructions for using plant roots, flowers, and other substances, often combined with specific rituals or times.

story.title: Herbal Remedies and Rituals
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a list of remedies.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical manual.

teachings.teaching: Various plant-based remedies can cure ailments like dental pain, fever, poisoning, and fear.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these remedies may be seen as practical applications of harnessing natural energies (prāṇa) through specific herbs and rituals, aligning with the principle of using material means for spiritual and physical purification.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a series of herbal and natural remedies for various ailments such as skin diseases (kuṣṭha, dadru, pāmā, sidhma), abdominal disorders, cough, fever, and others. It includes recipes using plants like aparājitā, indravāruṇī, punarnavā, kadalī, nimbadalā, harītakī, and many others, often combined with cow's urine, milk, ghee, or honey. The remedies are presented as instructions from Lord Hari (Viṣṇu) to Rudra (Śiva).

story.title: Remedies for Various Diseases
story.arc: Instructional dialogue between Hari and Rudra on herbal medicine
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a straightforward list of remedies.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal combinations can cure skin diseases, abdominal disorders, and other ailments.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, these remedies can be seen as external practices (kriyas) that purify the body, supporting inner spiritual practice by removing physical obstacles.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a comprehensive list of herbal and natural remedies for various types of poisons, including snake bites, scorpion stings, insect bites, and other toxins. It details specific plant roots, mixtures, and applications (internal and external) to neutralize venom, often with instructions for preparation and administration. The chapter is framed as a dialogue where Lord Hari (Vishnu) instructs Shiva (Maheshvara) on these antidotes.

story.title: Antidotes for Poisons
story.arc: Instructional dialogue between Hari and Shiva on herbal remedies for venom.
story.timeline_note: Mythological setting; no specific historical timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a technical list of remedies.

teachings.teaching: Various plant roots and mixtures can neutralize snake venom, scorpion venom, and other poisons when prepared and administered correctly.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this reflects the principle of using natural substances to purify the body and mind, aligning with the yogic practice of detoxification and balance.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides detailed recipes for various medicated oils and preparations to treat ailments such as ear diseases, skin disorders, digestive issues, and to enhance memory and intelligence. It includes instructions for making modakas, tailas (oils), ghṛta (ghee), and vartis (collyrium) using specific herbs and minerals.

story.title: Medicinal Formulations in Garuda Purana
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; the chapter is a compendium of recipes.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical text.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal and mineral formulations can cure diseases like ear pain, skin disorders, digestive issues, and enhance memory.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, these formulations can be seen as external aids to purify the body and mind, supporting the internal practice of yoga by removing obstacles to health and concentration.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a collection of Ayurvedic herbal remedies for various ailments including skin diseases, fever, cough, digestive issues, and mental disorders. It emphasizes the power of remembering Lord Vishnu as the ultimate medicine.

story.title: Herbal Remedies and the Power of Vishnu
story.arc: Instructional discourse on medicinal preparations and the supremacy of divine remembrance.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a list of recipes and remedies.

teachings.teaching: Remembering Lord Vishnu cures all diseases.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle that divine consciousness (Ishvara pranidhana) purifies the mind and body, removing obstacles to spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Herbal preparations can cure physical and mental ailments.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views such remedies as supportive for the physical body, which is the vehicle for yoga practice; a healthy body aids in meditation and self-discipline.

chapter_summary: This chapter presents the Vaiṣṇava Kavaca, a protective hymn revealed by Lord Hari (Viṣṇu). It describes how the armor invokes various forms and weapons of Viṣṇu to protect the devotee's body parts, and includes mantras for warding off dangers, diseases, and enemies. The chapter also lists the benefits of reciting this kavaca in various perilous situations.

entities.name: Madhukaiṭabhamardana
aliases: Viṣṇu as slayer of Madhu and Kaiṭabha

story.title: The Vaiṣṇava Kavaca
story.arc: Instruction on the protective armor of Viṣṇu
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a discourse by Hari to Garuḍa, set in the context of the Garuḍa Purāṇa.
story.comic_potential: The vivid descriptions of Viṣṇu's weapons and incarnations protecting the devotee's body parts could be illustrated as a visual armor, with each deity or weapon assigned to a specific limb.

teachings.teaching: Recitation of the Vaiṣṇava Kavaca protects from all diseases, dangers, and enemies.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this kavaca is a meditative practice where the devotee visualizes each deity and weapon on corresponding body parts, integrating the protective energy of Viṣṇu into one's own being.

teachings.teaching: The kavaca should be recited in times of fear, danger, or illness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such recitation is a form of Kriya-Yoga, using sound vibration to align the practitioner with divine protection and purify the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: The devotee becomes invincible and free from fear by wearing this armor.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga goal of achieving a state of fearlessness and unity with the divine, where the practitioner realizes their identity with Viṣṇu.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a hymn of praise to Lord Vishnu in his various forms, including Vasudeva, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, and Samkarshana. It describes the supreme nature of Vishnu as the ultimate reality, beyond mind and senses, and mentions the power of the Vishnudharma vidya to grant kingship and celestial status. The chapter includes a mantra and concludes with a statement about the vidya's efficacy.

story.title: Hymn to Vishnu and the Power of Vishnudharma Vidya
story.arc: The chapter is a devotional hymn praising Vishnu's supreme nature and the efficacy of the Vishnudharma vidya in granting kingship and celestial status.
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; the chapter is a standalone hymn.
story.comic_potential: The vivid descriptions of Vishnu's forms and the mantra could be visually depicted in a comic format.

teachings.teaching: The supreme reality is Vishnu, who is beyond mind, senses, and duality, and is the source of all.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the practice of inner stillness and meditation to realize the self as one with the supreme consciousness, transcending the dualities of mind and senses.

teachings.teaching: Recitation of the Vishnudharma vidya can grant kingship and celestial status, as exemplified by Citraketu.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the power of mantra and focused intention (sankalpa) to manifest desired outcomes, aligning one's will with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes a mantra nyasa and protective visualization practice centered on the twelve-syllable mantra 'Om Namo Narayanaya'. It details the placement of syllables on body parts, the invocation of Vishnu's avatars and other deities for protection from various dangers, and the benefits of this practice.

story.title: Mantra Nyasa and Protective Visualization
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a ritual manual with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: The practice of nyasa (placing syllables on body parts) and visualization of Vishnu's avatars for protection from various dangers leads to freedom from sin, disease, and attainment of heaven.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this practice embodies the integration of mantra, mudra, and dhyana to purify the subtle body and align with divine will, transforming the practitioner's karma.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Garuda mantra and its associated nyasa (ritual placement) and meditation practices. It details the five elements, the placement of deities and nagas in the body, the visualization of Garuda as a fierce form, and the method for achieving siddhi (perfection) in all endeavors, especially for counteracting poison.

story.title: The Garuda Mantra and Nyasa Vidhi
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a discourse by Dhanvantari, recounting the Garuda mantra as originally spoken by Garuda to Kashyapa.
story.comic_potential: Visualizing Garuda as a multi-armed, multi-faced deity with serpents and cosmic elements could be depicted in a dramatic, symbolic panel.

teachings.teaching: The five elements (earth, water, fire, air, ether) are the abodes of deities and are to be meditated upon within the body.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the internalization of the elements through pranayama and dharana, purifying the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: Nyasa (ritual placement) of mantras on the body, including the six limbs (ṣaḍaṅga) of Śiva, is essential for siddhi.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that nyasa aligns the practitioner's microcosm with the macrocosm, activating energy centers.

teachings.teaching: Meditation on Garuda as the destroyer of poison and all obstacles, visualized with fierce form and cosmic dimensions.
teachings.lens_note: This visualization is a form of ishta-devata yoga, focusing the mind on a protective deity to overcome inner and outer poisons (vishas).

teachings.teaching: The mantra 'Oṃ svāhā' combined with the five elements is the direct form of Garuda, effective for all actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the bija mantra's resonance with the five tattvas, harmonizing the practitioner's energy.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the power of Garuda and a mantra for Tripura. It begins with a statement that by seeing Garuda, one can destroy pretas, bhutas, yakshas, nagas, gandharvas, and rakshasas, as well as fevers like chaturthika. Then Dhanvantari narrates how Garuda taught this knowledge to Kashyapa, and Maheshvara taught it to Gauri. The chapter then transitions to a mantra for Tripura, the goddess who grants enjoyment and liberation, with various bija mantras and instructions for worship.

story.title: The Power of Garuda and the Tripura Mantra
story.arc: Instruction on divine knowledge and mantra
story.timeline_note: Mythological past; transmission of knowledge from deities to sages.
story.comic_potential: Visual of Garuda destroying various beings, and the chanting of bija mantras.

teachings.teaching: Seeing Garuda destroys pretas, bhutas, yakshas, nagas, gandharvas, rakshasas, and fevers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the power of divine vision (darshana) to purify the subtle body and remove obstacles.

teachings.teaching: The Tripura mantra grants bhukti (enjoyment) and mukti (liberation).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the integration of worldly life and spiritual liberation through the practice of mantra and devotion.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Jvalamukhi ritual, including the invocation of various Bhairava and Matrika deities in the cardinal and intermediate directions, the worship of Ratipriti and Kamadeva, and the benefits of the practice for removing enemies and diseases.

story.title: Jvalamukhi Ritual and the Cudamani
story.arc: Bhairava describes the Jvalamukhi ritual for removing enemies and diseases, and then begins to explain the Cudamani for purifying auspicious and inauspicious effects.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a continuation of the Garuda Purana's teachings, with Bhairava as the speaker.
story.comic_potential: The detailed invocation of fierce deities and the promise of destroying enemies could be depicted as a dramatic ritual scene.

teachings.teaching: The Jvalamukhi ritual involves invoking Bhairava and Matrika deities in the directions, worshipping Ratipriti and Kamadeva, and performing meditation, recitation, and offerings to achieve success and destroy diseases.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this ritual symbolizes the internal purification and activation of subtle energies (kundalini) through systematic invocation and worship, aligning the practitioner's microcosm with the macrocosm.

teachings.teaching: The Cudamani is to be explained for the purification of auspicious and inauspicious effects.
teachings.lens_note: This likely refers to a method for neutralizing karmic residues, which in Kriya-Yoga corresponds to the cleansing of samskaras through pranayama and meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the method of drawing a cūḍāmaṇi (crest-jewel) diagram for determining auspicious and inauspicious outcomes. It lists eight symbols (dhvaja, dhūma, siṃha, śvā, vṛṣa, khara, gaja, dhvāṅkṣa) placed in eight positions, and provides interpretations for each combination of symbol and position, covering results related to wealth, health, relationships, and more.

story.title: The Crest-Jewel Diagram
story.arc: Instruction on divination through the cūḍāmaṇi diagram
story.timeline_note: Part of the Garuda Purana, likely a dialogue between Bhairava and Devi.
story.comic_potential: A visual guide to interpreting omens, with each symbol and position yielding a specific prediction.

teachings.teaching: The cūḍāmaṇi diagram is used to determine auspicious and inauspicious outcomes by placing eight symbols in eight positions and interpreting the combinations.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this practice can be seen as a form of external ritual that mirrors internal purification; the diagram serves as a tool to focus the mind and align with cosmic rhythms, aiding in the discernment of karmic patterns.

teachings.teaching: Each symbol in a specific position yields a specific result, such as wealth, health, or conflict.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle that external symbols correspond to internal states; by observing the diagram, one can gain insight into the workings of prana and consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the effects of the movement of vital airs (vāyu) in the body, correlating them with the phases of the moon and seasons. It describes how the flow of vāyu in the right and left nostrils influences health, activities, and outcomes such as victory in battle or rainfall. The chapter also mentions the four types of winds (vāyu, agni, jala, śakra) and their auspiciousness.

story.title: The Movement of Vital Airs and Their Effects
story.arc: Exposition on the science of breath and its impact on health and worldly affairs.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a discourse by Dhanvantari to Devi, likely part of a larger Ayurvedic section.
story.comic_potential: The detailed descriptions of wind directions and their effects could be visualized as a diagram or animated sequence showing the flow of breath and its consequences.

teachings.teaching: The movement of vāyu in the right and left nostrils determines auspiciousness for activities like eating, sex, and battle.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the regulation of prāṇa through nāḍī śodhana, where the balance of idā and piṅgalā influences mental and physical states.

teachings.teaching: When vāyu flows in the right nostril (dakṣiṇa), it is favorable for food and sex; when in the left (vāma), it is best for all actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the left nostril (idā) is cooling and lunar, suitable for relaxation and creativity, while the right (piṅgalā) is heating and solar, for dynamic activities.

teachings.teaching: The four types of winds (vāyu, agni, jala, śakra) correspond to the four directions and have specific effects on health and weather.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of the five prāṇas and their directional flows, which can be balanced through specific breathing techniques.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 201) is a treatise on equine and elephant medicine (Haya-Ayurveda and Gaja-Ayurveda). It describes the characteristics of good and bad horses, various diseases, treatments, dietary regimens, and protective rituals. It also covers elephant diseases and their remedies, including mantras and offerings.

story.title: Gaja-Ashva-Ayurveda Nirupana (Treatise on Elephant and Horse Medicine)
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; it is a prescriptive text.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical medical manual.

teachings.teaching: Horses with certain physical defects (e.g., crow-beak, black tongue, tree-like face, hot palate, etc.) should be rejected.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this can be seen as a metaphor for purifying the body and mind: one must reject impure or defective tendencies to achieve spiritual health.

teachings.teaching: Horses are classified as uttama (best), madhyama (medium), and kanīyān (inferior) based on height.
teachings.lens_note: This classification mirrors the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) in Kriya-Yoga, where sattva is the highest quality for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Revanta and homa (fire sacrifice) protect horses.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, external rituals like homa symbolize inner purification through breath control and meditation.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal remedies are prescribed for horse diseases, such as using nimba leaves, guggulu, etc.
teachings.lens_note: These natural remedies correspond to the Kriya-Yoga principle of using the body's own energies (prana) to heal, rather than external substances.

teachings.teaching: For epidemics (upasarga) among horses, perform homa, offerings to Brahmins, and use haritaki formulations.
teachings.lens_note: Epidemics represent collective karmic imbalances; Kriya-Yoga teaches that group meditation and purification can restore harmony.

teachings.teaching: Elephant diseases are treated with similar principles, with dosages quadrupled.
teachings.lens_note: The elephant symbolizes the mind's strength; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the mind requires stronger practices (e.g., longer meditation) to overcome its afflictions.

teachings.teaching: Protective rituals for elephants include worship of deities, offering bali to bhutas, and bathing with four pots.
teachings.lens_note: These rituals externalize the inner process of invoking divine protection and purifying the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: A decoction of triphala, panchakola, dashamula, etc., is prescribed for elephant diseases.
teachings.lens_note: The combination of herbs represents the integration of multiple spiritual practices (e.g., asana, pranayama, meditation) to heal the whole being.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various herbal remedies and practices for women's health issues such as childbirth pain, menstrual disorders, and lactation, as well as remedies for children's protection and rejuvenation treatments for men. It includes recipes using plants like apāmārga, kūṣmāṇḍa, and palāśa, often combined with ghee, honey, and milk.

story.title: Herbal Remedies for Women and Children
story.arc: Instructional discourse on medicinal practices
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's teachings.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily a list of remedies.

teachings.teaching: Various herbal preparations can alleviate women's health issues such as childbirth pain, menstrual disorders, and lactation problems.
Specific remedies protect children from diseases and evil influences.
Consumption of certain herbal mixtures can rejuvenate men, enhance virility, and prolong life.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides various remedies and treatments for ailments affecting cows, buffaloes, horses, and other animals, using herbal preparations and specific practices. It includes instructions for using milk, salt, and plant-based medicines to cure conditions like worm infestations, skin diseases, and other afflictions.

story.title: Remedies for Animal Ailments
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Garuda Purana's teachings on Ayurveda and animal care.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a straightforward list of remedies without narrative drama.

teachings.teaching: A cow that dislikes her calf should be given her own milk mixed with salt to make her love the calf.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this may symbolize the need to sweeten one's actions (kriya) with devotion to create harmony.

teachings.teaching: A dog's bone tied around the neck of buffaloes and cows causes worm infestation to fall away.
teachings.lens_note: This could represent the removal of inner impurities through external symbolic acts.

teachings.teaching: Eating the root of guñjā (Abrus precatorius) causes gojaṅganā (a disease) in cows.
teachings.lens_note: Highlights the importance of avoiding harmful substances, akin to avoiding negative influences in yoga.

teachings.teaching: The juice of varuṇa fruit (Crataeva religiosa) churned by hand destroys worms in quadrupeds and bipeds.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the power of effort (kriya) to cleanse the system.

teachings.teaching: Drinking elephant urine cures afflictions of cows and buffaloes.
teachings.lens_note: May represent using potent natural substances for purification, akin to using prana.

teachings.teaching: Rice seeds (śālibīja) ground with buttermilk and drunk with milk of cow or buffalo is beneficial for the cow and its owner.
teachings.lens_note: Illustrates the synergy of ingredients, mirroring the integration of body and mind in yoga.

teachings.teaching: Leaf of śarapuṅkhā (Tephrosia purpurea) with salt destroys water-blisters and mane diseases in horses.
teachings.lens_note: Shows the use of specific herbs for specific ailments, akin to targeted yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: Leaf of ghṛtakumārī (Aloe vera) with salt destroys itching in horse manes.
teachings.lens_note: Aloe's soothing property parallels the calming effect of meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana lists the names and synonyms of various medicinal plants, herbs, and substances used in Ayurveda. It begins with Dhanvantari teaching Suśruta, then enumerates plants like sthirā, vidārigandhā, śālapaṇya, etc., along with their alternative names. The chapter is a comprehensive botanical glossary.

story.title: Enumeration of Medicinal Plants
story.arc: Dhanvantari teaches Suśruta the names of herbs
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; part of the Garuda Purana's medical section.
story.comic_potential: Low; it's a list of plant names.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge of plant names and synonyms is essential for Ayurveda.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle of svādhyāya (self-study) and the importance of precise knowledge for action.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 204) is a comprehensive list of synonyms and classifications of various herbs, minerals, and substances used in Ayurveda and alchemy. It includes plants like puṣkara, yāsa, dhanvayāsa, vākucī, somarājī, mārkava, keśarāja, bhṛṅgarāja, etc.; minerals like kāśīśa, dhātukāśīśa, saurāṣṭrī, manaḥśilā, haritāla, gandhaka, pārada, tāmra, ayastīkṣṇa, etc.; and other substances like madhu, kṣaudra, sarkarā, etc. It also defines measurement units (pala, prastha, āḍhaka, droṇa, tulā, etc.) and concludes with Sūta's statement that these names are for forest medicines and that the explanation will follow as taught by Kumāra to Śaunaka.

story.title: Enumeration of Herbs, Minerals, and Measures
story.arc: The chapter is a technical list without a narrative arc; it catalogs synonyms and classifications of medicinal substances and measurement units.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Garuda Purana's Ācārakāṇḍa, likely a later section dealing with practical knowledge.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a dense list of synonyms with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge of plant and mineral names is essential for medicine and alchemy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle of precise action (kriya) requiring accurate knowledge of materials used in purification and healing practices.

teachings.teaching: Measurement units are standardized for consistency in preparation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga requires exact measures to achieve desired effects; this reflects the discipline of precise action.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a concise exposition of Sanskrit grammar (vyākaraṇa), covering the classification of words into subanta (nominal) and tiṅanta (verbal), the seven cases (vibhaktis) with their meanings, the ten lakāras (tenses and moods), the three persons (puruṣa), and the formation of kṛt (derived) words. It is attributed to Kumāra (Skanda) instructing Kātyāyana.

story.title: Vyākaraṇa-nirūpaṇa (Exposition of Grammar)
story.arc: Didactic exposition
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; a teaching dialogue.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely technical grammar.

teachings.teaching: Words are of two types: subanta (nominal) and tiṅanta (verbal). There are seven cases (vibhaktis) with specific meanings: prathamā (nominative), dvitīyā (accusative), tṛtīyā (instrumental), caturthī (dative), pañcamī (ablative), ṣaṣṭhī (genitive), saptamī (locative).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the grammatical cases correspond to the functional relationships in action (kriyā).
